## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Define the term "value addition" in the context of cereal processing. Give two examples each for rice and wheat.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Value addition refers to the process of converting raw cereal grains into processed products with enhanced economic value, nutritional quality, shelf life, convenience, and consumer appeal through primary, secondary, and tertiary processing operations.
>
> **Rice examples (1 mark):** (i) Paddy → Parboiled rice (Sella) — nutrient retention; (ii) Paddy → Puffed rice (Murmura) — convenience snack.
>
> **Wheat examples (1 mark):** (i) Wheat grain → Semolina (Suji) — improved culinary utility; (ii) Maida → Biscuits — ready-to-eat convenience.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between parboiled rice and raw (white) rice in three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Parboiled Rice | Raw White Rice |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Processing | Soaked, steamed, dried before milling | Soaked and milled directly |
| Nutrient retention | High (B-vitamins migrate to endosperm) | Low (B-vitamins lost in bran) |
| Head Rice Yield | 65–70% (harder kernel) | 55–60% (brittle) |
| Cooking quality | Firmer, less sticky | Softer, more sticky |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, every Part B module-level question offers an internal choice. Both options below carry 14 marks, split into 7 + 7.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Treatment**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **manufacturing process of parboiled rice**, with a flow diagram and mass balance. Explain the physico-chemical changes during parboiling. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **production of puffed rice (murmura)**. Describe the principle of puffing, the role of moisture and temperature, and the expansion ratio concept. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Parboiled Rice (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (0.5 mark):**
Parboiling is a hydrothermal treatment in which paddy is soaked, steamed, and dried before milling to gelatinize the starch inside the endosperm and migrate nutrients from the bran to the inner kernel.

**Step 2 — Process Steps (3.5 marks, 0.7 each step):**
1. **Cleaning and Grading:** Paddy is cleaned using air-screen cleaners, magnetic separators, and de-stoners.
2. **Soaking:** Paddy is soaked in water at **50–70 °C for 3–4 hours** until moisture reaches **28–32%**.
3. **Steaming:** Soaked paddy is exposed to **saturated steam at 1.0–1.5 kg/cm² for 15–20 minutes**. This gelatinizes the starch and seals the nutrients inside.
4. **Drying:** The steamed paddy is dried in a **mechanical dryer (LSD / fluidized bed)** to **13–14% moisture** for safe storage and milling.
5. **Milling:** Dried parboiled paddy is dehusked and polished using rubber roll sheller and cone polisher.

**Step 3 — Physico-Chemical Changes (2 marks):**
- **Starch gelatinization:** Crystalline starch converts to amorphous form above 68 °C.
- **Nutrient diffusion:** ~80% of water-soluble B-vitamins and minerals migrate inward.
- **Kernel hardening:** Starch retrogradation makes the kernel **2–3× harder** than raw rice.
- **Pellicle formation:** Bran layer fuses with endosperm, reducing breakage.

**Step 4 — Mass Balance (1 mark):**
For 1000 kg paddy input: clean paddy 990 kg → soaked 1410 kg → cooked 1410 kg → dried 870 kg → parboiled rice 600 kg + husk 200 kg + bran 50 kg + broken 20 kg.

---

**Part (b) — Puffed Rice / Murmura (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Puffed rice is a secondary processed product obtained by rapid heating of pre-conditioned rice (or paddy) at 230–250 °C, causing the internal moisture to vaporize explosively, expanding the kernel to 8–12 times its original volume.

**Step 2 — Raw Material (1 mark):**
Paddy is pre-conditioned to **12–14% moisture** by soaking (1–2 hr) and partial drying. The moisture content is the **critical control parameter** for puffing.

**Step 3 — Process (3 marks):**
1. **Sand/Salt Roasting:** Pre-heated sand or salt is heated to **230–250 °C** in a cast-iron pan.
2. **Puffing Step:** Pre-conditioned paddy (50–100 g batch) is added; stirring is done for **15–20 seconds**.
3. **Popping Action:** Kernel moisture rapidly vaporizes → internal pressure **3–5 atm** → cell wall ruptures → starch granules expand → porous puffed structure forms.
4. **Sieving & Cooling:** Puffed rice is separated from hot sand using a vibrating sieve and cooled.

**Step 4 — Expansion Ratio (2 marks):**
$$
ER_{\text{puff}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{raw}}}{\rho_{\text{puffed}}} = \dfrac{0.85}{0.10} = 8.5
$$
Higher ER indicates better puffing quality. **Factors affecting ER:** moisture content, roasting temperature, time, starch amylose content, and pre-conditioning.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Describe the **roller milling process of wheat** with a flow diagram. Explain the production of **maida, suji, and atta** along with their extraction rates. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **principle of extrusion cooking** in cereal value addition. Discuss the hardware components of a single-screw extruder and the formation of third-generation snacks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Wheat Milling (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Pre-Milling Preparation (1 mark):**
Cleaned wheat is conditioned (tempered) to **15.5% moisture** by adding water and resting for **18–24 hours** to toughen the bran and soften the endosperm.

**Step 2 — Break System (2 marks):**
Wheat passes through **3–5 break rolls** with corrugated surfaces rotating at differential speeds (2.5:1). Each break roll progressively opens the kernel and scrapes out endosperm chunks. After each break, a **plansifter (sieve stack)** separates:
- **Coarse stock** → next break
- **Semolina/middlings** → reduction rolls
- **Bran** → final bran stream

**Step 3 — Reduction System (2 marks):**
The middlings (semolina) pass through **5–7 reduction rolls with smooth surfaces**, which gradually reduce particle size to flour. **Purifiers** use air aspiration to separate pure endosperm from bran-contaminated particles.

**Step 4 — Products and Extraction Rates (2 marks):**

| Product | Extraction Rate | Particle Size | Application |
|---------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| Maida (refined flour) | 65–70% | 80–120 µm | Biscuits, bread, cakes |
| Suji (semolina) | 8–10% | 250–500 µm | Halwa, upma, pasta |
| Atta (whole wheat flour) | 15–20% | 150–250 µm | Chapati, roti |
| Bran + Germ | 7–8% | Variable | Animal feed, oil |

**Total edible flour yield: 88–92% of clean wheat.**

---

**Part (b) — Extrusion Cooking (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Principle (1.5 marks):**
Extrusion cooking is a **High Temperature Short Time (HTST)** continuous process in which moistened, expanded starchy and/or proteinaceous food material is forced through a die under high pressure (30–200 atm), temperature (120–200 °C), and shear, causing starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and sudden expansion upon die exit.

**Step 2 — Single-Screw Extruder Components (2.5 marks):**
1. **Feeder:** Metered introduction of preconditioned feed (12–18% moisture).
2. **Barrel:** Outer cylindrical housing with heating/cooling jackets.
3. **Screw:** Rotating helical screw with **compression ratio 2:1 to 3:1**.
4. **Steam Injection Points:** For moisture and temperature control.
5. **Die Plate:** Shapes the product (curls, puffs, third-generation shells).
6. **Cutter Knife:** Rotating blades that cut the extrudate into desired length.
7. **Conditioner:** Pre-conditioning chamber that hydrates and pre-heats feed.

**Step 3 — Process Zones (1.5 marks):**
- **Feed Zone:** Conveying and initial mixing (40–60 °C).
- **Compression Zone:** Material compressed, temperature rises (80–120 °C).
- **Metering/Heating Zone:** High shear and pressure build-up (120–180 °C, 30–100 atm).
- **Die Zone:** Sudden pressure drop → flash evaporation → porous structure.

**Step 4 — Third-Generation Snacks (1.5 marks):**
Third-generation snacks (3G) are produced by re-extruding **pelletized (half-product)** raw materials at low moisture (10–12%) and high temperature (150–170 °C). They exhibit:
- **Low density (0.1–0.4 g/cm³)**
- **Highly porous, crispy texture**
- **High expansion (ER 5–10×)**
- **Hydrolyzed starch and dextrin formation** → improved digestibility and water solubility.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Missing flow diagram** in a "process description" question costs **2 marks minimum** under KTU valuation. Always draw a labeled block diagram.
> 2. **Confusing moisture basis:** KTU expects answers in **wet basis (wb)** by default. Converting between wb and db without stating the basis loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting extraction rates** in wheat milling — students often give only the products, not the **percentage split**, which is the examiner's main check.
> 4. **Oversimplifying parboiling:** Mention **all three** stages (soak, steam, dry) with temperature and time — partial answers score 3/7 maximum.
> 5. **Mixing paddy-to-rice and rice-to-products:** Paddy → Rice is **primary processing**; Rice → Poha/Murmura is **secondary processing**. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are merged.
> 6. **Skipping the "why" of puffing:** Just stating "heat the rice" is incomplete. Explain the **moisture-pressure-explosion physics** to score full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Module 1 — Cereals Value Addition: Last-Minute Recap**

- ✅ **Cereal anatomy = Husk + Bran + Endosperm + Germ** — every processing step modifies one or more of these layers.
- ✅ **Primary processing = Cleaning + Conditioning + Dehusking + Milling** — the "raw → edible grain" transformation.
- ✅ **Secondary processing = Flour/Flake/Puff/Oil** — the "grain → intermediate product" transformation.
- ✅ **Tertiary processing = Bakery/Pasta/RTE/Mixes** — the "intermediate → consumer product" transformation.
- ✅ **Parboiling improves HRY from 55% to 65–70%** — this is a KTU-favorite numerical example.
- ✅ **Wheat extraction hierarchy: Bran (7%) < Maida (68%) < Suji (10%) < Atta (15%)** — remember the 7-68-10-15 sequence.
- ✅ **Puffing ER = 8–12×** for murmura; uses moisture + heat + sudden pressure release.
- ✅ **Rice bran oil** — extracted with **n-hexane (BP 68.7 °C)**, yield **18–22%**, rich in **γ-oryzanol + tocotrienols**.
- ✅ **Gluten = Gliadin + Glutenin** — only wheat has functional gluten; rice, maize, and millets are gluten-free.
- ✅ **Gelatinization temperature**: Rice **68–78 °C**, Wheat **58–65 °C**, Maize **62–72 °C**.
- ✅ **Extrusion principle: HTST (High Temperature Short Time)** — combines cooking, forming, and puffing in one operation.
- ✅ **Value-addition rule of thumb:** Each processing step adds **1.5×–3× value**; specialty/export products reach **10×–25×**.
- ✅ **Atta vs Maida:** Atta is **95–98% extraction** (whole grain); Maida is **65–70% extraction** (endosperm only).
- ✅ **FSSAI standards** to remember: Maida ash content **≤ 0.7%**, Moisture **≤ 14%**, Acid-insoluble ash **≤ 0.05%**.
- ✅ **"Zero-waste milling"** is achievable — bran (feed/oil), germ (oil/vitamin E), broken rice (flour/snacks) all become revenue streams.
- ✅ **Head Rice Yield (HRY) formula** = (whole kernel mass / paddy input mass) × 100 — frequently asked in numerical problems.
- ✅ **WAI/WSI** are extrusion quality parameters — high WAI = good water uptake; high WSI = starch degradation (controlled dextrinization).
- ✅ **Gluten yield of 25–35%** in hard wheat → suitable for bread; **<25%** → suitable for biscuits and cakes.
- ✅ **Rice flakes (Poha)** process = Soak (2 hr) → Roast (1 min) → Flatten using roller → Dry. The poha yields ~70% of paddy mass.
- ✅ **Third-generation (3G) snacks** = re-extruded pellets, ultra-low density (0.1–0.4 g/cm³), the "king of snack engineering."
- ✅ **Always mention flow diagrams** in Part B process questions — 2 marks saved on every KTU answer script.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parboiling of rice, quality of processed products of rice and wheat, processing of pulses, spices, and condiments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT862 - DEVELOPMENT OF PROCESSED FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parboiling of rice, quality of processed products of rice and wheat, processing of pulses, spices, and condiments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Parboiling of Rice — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parboiling (Sella Process / Husked Rice Pre-Treatment):** It is a hydrothermal treatment of rough rice (paddy) in which the grain is first soaked in water, then steamed (gelatinized) under pressure, and finally dried before milling. The Latin roots *par* = "partial" and *boil* = "to boil" refer to the partial boiling of starch within the husk prior to dehusking.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine making popcorn: the moisture inside the kernel turns to steam, expands, and "locks" the structure. Parboiling does the reverse — it uses controlled steam **inside** the rice grain to **firm up** the endosperm and **glue** the bran layers to the starchy core. The result is a tougher, nutritionally superior, less breakable grain.

---

## 1.2 Quality of Processed Rice & Wheat Products — Core Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Rice Quality** is governed by four pillars: **Milling Yield, Head Rice Recovery (HRR), Cooking Quality (gelatinization temperature, amylose content), and Nutritional Profile (B-vitamin retention, fiber).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Wheat Quality** is dictated by **Hardness (endosperm texture), Gluten Strength (glutenin:gliadin ratio), Protein Content (10–14 %)** and downstream functionality in bakery, pasta, or chapati making.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of wheat as a "rubber band dough" — **gluten** is the rubber band. Hard wheat = strong rubber bands (good for bread). Soft wheat = weak rubber bands (good for cakes). The miller's job is to preserve the right "rubber band chemistry."

---

## 1.3 Pulses, Spices & Condiments — Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulses** are dry, edible seeds of plants in the family *Fabaceae (Leguminosae)* with low fat content (1–5 %), high protein (20–30 %), and a characteristic cotyledonary structure requiring **dehusking + splitting** to yield *dhal*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spices** are dried aromatic parts (seeds, fruits, bark, rhizomes) of tropical plants used in sub-5 % quantities for flavoring. **Condiments** are spice-based preparations (mustard paste, sauces, curry powders) usually consumed as accompaniments.

### Intuitive Analogy
If *cereals are the body of a meal*, **pulses are the muscle** (protein), and **spices are the perfume** — small quantities, but they dictate acceptance.

---

## 1.4 Standard Process Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Soaking temperature for parboiling:** **65 – 75 °C**
- **Steaming pressure:** **1.0 – 1.5 kg/cm² (≈ 100–150 kPa)**
- **Soaking duration (traditional):** **36 – 72 hours**
- **Standard milling recovery of paddy:** **≈ 70 %**
- **Head Rice Recovery (HRR) target:** **> 55 %**
- **Ideal moisture for storage of parboiled rice:** **12 – 14 % (wet basis)**
- **Gluten content of hard wheat:** **> 11 %**
- **Standard yield of dhal from chickpea:** **≈ 70 – 75 %**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parboiling — The Three-Stage Engineering Process

### Stage A — Soaking (Hydration Phase)
- Paddy is steeped in water tanks at **65 – 75 °C**.
- **Moisture uptake** occurs until grain reaches **~ 30 – 35 % moisture (w.b.)**.
- Diffusion-driven; follows Fick's Law at low temperature; capillary-driven at > gelatinization temperature (≈ 68 °C).

### Stage B — Steaming (Gelatinization Phase)
- Hydrated paddy exposed to **saturated steam at 1.0–1.5 kg/cm² for 10–20 minutes**.
- Starch granules undergo **irreversible gelatinization**:
  - Hydrogen bonds between amylose/amylopectin break.
  - Granule swells; crystalline order → amorphous paste.
- **Vitamin B₁ (thiamine)** migrates from the aleurone layer into the endosperm.
- Lipids from bran coat diffuse inward.

### Stage C — Drying (Tempering Phase)
- Two-step drying: Sun-drying → mechanical drying to **12–14 % moisture**.
- Prevents **"refraction cracks"** (stress fractures inside endosperm).

---

## 2.2 Methods of Parboiling — Comparison Matrix

| Method | Soaking | Steaming | Drying | Quality Outcome | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Traditional single boiling** | 36–72 h, ambient | Open pan, atmospheric | Sun | Uneven, dark color | Rural |
| **Double boiling (Malabar)** | Pre-soak + re-soak | Boil until husk splits | Sun | Hard, aromatic | Coastal Kerala/Karnataka |
| **CFTRI Pressure Parboil** | 65–75 °C, 3–4 h | 1.5 kg/cm², 10 min | Mechanical, 40 °C | Uniform, golden, high HRR | Industrial |
| **DDSH / DRP (CFTRI)** | Hot water + lime | Continuous steam | Fluidized bed | Best color + nutrition | Export houses |

---

## 2.3 Quality Characteristics of Processed Rice Products

> [!NOTE]
> **Rice Products & Their Definition:**
> 1. **Rice Flakes (Poha / Chiwra)** — Paddy soaked (60 °C, 36 h), roasted, then flaked by roller.
> 2. **Puffed Rice (Murmura / Kurmura)** — Milled rice heated to **250–300 °C**, sudden pressure drop.
> 3. **Rice Flour** — Ground from raw or parboiled rice; **< 12 % moisture**.
> 4. **Rice Bran Oil** — Solvent extraction from bran; rich in **oryzanol + vitamin E**.
> 5. **Instant / Pre-gelatinized Rice** — Roller-dried slurry; rehydrates in **3–5 min** in boiling water.

**Quality Standards (FSSAI/Codex):**
- **Maximum moisture:** **14 %**
- **Maximum brokens:** **25 % (Grade I)**
- **Maximum foreign matter:** **1 %**
- **Uric acid (insect infestation):** **< 100 mg/kg**

---

## 2.4 Wheat Processing — Quality Specifications

| Product | Extraction Rate | Protein (%) | Ash (%) | End Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Whole Atta** | 100 % | 11–12 | 1.4–1.8 | Chapati, roti |
| **Maida (refined)** | 60–70 % | 9–11 | 0.5–0.7 | Bread, biscuits, noodles |
| **Semolina (Rawa/Suji)** | 70–80 % | 11–12 | 0.8–1.0 | Upma, pasta |
| **Bran** | 28–30 % of grain | 14–16 | 5–7 | Feed, fiber |
| **Germ** | 2–3 % | 25–30 | 4–5 | Vitamin E extract |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Atta ≠ Maida.** *Atta* is whole wheat ground on stone chakki (retains bran/germ). *Maida* is endosperm only, bleached, sieved (210 µm). This is a **classic KTU 2-mark question.**

---

## 2.5 Pulses Processing — Sequence & Engineering

```
Raw Pulse → Cleaning → Grading → Conditioning (8–10 % moisture, 30 min)
        → Pitting (oil/water, 2–5 %) → Dehusking → Splitting 
        → Polishing (optional) → Grading → Packaging
```

**Two Main Milling Systems:**
1. **Traditional Chakki / Dhal Mill** — Low yield (60–65 %), high brokens.
2. **CFTRI / IIPR Roller Mill** — Yield **78–82 %**, low brokens, 80 % capacity utilization.

### Losses in Pulse Milling
- **Powder generation:** 3–5 %
- **Brokens:** 2–4 %
- **Undehusked splits:** 5–8 %
- **Overall milling loss:** **~ 25–30 %**

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Cooking Dhal (CFTRI Method):** Pulse is soaked → steamed 10 min → rolled → dried → pre-gelatinized. Cooks in **5–7 min** versus **35–45 min** for ordinary dhal.

---

## 2.6 Spices & Condiments — Processing Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spice Processing Unit Operations:**
> 1. **Cleaning** — winnowing, magnetic separation.
> 2. **Drying** — sun / solar / mechanical (cabinet / fluidized bed).
> 3. **Grinding** — hammer mill, pin mill, attrition mill.
> 4. **Sieving / Classification** — to required mesh (e.g., 60 BSS for curry powder).
> 5. **Packaging** — hermetic, vacuum, nitrogen flush.
> 6. **Value Addition** — oleoresin extraction, essential oil distillation.

**Typical Spice Moisture Targets (after drying):**
- Black pepper: **< 11 %**
- Cardamom: **< 13 %**
- Turmeric: **< 10 %**
- Chilli: **< 11 %**
- Coriander: **< 9 %**

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> These equations are **exam favorites** for numerical problems.

$$
\text{Milling Recovery (\%)} = \frac{\text{Mass of polished rice}}{\text{Mass of paddy}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{HRR (\%)} = \frac{\text{Mass of head rice (whole kernels)}}{\text{Mass of paddy}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Moisture Content (w.b.)} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Moisture Content (d.b.)} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Rehydration Ratio} = \frac{\text{Mass of rehydrated product}}{\text{Mass of dry product}}
$$

$$
\text{Extraction Rate of Maida (\%)} = \frac{\text{Mass of maida}}{\text{Mass of cleaned wheat}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Volume Expansion Ratio (Puffed Rice)} = \frac{V_f}{V_i}
$$

$$
\text{Milling Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Actual yield}}{\text{Theoretical yield}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Gluten Yield (\%)} = \frac{\text{Wet gluten mass}}{\text{Flour mass}} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** Always state the moisture basis (w.b. or d.b.) in your answer. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for missing basis specification.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Direct Application |
|---|---|
| **FSSAI Standards** | Parboiled rice HRR > 55 % mandatory for export. |
| **Bakery Industry** | Gluten content drives formulation decisions. |
| **Pulse Milling Plants** | CFTRI process reduces cooking time, energy, fuel-wood. |
| **Spice Export (Kerala)** | Cardamom, pepper — oleoresin yield × oleoresin price > whole spice. |
| **Breakfast Cereal Industry** | Puffed rice, flaked rice — HTST extrusion technology. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Effect of Parboiling on Head Rice Recovery

Let us derive **why HRR increases after parboiling** using starch gelatinization principles.

### Step 1 — Define milling stress
During dehusking, the unparboiled grain undergoes brittle fracture because the starch granules are tightly bound by hydrogen bonds in crystalline regions. Stress concentration follows:

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A}
$$

where $F$ = frictional force from rubber rollers, $A$ = kernel cross-section.

### Step 2 — Brittle vs. ductile failure
For unparboiled rice (low moisture ~ 12 %), the endosperm exhibits **brittle behavior** because the starch granules are **rigid (crystalline)**.

For parboiled rice, gelatinization destroys crystallinity. Starch becomes a **plasticized amorphous matrix** that absorbs stress via viscoelastic flow.

### Step 3 — Failure criterion
Apply **Griffith's fracture criterion:**

$$
\sigma_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}
$$

where $E$ = Young's modulus, $\gamma_s$ = surface energy, $a$ = flaw size.

- Unparboiled: $E \approx 300 \text{ MPa}$ → high $\sigma_f$ → **brittle** → high breakage.
- Parboiled: $E \approx 50 \text{ MPa}$ (soft, amorphous) → lower $\sigma_f$ → **absorbs stress** → fewer cracks.

### Step 4 — Quantitative HRR gain
Empirical observation (CFTRI data, 2024):

$$
\Delta \text{HRR} = \text{HRR}_{\text{parboiled}} - \text{HRR}_{\text{raw}} \approx 5\text{–}10\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Parboiling increases HRR by **5–10 %** due to starch plasticization and elimination of internal flaws.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Moisture Conversion Between Wet and Dry Basis

### Given: A wheat sample weighs $W_w = 105 \text{ g}$ wet, $W_d = 88 \text{ g}$ dry.

### Step 1 — Wet basis moisture
$$
M_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 = \frac{105 - 88}{105} \times 100
$$

$$
M_{wb} = \frac{17}{105} \times 100 = 16.19 \text{ \% (w.b.)}
$$

### Step 2 — Dry basis moisture
$$
M_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{17}{88} \times 100
$$

$$
M_{db} = 19.32 \text{ \% (d.b.)}
$$

### Step 3 — General conversion formula
$$
M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}} \quad ; \quad M_{wb} = \frac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}}
$$

Verify: $\dfrac{0.1619}{1 - 0.1619} = \dfrac{0.1619}{0.8381} = 0.1932 = 19.32 \text{ \%}$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Milling Systems for Pulses

| Parameter | Traditional Pounding | CFTRI Roller Mill | Modern Buhler Mill |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dhal yield** | 60–65 % | 78–82 % | 82–85 % |
| **Broken splits** | 15–20 % | 3–5 % | 2–3 % |
| **Capacity** | 100 kg/h | 1 t/h | 5–10 t/h |
| **Capital (₹ Lakhs)** | 0.5 | 25 | 150 |
| **Powder loss** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Energy kWh/t** | 30 | 50 | 45 |
| **End-use** | Household | SME industry | Export |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Numerical — Rice Flake (Poha) Process Yield

**Problem:** A poha-making unit processes **1000 kg of paddy** at **18 % moisture (w.b.)**. After soaking, moisture rises to **35 % (w.b.)**. After roasting + flaking + drying, finished poha weighs **680 kg at 10 % moisture (w.b.)**. Calculate **process yield on a dry basis.**

### Step 1 — Convert all to dry mass

Paddy dry mass:
$$
W_{d,\text{paddy}} = 1000 \times \left(1 - 0.18\right) = 820 \text{ kg}
$$

Poha dry mass:
$$
W_{d,\text{poha}} = 680 \times \left(1 - 0.10\right) = 612 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Yield on dry basis
$$
\eta_{db} = \frac{W_{d,\text{poha}}}{W_{d,\text{paddy}}} \times 100 = \frac{612}{820} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{db} = 74.63 \text{ \%}
$$

### Step 3 — Yield on wet basis (commercial reporting)
$$
\eta_{wb} = \frac{680}{1000} \times 100 = 68.0 \text{ \%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects both bases. **Always report the basis explicitly.**

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Oleoresin Yield from Black Pepper

### Given
Mass of pepper = $100 \text{ kg}$; Moisture = $12 \text{ \%}$; Piperine content = $5 \text{ \% (d.b.)}$.

### Step 1 — Solvent extraction principle
Oleoresin yield is governed by **Fick's first law of diffusion:**

$$
J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}
$$

where $J$ = mass flux, $D$ = diffusion coefficient, $dC/dx$ = concentration gradient.

### Step 2 — Final oleoresin mass
Standard industrial yield for Kerala pepper (KTU reference data):

$$
\text{Oleoresin yield} = 10\text{–}12 \text{ \% (d.b.)}
$$

$$
\text{Mass of oleoresin} = 100 \times (1 - 0.12) \times 0.11 \approx 9.68 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Piperine recovery (95 % extraction efficiency)
$$
\text{Piperine recovered} = 100 \times 0.88 \times 0.05 \times 0.95 = 4.18 \text{ kg}
$$

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step — Conditioning of Wheat for Atta Milling

**Aim:** Raise moisture of cleaned wheat from **11 %** to **15 % (w.b.)** for optimum grinding.

### Step 1 — Water to be added per 1000 kg wheat
Initial dry mass:
$$
W_d = 1000 \times (1 - 0.11) = 890 \text{ kg}
$$

Final wet mass required:
$$
W_f = \frac{W_d}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{890}{0.85} = 1047.06 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Net water addition
$$
\Delta W = W_f - W_i = 1047.06 - 1000 = 47.06 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Conditioning time
Required rest period at room temperature:
$$
t_{rest} = 24\text{–}36 \text{ h}
$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison — Parboiled vs Raw Rice Nutritional Profile

| Nutrient | Raw Rice | Parboiled Rice | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thiamine (mg/100 g) | 0.07 | 0.21 | **+200 %** |
| Niacin (mg/100 g) | 1.6 | 3.4 | **+112 %** |
| Energy (kcal) | 360 | 354 | -2 % |
| Protein (g) | 6.8 | 7.4 | **+9 %** |
| Fiber (g) | 0.6 | 0.7 | +17 % |
| Glycemic Index | 73 | 68 | Lower |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define parboiling. List three advantages and two disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Parboiling** is a hydrothermal treatment of paddy consisting of soaking, steaming, and drying prior to milling, which causes starch gelatinization inside the husk.
>
> **Advantages:**
> 1. Improved **head rice recovery** (5–10 % gain).
> 2. Better **nutritional retention** of B-vitamins in endosperm.
> 3. Enhanced **storage life** due to inactivation of lipase enzyme.
>
> **Disadvantages:**
> 1. **Dark color** of milled rice due to Maillard browning.
> 2. **Musty odor** if drying is improper.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between atta and maida (any three points).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Atta | Maida |
|---|---|---|
| Extraction | 100 % of wheat | 60–70 % of wheat |
| Composition | Contains bran + germ | Only endosperm |
| Fiber | High (1.6–1.8 %) | Very low (0.3 %) |
| Color | Creamish brown | White |
| Use | Chapati, roti | Bread, cake, biscuits |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **CFTRI method of parboiling of paddy** with a neat flow diagram. Explain the biochemical changes that occur during soaking and steaming. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **quality parameters** of milled parboiled rice as per **FSSAI standards**. A paddy lot of **1000 kg** gives **720 kg** of milled parboiled rice, of which **570 kg** are head rice. Calculate the **milling recovery** and **HRR**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — (a) CFTRI Parboiling Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CFTRI Process (Central Food Technological Research Institute, Mysore):**

**Step 1 — Soaking**
- Cleaned paddy soaked in **hot water at 65–75 °C** for **3–4 hours** in stainless steel tanks.
- Water-to-paddy ratio: **1.5 : 1**.
- Achieves **30–35 % moisture (w.b.)**.

**Step 2 — Steaming**
- Hydrated paddy fed to a **pressure vessel** and steamed at **1.0–1.5 kg/cm² for 10–20 min**.
- Saturated steam ensures uniform penetration.

**Step 3 — Drying**
- **Stage I:** Hot air at **80–90 °C** to drop moisture from 35 % → 18 %.
- **Tempering:** 4–6 h rest.
- **Stage II:** Hot air at **40–45 °C** to reach final **12–14 %**.

**Biochemical Changes:**
- **Starch gelatinization** at 68–78 °C.
- **Thiamine migration** from aleurone to endosperm (200 % increase).
- **Lipase inactivation** in bran.
- **Maillard browning** at high drying temperature → characteristic amber color.

### Model Solution — (b) Quality Parameters & Calculation

**FSSAI Quality Standards for Parboiled Rice:**
- Moisture: **≤ 14 %**
- Foreign matter: **≤ 1 %**
- Damaged/discolored grains: **≤ 3 %**
- HRR: **≥ 55 %**
- Free from weevils, insects, fungal hyphae.

**Calculations:**

Milling recovery:
$$
\text{MR} = \frac{720}{1000} \times 100 = 72.0 \text{ \%}
$$

Head rice recovery:
$$
\text{HRR} = \frac{570}{1000} \times 100 = 57.0 \text{ \%}
$$

Both parameters meet FSSAI norms for export-grade parboiled rice.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Describe the **unit operations** involved in **pulse processing** for the production of dhal. Compare the **traditional and modern (CFTRI) milling methods** with respect to yield and quality. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a process flow diagram, explain the **processing of black pepper** for oleoresin production. A pepper sample weighing **100 kg (10 % moisture)** yields **9.5 kg** oleoresin. Calculate the **oleoresin percentage on dry basis**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — (a) Pulse Processing & Milling Comparison

**Unit Operations:**
1. **Cleaning** — Sieving, air-classification, magnetic separation.
2. **Grading** — Size and density grading.
3. **Conditioning** — Moisture adjustment to **8–10 %** with 30-min rest.
4. **Pitting** — Scarification of seed coat using emery rollers + oil/water treatment (2–5 %).
5. **Dehusking** — Emery roller removes loosened husk.
6. **Splitting** — Cone or roller splitter separates cotyledons.
7. **Polishing** — Optional; improves luster but reduces nutrients.
8. **Grading** — Brokens and whole dhal separated.
9. **Packaging** — 25 / 50 kg HDPE bags or retail pouches.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Traditional | CFTRI Roller |
|---|---|---|
| Dhal yield | 60–65 % | 78–82 % |
| Broken splits | 15–20 % | 3–5 % |
| Powder loss | High | Moderate |
| Energy | Low | Medium |
| Capacity | Low | High |
| Hygiene | Poor | GMP-compliant |

### Model Solution — (b) Black Pepper Oleoresin Process

**Process Flow:**

```
Black Pepper → Cleaning → Drying (10–11 % moisture)
            → Coarse Grinding → Solvent Extraction (Hexane/Ethanol)
            → Filtration → Solvent Recovery (Vacuum Distillation)
            → Concentration → Oleoresin
```

**Calculation:**

Given: $W_{wet} = 100 \text{ kg}$, moisture = $10 \text{ \%}$

Dry mass:
$$
W_d = 100 \times (1 - 0.10) = 90 \text{ kg}
$$

Oleoresin on dry basis:
$$
\% \text{Oleoresin}_{db} = \frac{9.5}{90} \times 100 = 10.56 \text{ \%}
$$

This is within the **industrial range of 10–12 %** for Kerala pepper.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Not stating the moisture basis** (w.b. vs d.b.) → lose **1–2 marks**.
> 2. Confusing **milling recovery** with **head rice recovery** — these are different parameters.
> 3. Forgetting to mention **FSSAI/Codex standards** in quality answers.
> 4. Missing the **flow diagram** in 7-mark questions → lose **1–2 marks**.
> 5. Spelling "parboiling" as "pre-boiling" / "parboiling" is the correct term.
> 6. **Atta ≠ Maida** — students often interchange; examiners are strict.
> 7. Always mention **temperature AND time** in process descriptions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize:**

- ✅ Parboiling = **Soaking → Steaming → Drying** (3 stages, in that order).
- ✅ Soaking temperature: **65–75 °C**; Steaming: **1.0–1.5 kg/cm², 10–20 min**.
- ✅ Starch gelatinization range: **68–78 °C**.
- ✅ Parboiled rice has **higher thiamine (200 %)** and **higher HRR (5–10 % gain)**.
- ✅ **Atta** = whole wheat; **Maida** = endosperm only (60–70 % extraction).
- ✅ **Gluten** = gliadin + glutenin; hard wheat → strong gluten (bread).
- ✅ Pulse dhal yield: **traditional 60–65 %**, **CFTRI 78–82 %**.
- ✅ Pulse milling losses: powder + brokens + undehusked ≈ **25–30 %**.
- ✅ **Quick-cooking dhal** cooks in **5–7 min** vs ordinary **35–45 min**.
- ✅ Final moisture for safe storage: rice **14 %**, wheat **13 %**, pulses **12 %**, spices **9–11 %**.
- ✅ **HRR (Head Rice Recovery)** is the single most important rice quality metric.
- ✅ Kerala spices: **pepper, cardamom, clove, nutmeg, turmeric, ginger, vanilla**.
- ✅ Oleoresin yield from pepper: **10–12 % (d.b.)**.
- ✅ **Codex/FSSAI** standards are the universal benchmark — cite them in answers.
- ✅ Always state **moisture basis (w.b. or d.b.)** explicitly.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework (CO Mapping: CO2, CO3 — Bloom Levels: Understand → Apply → Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extruded food product, fermented food product, frozen and dried product, technology of meat, fish and poultry products, technology of milk and milk products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT862 - DEVELOPMENT OF PROCESSED FOOD PRODUCTS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Extruded food product, fermented food product, frozen and dried product, technology of meat, fish and poultry products, technology of milk and milk products ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Extruded Food Products **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** Extrusion is a continuous thermo-mechanical process in which food material is forced through a die of designed shape under controlled conditions of temperature, pressure, and shear, resulting in products with specific texture, shape, and density. Extrusion cooking is a High Temperature Short Time (HTST) process that simultaneously cooks, mixes, shapes, and sterilizes the food material. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Highlight:** Extrusion is classified as a **HTST (High Temperature Short Time)** process operating typically at **120–200°C** for **10–30 seconds** with moisture content of the feed maintained between **12–22% (w/w)**. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine squeezing toothpaste out of a tube — the paste comes out shaped according to the nozzle. Extrusion works exactly like that, but on a much more powerful scale and inside a "screw" barrel where heat and pressure cook the food at the same time. Think of an industrial play-dough machine that also cooks the dough while shaping it into noodles, curls, or puffs. > [!NOTE] > **Product Examples:** Ready-to-eat (RTE) breakfast cereals, snack pellets, pasta, textured vegetable protein (TVP), pet food, and confectionery items. --- ### 1.2 Fermented Food Products **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** Fermentation is a biochemical process in which microorganisms (bacteria, yeast, or mold) enzymatically transform carbohydrates and other food components into simpler compounds such as lactic acid, ethanol, carbon dioxide, and other metabolites, thereby enhancing shelf life, nutritional value, flavor, and texture of the food. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Highlight:** Three major types of fermentation in food processing are: > 1. **Lactic Acid Fermentation (LAF)** — produces yogurt, cheese, sauerkraut > 2. **Alcoholic (Ethanol) Fermentation** — produces beer, wine, bread > 3. **Acetic Acid Fermentation** — produces vinegar, kombucha **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of fermentation as "controlled microbial cooking." Just like you would leave idli batter overnight so it rises and becomes sour, fermentation lets friendly microbes "pre-digest" food, producing tangy flavors, preserving nutrients, and creating new textures — like turning milk into thick, tangy yogurt. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Important Organisms:** *Lactobacillus*, *Streptococcus*, *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, *Acetobacter*, *Penicillium*. --- ### 1.3 Frozen and Dried Food Products **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** **Freezing:** A preservation method where food temperature is reduced below its freezing point (typically **−18°C** or lower), converting most of the water into ice crystals, thereby inhibiting microbial growth and slowing enzymatic and chemical reactions. **Drying (Dehydration):** A preservation method that removes moisture from food to a level (typically **a_w < 0.6**) at which microbial spoilage and deteriorative reactions are minimized. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Highlight:** The **Freeze-Drying (Lyophilization)** process combines both — it freezes the product first and then removes water by **sublimation** under vacuum. This retains nutrients, flavor, and shape far better than conventional drying. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Frozen food is like "pause button" for food — everything stops (bacteria, enzymes), and when thawed, food is almost as fresh. Drying is like taking out the "water life-support" from food — without water, microbes cannot grow, so dry food lasts for months or years, like raisins (dried grapes) or instant noodles. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Storage Parameters:** > - Frozen food storage: **−18°C to −25°C** > - Dried food storage: **25°C**, **a_w < 0.6**, moisture content **< 10–12%** --- ### 1.4 Technology of Meat, Fish and Poultry Products **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** Meat, fish, and poultry processing encompasses a series of unit operations — including slaughtering, bleeding, scalding, evisceration, chilling, cutting, tenderizing, curing, smoking, cooking, and packaging — that convert raw muscle tissue into safe, palatable, and shelf-stable products. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Highlight:** Key preservation methods used in meat/fish/poultry: > - **Chilling** (0 to 4°C) — short term (1–2 weeks) > - **Freezing** (−18°C) — long term (6–12 months) > - **Curing** (salt + nitrite) — increases shelf life > - **Smoking** + **Heat processing** — gives characteristic flavor **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of the meat processing chain as a "factory assembly line" that starts with a live animal and ends with neatly packaged, safe, ready-to-cook cuts. Each step (cleaning, chilling, portioning, packaging) is like a checkpoint ensuring quality and safety. --- ### 1.5 Technology of Milk and Milk Products **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** Milk processing involves a series of operations — reception, clarification, standardization, pasteurization/sterilization, homogenization, separation, and conversion — that transform raw milk into safe, shelf-stable dairy products such as butter, cheese, yogurt, ice cream, condensed milk, and milk powder. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Highlight:** Two foundational thermal processes: > - **Pasteurization (LTLT):** 63°C / 30 min or **HTST:** 72°C / 15 sec > - **Sterilization (UHT):** **135–150°C / 2–5 sec** > - **Homogenization pressure:** typically **10–25 MPa (100–250 bar)** **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Milk processing is like running milk through a "safety and makeover clinic." First, it gets filtered (clarified), then fat is adjusted (standardized), then it is heated (pasteurized) to kill germs, then fat droplets are broken into uniform tiny balls (homogenized), and finally packaged or converted into other products like cheese or butter. > [!NOTE] > **Compositional Reference:** Standardized cow milk contains approximately **3.5% fat**, **8.5% SNF (Solids-Not-Fat)**, and **87.5% water**, with a specific gravity of **1.028–1.032** at 15.5°C. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Extrusion Technology — Operational Steps **Working Principle:** Food material (flour, starch, protein mix) is fed into the extruder barrel where a rotating screw conveys, compresses, shears, and heats the material. The die at the end shapes the product. Steam puffs up the product as it exits due to sudden pressure drop. **Bullet Logic Steps:** 1. **Feeding:** Pre-conditioned feed (correct moisture 12–22%) enters hopper. 2. **Conveying:** Screw moves material forward — increasing pressure. 3. **Mixing/Shearing:** Mechanical energy + barrel heaters raise temperature (often to 120–200°C). 4. **Cooking:** Starch gelatinizes, proteins denature, enzymes inactivate. 5. **Shaping:** Material forced through die of desired shape. 6. **Expansion/Puffing:** Sudden pressure drop causes "flash evaporation" of moisture → puffs the product. 7. **Cutting/Sizing:** Rotating knives cut extrudate to length. 8. **Drying/Cooling:** Post-extrusion drying (to 3–5% moisture) and cooling. 9. **Flavoring/Coating:** Oil/sugar/salt coating applied in tumbler. > [!NOTE] > **Why extrusion works:** Rapid pressure drop and water vapor flash at die exit creates porous, crisp, ready-to-eat structure. --- ### 2.2 Fermentation Technology — Operational Steps **Bullet Logic Steps:** 1. **Substrate Preparation:** Raw material cleaned, ground, cooked (if required). 2. **Sterilization/Pasteurization:** Kills competing microbes. 3. **Inoculation:** Starter culture added (specific strain of bacteria/yeast). 4. **Fermentation:** Controlled conditions (pH 4.0–6.5, temp 25–45°C, time 4–48 hr). 5. **Product Formation:** Acid, gas, alcohol, flavor compounds produced. 6. **Post-treatment:** Cooling, packaging, aging (for cheese). **Core Biochemical Reactions:** $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{LAB (Lactic Acid Bacteria)}} 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH}$$ $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$ --- ### 2.3 Freezing & Drying Technology — Operational Steps **Freezing Bullet Logic Steps:** 1. **Pre-treatment** — Blanching (for vegetables to inactivate enzymes). 2. **Quick freezing** at **−30 to −40°C** (air blast / IQF / cryogenic). 3. **Ice crystal formation** — small crystals (slow freezing = large crystals = cell damage). 4. **Storage** at **−18 to −25°C**. 5. **Thawing** before use. **Drying Bullet Logic Steps:** 1. **Pre-treatment** — Peeling, slicing, blanching, sulphiting. 2. **Heat application** — Sun / hot air / drum / spray / freeze drying. 3. **Moisture migration** from interior to surface → evaporation. 4. **Final moisture target** of **< 10% (w.b.)** or **a_w < 0.6**. 5. **Packaging** in moisture-proof containers. --- ### 2.4 Meat / Fish / Poultry Technology — Bullet Logic Steps 1. **Slaughter / Harvesting** (Bleeding → Scalding → De-feathering → Evisceration). 2. **Chilling** at 0–4°C (prevents rigor mortis issues). 3. **Cutting & Deboning.** 4. **Tenderization** (mechanical / enzymatic / marination). 5. **Curing** (NaCl + NaNO₂/NaNO₃). 6. **Smoking** (cold or hot). 7. **Cooking / Canning.** 8. **Packaging** (vacuum / MAP). --- ### 2.5 Milk & Milk Products Technology — Bullet Logic Steps 1. **Reception & Testing** (clot-on-boiling, alcohol test, acidity). 2. **Filtration / Clarification.** 3. **Standardization** (fat adjustment to 3.5% / 6% as required). 4. **Homogenization** (15–25 MPa, 60–70°C). 5. **Pasteurization / Sterilization.** 6. **Cooling & Packaging.** 7. **Conversion** → Butter (churning), Cheese (rennet + acidification), Yogurt (fermentation), Ice cream (freezing + aeration), Milk powder (spray drying). --- ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Moisture Content (w.b.) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$ | % | Drying, storage stability | | 2 | Moisture Content (d.b.) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100$ | % | Engineering calculations | | 3 | Water Activity | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$ | dimensionless | Shelf-life prediction | | 4 | Pasteurization Value | $F = \int 10^{(T - T_{ref})/z}\, dt$ | min | Thermal process lethality | | 5 | Drying Rate | $\dfrac{dW}{dt} = \dfrac{hA(T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_s}$ | kg/hr | Drying time estimation | | 6 | Mass balance (drying) | $m_1(1 - X_1) = m_2(1 - X_2)$ | kg | Material balance in dryers | | 7 | RMR (Relative Moisture Removal) | $RMR = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1} \times 100$ | % | Drying efficiency | | 8 | Homogenization Pressure | $P_{homo} = 10 - 25$ MPa | MPa | Fat globule size reduction | | 9 | Extruder Throughput | $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot N \cdot \rho \cdot \eta_v$ | kg/h | Capacity estimation | | 10 | Centrifuge (cream separator) | $V_{cream} = \dfrac{V_{milk}(F_{milk} - F_{skim})}{F_{cream} - F_{skim}}$ | L | Standardization | | 11 | Acidification rate | $pH(t) = pH_0 - k \cdot \ln(1 + t)$ | dimensionless | Fermentation kinetics | | 12 | Freezing Point Depression | $\Delta T_f = i \cdot K_f \cdot m$ | °C | Cryoscopy of milk | | 13 | Cheese Yield | $Y = \dfrac{(0.93 \times F) + (C - 0.1)}{1 - M}$ | kg cheese/100 kg milk | Process economics | | 14 | Sensible Heat | $Q_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ | Pasteurizer sizing | | 15 | Latent Heat (water) | $Q_L = m \cdot \lambda = 334$ kJ/kg | kJ | Freezing load | > [!NOTE] > **Critical:** The freezing point of milk is **−0.55°C** (lower than water) due to dissolved solutes — important for cold storage and cryoscopy tests. ### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields - **Extrusion** is used in breakfast cereals (Kellogg's), pet food (Pedigree), and snack pellets. - **Fermentation** powers dairy (Amul yogurt), bakery (yeast bread), beverages (beer), and traditional Indian foods (idli, dosa, pickles). - **Freezing** preserves seafood exports (Indian shrimp), meat, and ready-meals globally. - **Drying** enables production of milk powder (Amul, Nestle), instant coffee, and freeze-dried fruits. - **Milk processing** underpins multi-billion dollar dairy industry — Amul alone handles >25 million liters/day. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Moisture Content Conversion (Wet Basis to Dry Basis) Used in every drying calculation in food processing. **Step 1 — Definition of wet basis moisture:** $$M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Definition of dry basis moisture:** $$M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100$$ **Step 3 — Express $m_w$ from the $M_{wb}$ equation:** $$m_w = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100}(m_w + m_d)$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to find $m_w$ in terms of $m_d$ and $M_{wb}$:** $$m_w \left(1 - \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100}\right) = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100} m_d$$ $$m_w = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \cdot m_d$$ **Step 5 — Substitute into $M_{db}$ formula:** $$M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100 = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$$ **Final Conversion Formula:** $$\boxed{M_{db} = \dfrac{100 \cdot M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}}}$$ **Reverse Conversion:** $$\boxed{M_{wb} = \dfrac{100 \cdot M_{db}}{100 + M_{db}}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Material Balance for Drying Process **Given:** A wet food with mass $m_1$ (kg) and moisture content $X_1$ (dry basis) is dried to mass $m_2$ (kg) and moisture $X_2$ (dry basis). Find moisture removed. **Step 1 — Total solids are conserved (no loss of dry matter during drying):** $$m_1 (1 - X_1) = m_2 (1 - X_2)$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $m_2$:** $$m_2 = m_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 - X_1}{1 - X_2}$$ **Step 3 — Mass of water removed:** $$m_{w,removed} = m_1 - m_2$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $m_2$:** $$m_{w,removed} = m_1 - m_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 - X_1}{1 - X_2}$$ **Step 5 — Factor out $m_1$:** $$m_{w,removed} = m_1 \left[1 - \dfrac{1 - X_1}{1 - X_2}\right]$$ **Step 6 — Common denominator $(1 - X_2)$:** $$m_{w,removed} = m_1 \left[\dfrac{(1 - X_2) - (1 - X_1)}{1 - X_2}\right]$$ **Step 7 — Simplify numerator:** $$m_{w,removed} = m_1 \cdot \dfrac{X_1 - X_2}{1 - X_2}$$ **Final Formula:** $$\boxed{m_{w,removed} = m_1 \cdot \dfrac{X_1 - X_2}{1 - X_2}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Energy Required for Freezing **Step 1 — Total cooling load = Sensible heat (cooling from initial $T_i$ to freezing point $T_f$) + Latent heat (phase change at $T_f$) + Sensible heat (cooling frozen product from $T_f$ to final $T_{end}$).** **Step 2 — Sensible heat before freezing:** $$Q_1 = m \cdot C_{p,unfrozen} \cdot (T_i - T_f)$$ **Step 3 — Latent heat of freezing (water removal from food = $w$ fraction):** $$Q_2 = m \cdot w \cdot \lambda_f$$ **Step 4 — Sensible heat after freezing:** $$Q_3 = m \cdot C_{p,frozen} \cdot (T_f - T_{end})$$ **Step 5 — Total freezing load:** $$\boxed{Q_{total} = m\left[C_{p,unfrozen}(T_i - T_f) + w\lambda_f + C_{p,frozen}(T_f - T_{end})\right]}$$ For water: $\lambda_f = 334 \text{ kJ/kg}$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Drying of Apple Slices **Problem:** 100 kg of fresh apple slices (initial moisture 80% w.b.) are dried to final moisture 5% w.b. Calculate: (a) mass of dried product, (b) moisture removed. **Step 1 — Convert to dry basis:** $$X_1 = \dfrac{80}{100 - 80} = \dfrac{80}{20} = 4.0 \;\text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$ $$X_2 = \dfrac{5}{100 - 5} = \dfrac{5}{95} = 0.0526 \;\text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$ **Step 2 — Find dry solid mass in original product:** $$m_d = m_1 (1 - X_1) = 100 \times (1 - 0.80) = 20 \;\text{kg}$$ **Step 3 — Final product mass:** $$m_2 = \dfrac{m_d}{1 - X_2} = \dfrac{20}{1 - 0.0526} = \dfrac{20}{0.9474} = 21.11 \;\text{kg}$$ **Step 4 — Moisture removed:** $$m_{w} = 100 - 21.11 = 78.89 \;\text{kg}$$ **Result:** - Dried product = **21.11 kg** - Moisture removed = **78.89 kg** --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Cheese Yield **Problem:** Given 1000 kg milk with **Fat = 4.0%** and **Casein = 2.6%**, expected moisture in cheese = **37%**. Calculate cheese yield using: $$Y = \dfrac{(0.93 \times F) + (C - 0.1)}{1 - M} \times 10$$ **Step 1 — Convert formula units (result per 100 kg milk then scale):** Per 100 kg milk: $F = 4.0$, $C = 2.6$, $M = 0.37$ $$Y_{100} = \dfrac{(0.93 \times 4.0) + (2.6 - 0.1)}{1 - 0.37} = \dfrac{3.72 + 2.5}{0.63}$$ $$Y_{100} = \dfrac{6.22}{0.63} = 9.87 \;\text{kg cheese/100 kg milk}$$ **Step 2 — Scale to 1000 kg milk:** $$Y_{1000} = 9.87 \times 10 = 98.7 \;\text{kg cheese}$$ --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Preservation Methods | Method | Mechanism | Shelf-life Extension | Quality Retention | Cost | |---|---|---|---|---| | Refrigeration (4°C) | Slows microbes/enzymes | Days to weeks | Excellent | Low | | Freezing (−18°C) | Inhibits microbes via ice | 6–12 months | Very good (texture loss) | High | | Pasteurization | Kills pathogens | 2–3 weeks (refrigerated) | Good | Medium | | UHT Sterilization | Commercial sterility | 6–9 months (ambient) | Good | High | | Drying | Low $a_w$ (<0.6) | 6–24 months | Moderate (flavor loss) | Medium | | Freeze Drying | Sublimation, low $a_w$ | 1–5 years | Excellent | Very high | | Curing/Smoking | Salt + nitrite + antimicrobial smoke | Weeks to months | Good flavor change | Low | | Fermentation | pH reduction, antimicrobial metabolites | Weeks to months | Variable | Low | --- ## 4.
