KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define **extrusion cooking**. List any four extruded food products and state two key process parameters. **Model Answer:** Extrusion cooking is a High Temperature Short Time (HTST) process in which food material is forced through a die under conditions of high temperature (typically 120–200°C), pressure (3–15 MPa), and mechanical shear, causing simultaneous cooking, shaping, and partial sterilization of the food. **Four Extruded Products:** 1. Ready-to-eat breakfast cereals (corn flakes, oats rings) 2. Snack pellets (kurkure, cheese balls) 3. Pasta (macaroni, noodles) 4. Textured Vegetable Protein (TVP) / Pet food **Two Key Process Parameters:** 1. Barrel temperature (120–200°C) 2. Feed moisture content (12–22% w.b.) *(Examiner Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for products, 1 mark for parameters)* --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is **water activity (a_w)**? Why is it critical in dried food storage? **Model Answer:** Water activity ($a_w$) is the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in a food substrate ($p$) to the vapour pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature: $$a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0}$$ **Critical Importance:** - Microbial growth generally requires $a_w > 0.60$. - Below $a_w = 0.60$, most bacteria, yeasts, and molds cannot grow. - Dried foods are typically stored at $a_w < 0.60$ (often $a_w \leq 0.30$) for long shelf-life. - It also influences lipid oxidation, Maillard browning, and enzymatic activity. *(Examiner Note: 1.5 marks for definition + formula, 1.5 marks for importance)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a **single-screw extruder** for food processing. Discuss the role of temperature, moisture, and screw speed on product quality. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **principle, types, and applications of freeze-drying (lyophilization)** in food preservation. List four advantages over conventional drying. **Model Answer:** **(a) Working of Single-Screw Extruder:** A single-screw extruder consists of a **hopper** (feed inlet), a **barrel** (cylindrical housing), a **rotating screw** (helical flight conveying shaft), **barrel heaters/coolers**, a **die** (shaping outlet), and a **cutter** for sizing. **Working Steps:** 1. **Feeding Zone:** Moistened raw material (flour + 12–22% water) enters from hopper. 2. **Conveying Zone:** Screw rotates, pushing material forward; pressure begins to build. 3. **Kneading/Compression Zone:** Mechanical shear + heat (120–200°C) cooks material; starch gelatinizes, proteins denature. 4. **Metering Zone:** Pressure peaks (3–15 MPa); material is fully plasticized. 5. **Die Zone:** Material forced through die — sudden pressure drop causes "flash evaporation" of moisture → puffs the product. 6. **Cutting:** Rotating knives cut the extrudate into uniform pieces. 7. **Post-treatment:** Drying (to <5% moisture), flavor coating, packaging. **Block Diagram:** ``` Hopper → Screw Conveyor → Heated Barrel → Die → Cutter → Drier → Coater → Packing ``` **Effect of Process Parameters:** | Parameter | Effect | |---|---| | Temperature ↑ | More starch gelatinization, expansion, but may scorch | | Moisture ↑ | Less shear, denser product, less expansion | | Screw Speed ↑ | Higher throughput, more shear, finer texture | --- **(b) Freeze Drying (Lyophilization):** **Principle:** Water is removed from frozen food by **sublimation** — direct conversion of ice to vapour under vacuum — without passing through the liquid phase. **Process Steps:** 1. **Pre-treatment** — Cleaning, slicing, optional blanching. 2. **Freezing** — Rapid freezing to −30 to −40°C. 3. **Primary Drying (Sublimation)** — Under high vacuum (10–100 Pa), ice sublimates; shelves provide latent heat (~600 kJ/kg). 4. **Secondary Drying (Desorption)** — At 30–50°C, residual bound moisture is removed to final moisture <2–5%. 5. **Packaging** — Under nitrogen or vacuum. **Four Advantages over Conventional Drying:** 1. Excellent retention of heat-sensitive nutrients (vitamins, colors). 2. Minimal shrinkage — original shape preserved. 3. Excellent rehydration due to porous structure. 4. Long shelf life (1–5 years) without refrigeration. **Applications:** Coffee, instant soups, herbs, fruits (strawberries, mango), freeze-dried curries (MTR). --- #### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **principle of lactic acid fermentation**. With a flow diagram, describe the production of **yogurt from milk**. Mention the role of starter cultures. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **technology of pasteurized milk** with reference to **HTST process**. Sketch a plate heat exchanger and state the legal pasteurization standards. **Model Answer:** **(a) Lactic Acid Fermentation & Yogurt Production:** **Principle:** Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB) — primarily *Streptococcus thermophilus* and *Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus* — ferment lactose in milk to lactic acid, lowering pH from 6.6 to ~4.5. This causes casein coagulation (gel formation) and gives yogurt its characteristic tangy flavor and thick texture. **Flow Diagram:** ``` Raw Milk → Standardization (Fat 3.5%) → Homogenization (15 MPa) → Heat Treatment (90°C / 10 min) → Cooling to 42–45°C → Inoculation (Starter Culture 2–3%) → Incubation (42°C / 4–6 hours till pH 4.5) → Cooling to 4°C → Fruit Addition (Optional) → Packaging ``` **Role of Starter Cultures:** - *S. thermophilus* — fast acidifier, produces aroma compounds (acetaldehyde). - *L. bulgaricus* — produces proteases, generates peptides that stimulate *S. thermophilus* (proto-cooperation). - Together, they ensure proper acidification, texture, and flavor. --- **(b) Pasteurized Milk Technology (HTST):** **Principle:** HTST (High Temperature Short Time) pasteurization subjects milk to **72°C for 15 seconds** (legal minimum) followed by rapid cooling to 4°C. This destroys pathogenic microorganisms while preserving nutritional and sensory qualities. **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) Sections:** 1. **Regeneration Section** — Incoming cold milk pre-heated by outgoing hot milk (saves ~85% energy). 2. **Heating Section** — Milk heated to 72°C by hot water. 3. **Holding Tube** — Provides 15 seconds residence time at 72°C. 4. **Cooling Section** — Milk cooled first by incoming cold milk (regeneration), then by chilled water to 4°C. **Legal Pasteurization Standards:** - HTST: **72°C / 15 sec** (most common) - LTLT: **63°C / 30 min** (batch) - Phosphatase test must be negative (confirms proper pasteurization). - Standard Plate Count after pasteurization must be <30,000 CFU/mL. **Sketch of Plate Heat Exchanger:** ``` [Milk IN cold] →→ [Regeneration ← hot milk OUT] →→ [Hot water heater] →→ [Hold tube 15s] →→ [Regeneration] →→ [Chilled water] →→ [Milk OUT 4°C] ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **dry basis** and **wet basis** moisture content — KTU examiner will award **zero marks** if wrong unit is used in numericals. > 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to mention both **starter organisms** in yogurt — students often write only "Lactobacillus" losing 2 marks. > 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Writing "HTST 72°C / 30 sec" or "LTLT 72°C / 15 sec" — these are wrong; remember 72°C/**15 sec** = HTST, 63°C/**30 min** = LTLT. > 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In extrusion questions, forgetting that it is **HTST (not UHT)** and **NOT a sterilization** process — partial sterility only. > 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Not labelling block diagrams with **flow direction arrows** — examiner deducts 1–2 marks for missing arrows. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Extrusion** = HTST process, 120–200°C, 12–22% feed moisture, 3–15 MPa pressure, used for RTE cereals, snacks, pasta, TVP. - **Fermentation** types = Lactic Acid, Alcoholic, Acetic Acid; key organisms: *Lactobacillus*, *Saccharomyces*, *Streptococcus thermophilus*, *Acetobacter*. - **Freezing** storage standard = **−18°C**; quick freezing = small ice crystals = better texture. - **Freeze drying** uses **sublimation** under vacuum; preserves nutrients, color, shape; cost is high. - **Water activity ($a_w$)** below **0.60** = shelf-stable dried food. - **Pasteurization:** HTST = **72°C/15 sec**; LTLT = **63°C/30 min**; UHT = **135–150°C / 2–5 sec**. - **Milk composition:** 3.5% fat, 8.5% SNF, 87.5% water; freezing point = **−0.55°C**. - **Meat preservation:** Chilling (0–4°C), Freezing (−18°C), Curing (NaCl + NaNO₂), Smoking, Canning. - **Cheese yield formula** (Van Slyke): $Y = [(0.93 F) + (C - 0.1)] / (1 - M)$ - **Moisture conversion:** $M_{db} = 100 \cdot M_{wb} / (100 - M_{wb})$ - **Mass balance in drying:** $m_1(1 - X_1) = m_2(1 - X_2)$ — total solids are conserved. - **Freezing load:** includes sensible heat + latent heat (334 kJ/kg) + sensible heat after freezing. - **Critical $a_w$ thresholds:** Bacteria >0.91, Yeasts >0.88, Molds >0.80, Xerophilic molds >0.60. - **Yogurt starter:** *S. thermophilus* + *L. bulgaricus* at 42–45°C, pH drop 6.6 → 4.5. - **Homogenization pressure:** 15–25 MPa, reduces fat globule size to <2 μm. - **Curing agents:** NaCl (2–4%) + NaNO₂ (≤200 ppm) — gives characteristic cured color (nitrosomyoglobin). ---

topics.title: Technology of oilseeds and fat products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT862 - DEVELOPMENT OF PROCESSED PROCESSED FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Technology of oilseeds and fat products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Oilseeds** are seeds of plants that are cultivated primarily for the extraction of edible or industrial oils. From the KTU 2024 Scheme perspective (Food Process Technology), oilseeds constitute the second most important agricultural commodity after cereals, and they are the principal raw material source of **lipids (fats and oils)** in the human diet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> *"Oilseeds are plant-derived raw materials containing significant quantities of recoverable lipid matter (15–50%) trapped within a cellular matrix of proteins, carbohydrates, lignocellulosic structures, and minor bioactives, requiring mechanical, thermal, chemical, or enzymatic processing to liberate the entrapped oil/fat."*

**Fats and oils** are chemically **triacylglycerols (TAGs)** — esters of glycerol with three fatty acid chains — differing primarily in physical state at room temperature:
- **Oils**: liquid at 25 °C (high unsaturated fatty acid content).
- **Fats**: solid/semi-solid at 25 °C (high saturated fatty acid content).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Coconut as a Treasure Chest":**
> Imagine an oilseed as a **locked iron safe** buried inside a concrete vault. The **oil** is the treasure, the **cell wall** is the safe, the **protein/carbohydrate matrix** is the concrete vault, and **gums/phospholipids** are the alarm systems stuck to the treasure. To extract the oil, you must:
> 1. **Crack open** the seed coat (cleaning, dehulling, flaking).
> 2. **Break the safe** (cell disruption by flaking, cooking, or extrusion).
> 3. **Disarm the alarms** (degumming, neutralizing gums/FFAs).
> 4. **Polish the treasure** (refining, bleaching, deodorizing).
> Just like a treasure must be cleaned before display, the crude oil must be refined before it reaches your kitchen as cooking oil or margarine.

### 1.3 Classification of Major Oilseeds (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Category | Oilseed Examples | Oil Content (% w/w) | Primary Fatty Acid |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Major Oilseeds** | Soybean (*Glycine max*) | 18–22 | Linoleic (C18:2) |
| | Groundnut (*Arachis hypogaea*) | 45–55 | Oleic (C18:1) |
| | Rapeseed/Mustard (*Brassica* spp.) | 38–44 | Erucic / Oleic |
| | Sunflower (*Helianthus annuus*) | 38–48 | Linoleic / Oleic |
| | Sesame (*Sesamum indicum*) | 45–55 | Oleic / Linoleic |
| | Safflower (*Carthamus tinctorius*) | 30–40 | Linoleic |
| | Linseed (*Linum usitatissimum*) | 35–45 | α-Linolenic (C18:3) |
| **Tree-Borne / Perennial** | Coconut (*Cocos nucifera*) | 35–45 (kernel) | Lauric (C12:0) |
| | Oil Palm (*Elaeis guineensis*) | 45–55 (mesocarp) | Palmitic / Oleic |
| | Olive (*Olea europaea*) | 15–25 | Oleic (C18:1) |
| **Industrial / Minor** | Castor (*Ricinus communis*) | 45–55 | Ricinoleic |
| | Cottonseed (*Gossypium* spp.) | 18–25 | Linoleic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric**: The KTU 2024 reference benchmark for industrial **oil yield efficiency (η)** in solvent extraction is **≥ 95%** of available lipid, with residual oil in meal **≤ 1% w/w**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Composition of an Oilseed

A typical oilseed consists of the following anatomical and chemical layers:

1. **Hull / Seed Coat**: Lignocellulosic outer covering (protection).
2. **Cotyledon / Endosperm**: Storage tissue containing the oil bodies (lipid droplets), protein bodies (aleurone), and starch granules.
3. **Embryo / Germ**: Contains high enzymatic activity (lipases, lipoxygenases).

**Inside the cell**, oil is stored in discrete **oleosomes / lipid bodies** (0.2–2.5 μm diameter) — spherical organelles stabilized by a monolayer of **phospholipids and oleosin proteins**. Liberation of oil therefore requires **complete disruption of the cell wall (cellulose/hemicellulose) and the oleosome membrane**.

### 2.2 Unit Operations in Oilseed Processing — The Master Flow

The KTU 2024 syllabus organizes oilseed technology into **three engineering domains**:

**Domain A — Pre-treatment (Mechanical Conditioning)**
1. Cleaning → 2. Drying → 3. Dehulling/Decortication → 4. Cracking → 5. Flaking → 6. Cooking/Conditioning.

**Domain B — Oil Extraction (Two Principal Routes)**
- **Mechanical**: Expeller / Screw Press → yields "crude" oil + cake (residual oil ~6–10%).
- **Chemical (Solvent)**: Hexane percolation/immersion → yields miscella + meal (residual oil ≤ 1%).
- **Combined**: Pre-press + Solvent Extraction (most common industry workflow).

**Domain C — Post-extraction Refining (Purification of Crude Oil)**
1. **Degumming** (phospholipid removal) → 2. **Neutralization / Deacidification** (FFA removal) → 3. **Bleaching** (pigment removal) → 4. **Deodorization** (odor/flavor removal) → 5. **Winterization** (wax removal, optional) → 6. **Polishing/Filtration**.

### 2.3 Detailed Theoretical Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Cleaning & Drying**
- Magnetic separators, vibrating screens, and gravity tables remove stones, metals, chaff.
- Drying reduces moisture to **≤ 10%** to inhibit lipase activity (otherwise FFA rises rapidly → hydrolytic rancidity).

**Step 2 — Dehulling**
- Removes fibrous hull containing gums, waxes, and color bodies (e.g., gossypol in cottonseed).
- Improves protein quality of the meal and reduces refining losses.

**Step 3 — Cracking & Flaking**
- **Cracking** breaks kernels into 4–8 pieces using corrugated roller mills.
- **Flaking** compresses cracked pieces into **0.25–0.35 mm** flakes using smooth rollers.
- *Why so thin?* — Flake thickness controls diffusion distance of oil during solvent extraction. Optimal: **0.25–0.30 mm** (thinner flakes fracture and form fines; thicker flakes resist solvent penetration).

**Step 4 — Cooking / Conditioning**
- Flakes are heated to **80–110 °C** in stack cookers with live steam and moisture.
- **Effects**: (a) Cell wall rupture (protein denaturation → coalescence of oil droplets); (b) viscosity reduction; (c) inactivation of lipases/myrosinase; (d) moisture adjustment to ~3% for mechanical pressing or ~6–10% for solvent extraction.

**Step 5 — Mechanical Extraction (Expeller Pressing)**
- A screw shaft inside a horizontal barrel cage exerts pressure up to **1400–2000 bar**.
- Oil drains through cage slits; cake exits at ~105 °C.
- Residual oil in cake: **5–10%**.
- *Continuous vs. Batch*: Industry uses **continuous expellers** (Andersen, Rose, Downs, De Smet).

**Step 6 — Solvent Extraction (Hexane Process)**
- Most efficient method; uses food-grade **n-hexane (bp 65–70 °C)**.
- Two contactor designs:
  - **Deep-bed / Basket-type**: Batch, used in small plants.
  - **Loop-type (Rotocell extractor — Hildebrand, Lurgi, DeSmet)**: Continuous counter-current.
- Process stages: **Extraction → Miscella filtration → Meal desolventizing (DTDC — Desolventizer-Toaster-Dryer-Cooler)**.

**Step 7 — Refining Stages**

| Stage | Reagent/Process | What is Removed | Typical Conditions |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Degumming | Water / Acid (H₃PO₄, citric) | Hydratable & non-hydratable phospholipids | 60–80 °C, 0.05–0.1% H₃PO₄ |
| Neutralization | NaOH (caustic) | Free Fatty Acids (FFAs) as soapstock | 60–80 °C, 8–14% lye |
| Bleaching | Acid-activated clay (earth) | Carotenoids, chlorophyll, soap, peroxides | 90–110 °C, vacuum, 0.5–2% earth |
| Deodorization | Steam distillation at 3–8 mbar | Aldehydes, ketones, FFA traces, tocopherols | 220–260 °C, 1–3 h |
| Winterization | Controlled crystallization + filtration | Waxes, high-melting TAGs | 5–10 °C slow cooling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Two Refining Routes:**
> - **Physical Refining**: Degumming → Bleaching → Steam Refining (220–260 °C, < 5 mbar). Preferred for **high FFA oils** (palm, rice bran).
> - **Chemical (Caustic) Refining**: Degumming → Neutralization → Bleaching → Deodorization. Higher yield loss but superior oil quality.

### 2.4 Hydrogenation, Interesterification, and Fractionation (Fat Modification)

**Hydrogenation**:
$$\text{C=C (unsaturated)} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Ni catalyst, } 150–200\,°C, \text{pressure}} \text{C–C (saturated)}$$
- Converts liquid oils into semi-solid fats for **margarine, vanaspati, and shortening**.
- Selectivity controlled by temperature, pressure, and catalyst concentration.

**Interesterification** (chemical or enzymatic):
- Rearranges fatty acids among glycerol backbones without changing saturation → alters melting profile **without producing trans-fats** (enzymatic 1,3-specific is preferred).

**Fractionation**:
- **Dry fractionation**: Slow cooling → crystallization → filtration → separates into **stearin (solid)** and **olein (liquid)**.
- **Solvent fractionation**: Uses acetone/isopropanol (higher purity).
- **Detergent fractionation**: Uses sodium lauryl sulfate.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Oil Yield (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{\text{oil}}}{W_{\text{seed}} \times f_{\text{lipid}}} \times 100$ | % | Extraction efficiency |
| 2 | **Free Fatty Acid (FFA)** | $\text{FFA \% (as oleic)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 28.2}{W}$ | % w/w | Oil quality |
| 3 | **Iodine Value (IV)** | $\text{IV} = \dfrac{(B-S)\times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | g I₂/100 g | Unsaturation |
| 4 | **Saponification Value (SV)** | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{(B-S)\times N \times 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH/g | Molecular weight |
| 5 | **Peroxide Value (PV)** | $\text{PV (meq/kg)} = \dfrac{(S-B)\times N \times 1000}{W}$ | meq/kg | Oxidative rancidity |
| 6 | **Refining Loss (RL)** | $\text{RL} = \text{FFA \%} + \text{Gums \%} + 1.5 \text{–} 2.0$ | % | Process yield |
| 7 | **Mass Balance (Solvent Extraction)** | $W_{\text{seed}} = W_{\text{oil}} + W_{\text{meal}}$ | kg | Conservation |
| 8 | **Hydrogenation Selectivity** | $S = \dfrac{\ln(L_0/L_t)}{\ln(P_0/P_t)}$ | dimensionless | Catalyst selectivity |
| 9 | **Solid Fat Content (SFC)** | NMR-pulsed direct measurement | % | Melting profile |
| 10 | **Slip Melting Point (SMP)** | Open capillary tube method | °C | Fat identity |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, $V$ = titrant volume (mL), $N$ = normality, $W$ = sample weight (g), $B$ = blank titre, $S$ = sample titre, $L$ and $P$ denote linoleate and linolenate concentrations during hydrogenation.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry**: Cooking oils, margarine, shortening, confectionery fats (cocoa butter equivalents), ice-cream emulsions.
- **Industrial**: Biodiesel (trans-esterification of refined oil), soaps (saponification), paints (linseed oil — drying oil), lubricants.
- **Animal Feed**: Defatted oilseed meal (soybean meal, groundnut cake) — a major source of protein for poultry and aquaculture.
- **Pharmaceutical**: Refined oils as carriers in injectables, vitamin encapsulation.
- **Cosmetics**: Hydrogenated oils as emollients; castor oil derivatives (ricinoleate esters).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Oil Extraction Yield (Solvent Extraction)

> **Problem Statement (KTU Sample):**
> A batch of soybean flakes weighing **1000 kg** contains **20%** lipid (w/w). After solvent extraction, the recovered crude oil weighs **188 kg**. Determine: (a) the extraction yield (η), (b) the residual oil in the meal, and (c) verify mass balance.

**Step 1 — Identify available lipid in feed**

$$M_{\text{lipid available}} = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 0.20 = 200 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Extraction Yield**

$$\eta = \dfrac{W_{\text{oil recovered}}}{W_{\text{lipid available}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{188}{200} \times 100 = 94.0\,\%$$

**Step 3 — Residual Oil in Meal (ROM)**

By conservation of mass:
$$W_{\text{meal}} = W_{\text{seed}} - W_{\text{oil}} = 1000 - 188 = 812 \text{ kg}$$

The meal still contains the unrecovered oil:
$$M_{\text{residual oil}} = 200 - 188 = 12 \text{ kg}$$

Expressed as % of meal:
$$\text{ROM \%} = \dfrac{12}{812} \times 100 = 1.478\,\% \approx 1.48\,\%$$

> **Step 4 — Verification (mass balance)**
>
> Feed = Oil + Meal (in terms of dry matter):
>
> $$1000 = 188 + 812 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1000 = 1000 \;\;\checkmark$$
>
> Therefore, residual oil in meal = **1.48% w/w**, which is slightly above the industry standard of **≤ 1.0%**, suggesting the need for improved solvent contact (e.g., thinner flakes or longer residence time).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — FFA Determination (Neutralization Titration)

> **Problem Statement:**
> Crude rice bran oil (**5.00 g**) is titrated with **0.1 N KOH**. The titre value is **4.25 mL**. Express the FFA as **% oleic acid** (equivalent weight = 282).

**Step 1 — Recall FFA Formula**

$$\text{FFA \%} = \dfrac{V \times N \times E}{W \times 1000} \times 100$$

where $E$ = equivalent weight of oleic acid (282), $V$ = volume (mL), $N$ = normality, $W$ = weight (g).

**Step 2 — Substitute Values**

$$\text{FFA \%} = \dfrac{4.25 \times 0.1 \times 282}{5.00 \times 1000} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Numerator Evaluation**

$$4.25 \times 0.1 = 0.425$$
$$0.425 \times 282 = 119.85$$

**Step 4 — Divide by Denominator and Convert to %**

$$\dfrac{119.85}{5000} \times 100 = 2.397\,\%$$

> **Answer**: FFA (as oleic) = **2.40% w/w** → indicates the oil requires **chemical refining with NaOH** (since FFA > 2%).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Iodine Value Calculation (Wijs Method)

> **Problem Statement:**
> 0.30 g of sunflower oil is treated with Wijs solution. The blank titration consumes **45.20 mL of 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃**, and the sample consumes **19.80 mL**. Calculate the Iodine Value.

**Step 1 — Recall IV Formula**

$$\text{IV} = \dfrac{(B-S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$\text{IV} = \dfrac{(45.20 - 19.80) \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.30}$$

**Step 3 — Compute**

$$(45.20 - 19.80) = 25.40 \text{ mL}$$
$$25.40 \times 0.1 = 2.54$$
$$2.54 \times 12.69 = 32.2326$$
$$\dfrac{32.2326}{0.30} = 107.44$$

> **Answer**: IV = **107.44 g I₂/100 g** → confirms sunflower oil is **semi-drying** (high unsaturation, suitable for salad oil after partial hydrogenation if needed).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Refining Loss Estimation

> **Problem Statement:**
> Crude soybean oil has **FFA = 1.2%**, **Phosphatides (gums) = 1.8%**. Estimate the **refining loss** using the empirical rule.

**Step 1 — Apply Empirical Relation**

$$\text{RL (\%)} = \text{FFA \%} + \text{Gums \%} + 1.5$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$\text{RL} = 1.2 + 1.8 + 1.5 = 4.5\,\%$$

> **Interpretation**: For every **100 kg crude oil**, **4.5 kg** is lost as soapstock, gums, and entrained neutral oil → expected RBD (Refined, Bleached, Deodorized) oil yield = **95.5%**.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Hydrogenation Selectivity

> **Problem Statement:**
> During catalytic hydrogenation of soybean oil, the linoleate (L) drops from **55% → 15%**, while linolenate (P) drops from **8% → 4%**. Compute selectivity $S$.

**Step 1 — Recall Selectivity Formula**

$$S = \dfrac{\ln(L_0/L_t)}{\ln(P_0/P_t)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$S = \dfrac{\ln(55/15)}{\ln(8/4)} = \dfrac{\ln(3.667)}{\ln(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Logarithms**

$$\ln(3.667) = 1.299$$
$$\ln(2) = 0.693$$

**Step 4 — Compute**

$$S = \dfrac{1.299}{0.693} = 1.875$$

> **Interpretation**: $S > 1$ means **linoleate is reduced preferentially over linolenate** — a desirable outcome to minimize trans-fat formation. $S = 1.88$ indicates **moderate selectivity**; ideal industrial Ni catalysts operate at $S \geq 2$ under mild conditions (160 °C, 2 bar).

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Refining Routes

| Property | Chemical (Caustic) Refining | Physical (Steam) Refining |
|----------|-----------------------------|----------------------------|
| FFA Removal | Efficient (any level) | Best for FFA > 2% |
| Refining Loss | High (FFA + gums + 1.5–2%) | Lower (no soapstock) |
| Oil Quality | Light color, mild deodorization | Darker; needs strong bleaching |
| Capital Cost | Lower | Higher (vacuum + high temp) |
| Effluent (soapstock) | High (needs treatment) | None |
| Suitable Oils | Soy, sunflower, groundnut | Palm, rice bran, coconut |
| Energy Use | Moderate | High (220–260 °C, 3–8 mbar) |

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Extraction Methods

| Parameter | Mechanical Press | Solvent Extraction | Combined (Pre-press + Solvent) |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|--------------------------------|
| Oil Recovery | 85–92% | 95–99% | 98–99.5% |
| Residual Oil in Cake/Meal | 5–10% | 0.5–1.0% | 0.5–1.0% |
| Throughput | Low | High | Very High |
| Operating Cost | Low | Moderate-High | Optimal |
| Product Quality | Darker, high FFA | Cleaner miscella | Premium |
| Capital Cost | Low | Very High | High |
| Suitable Seeds | Coconut, palm kernel, high-oil seeds | Low-oil seeds (soybean) | Universal |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

### 4.1 Master Process Flow — Oilseed to Refined Oil

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Oilseeds Incoming] --> B[Cleaning Magnetic Sieves Destoners]
    B --> C[Drying Moisture less than 10 percent]
    C --> D[Dehulling Decortication]
    D --> E[Cracking Roller Mill]
    E --> F[Flaking 0.25 to 0.35 mm]
    F --> G[Cooking Conditioning 80 to 110 C]
    G --> H1[Mechanical Expeller Press]
    G --> H2[Solvent Extraction Hexane Loop]
    H1 --> I1[Crude Oil plus Cake 5 to 10 percent residual oil]
    H2 --> I2[Crude Miscella plus Meal less than 1 percent residual oil]
    I1 --> M1[Pre press Cake]
    M1 --> H2
    I1 --> J[Refining Train]
    I2 --> J[Refining Train]
    J --> K[Degumming H3PO4 Water]
    K --> L[Neutralization NaOH Caustic]
    L --> N[Bleaching Acid Clay Vacuum]
    N --> O[Deodorization Steam 220 to 260 C Vacuum]
    O --> P[RBD Oil Refined Bleached Deodorized]
    O --> Q[Winterization Optional Wax Removal]
    Q --> R[Specialty Fractions Olein Stearin]
    P --> S[Storage Packaging Nitrogen Blanketing]
    R --> S
    S --> T[Margarine Vanaspati Shortening Biodiesel]
    I2 --> U[Desolventized Toasted Meal]
    U --> V[Animal Feed Protein Supplement]
```

### 4.2 Fat Modification — Hydrogenation / Interesterification / Fractionation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RBD[RBD Oil Liquid] --> H[Hydrogenation Ni Catalyst 150 to 200 C]
    H --> HS[Hydrogenated Fat Solid Semi Solid]
    RBD --> IE[Interesterification 1 3 Specific Lipase or NaOMe]
    IE --> IEF[Modified Fat Altered Melting Profile]
    RBD --> FR[Fractionation Controlled Crystallization]
    FR --> ST[Stearin Solid Fraction]
    FR --> OL[Olein Liquid Fraction]
    HS --> M[Margarine Shortening Vanaspati]
    IEF --> M
    ST --> CB[Cocoa Butter Equivalent]
    OL --> CO[Cooking Oil Salad Oil]
```

### 4.3 Refining Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Section_X[Crude Oil Input]
        CR[Crude Oil FFA Gums Pigments Waxes]
    end
    subgraph Section_Y[Purification Stages]
        DG[Degumming Phospholipid Removal]
        NT[Neutralization Soapstock Separation]
        BL[Bleaching Adsorption Vacuum]
        DD[Deodorization Steam Stripping High Vacuum]
        WN[Winterization Wax Crystallization]
    end
    subgraph Section_Z[Final Output and By Products]
        RO[RBD Oil Finished Product]
        SK[Soapstock By Product]
        BL_E[Spent Bleaching Earth Hazardous Waste]
        SL[Deodorizer Distillate Tocopherol Recovery]
        WS[Wax Cake By Product]
    end
    CR --> DG
    DG --> NT
    NT --> BL
    BL --> DD
    DD --> WN
    WN --> RO
    NT --> SK
    BL --> BL_E
    DD --> SL
    WN --> WS
```

### 4.4 Quality Control Decision Tree for Crude Oil Acceptance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crude Oil Sample Received] --> B[Moisture and Impurities Check]
    B --> C{MI less than 0.5 percent}
    C -- Yes --> D[Proceed]
    C -- No --> E[Reject Return to Pre treatment]
    D --> F[FFA Test]
    F --> G{FFA threshold}
    G -- less than 2 percent --> H[Chemical Refining Route]
    G -- greater than 2 percent --> I[Physical Refining Route]
    G -- greater than 10 percent --> J[Reject for Industrial Use]
    H --> K[Standard RBD Process]
    I --> K
    K --> L[Pasteurize Store under Nitrogen]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Define the terms "degumming," "deodorization," and "winterization" as applied in oil refining. State the principal impurity removed in each step.*

**Model Answer:**

| Step | Definition | Impurity Removed |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **Degumming** | Treatment of crude oil with water or acid (H₃PO₄) at 60–80 °C to precipitate phospholipids. | Phosphatides (lecithin, cephalin) |
| **Deodorization** | Steam distillation at 220–260 °C under 3–8 mbar vacuum. | Aldehydes, ketones, residual FFA, odoriferous volatiles |
| **Winterization** | Slow cooling (5–10 °C) followed by filtration. | Waxes and high-melting triacylglycerols |

**(3 Marks — Each step correctly defined with impurity = 1 mark.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*List the major differences between mechanical extraction and solvent extraction of oilseeds.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Mechanical Extraction**: Uses an **expeller/screw press**; suitable for high-oil seeds; recovers 85–92% of oil; residual oil in cake = 5–10%; lower capital cost.
2. **Solvent Extraction**: Uses **n-hexane** in a continuous loop extractor; recovers 95–99% of oil; residual oil in meal ≤ 1%; suitable for low-oil seeds like soybean; high capital and operating cost.

**(3 Marks — Comparison with key parameters.)**

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the complete **unit operations involved in the pre-treatment of oilseeds** prior to oil extraction, with emphasis on the engineering rationale of **flaking** and **cooking**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **solvent extraction process** for soybean oil in detail, including the role of **miscella**, **desolventizer-toaster-dryer-cooler (DTDC)**, and the typical process parameters. Compute the extraction yield if 1500 kg of soybean flakes (lipid content 20%) yields 285 kg of crude oil. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pre-treatment Operations (7 Marks)**

1. **Cleaning (1 M)**: Removal of stones, chaff, metallic particles using vibrating screens, magnetic separators, and aspirators. Purpose: prevents equipment damage and ensures uniform downstream processing.
2. **Drying (1 M)**: Moisture reduced to ≤ 10% w/w to inactivate **lipases** (which catalyze hydrolysis of TAGs → free fatty acids, causing hydrolytic rancidity).
3. **Dehulling/Decortication (1 M)**: Mechanical abrasion removes fibrous hull. Improves protein content of meal (e.g., soybean meal increases from 44% → 48% protein) and reduces refining loss (removes pigment-rich hull).
4. **Cracking (1 M)**: Corrugated rollers reduce kernels into 4–8 pieces for uniform flaking.
5. **Flaking (1.5 M)**: Smooth rollers compress kernels into **0.25–0.35 mm** flakes. *Engineering rationale*: Reduces the diffusion path length for oil during solvent extraction; provides uniform surface for solvent contact; ruptures a significant fraction of cell walls.
6. **Cooking/Conditioning (1.5 M)**: Flakes heated to **80–110 °C** with live steam. *Engineering rationale*: (i) Coagulates proteins → releases oil from oleosomes; (ii) reduces oil viscosity → better flow; (iii) inactivates lipases, myrosinase (rapeseed), and lipoxygenases; (iv) moisture adjustment to 3% (pressing) or 6–10% (extraction).

---

**(b) Solvent Extraction — Soybean (7 Marks)**

**Process Description (4 Marks):**
- **Solvent**: Food-grade **n-hexane** (boiling point 65–70 °C, low toxicity, high oil solubility).
- **Equipment**: Continuous **loop-type extractor** (Hildebrand, De Smet, Lurgi). Flakes enter one end; miscella (oil + hexane) leaves from the other. Counter-current flow.
- **Miscella**: The oil-rich solution exiting the extractor (typically 25–35% oil in hexane). It is filtered and sent to **evaporators** (rising-film and falling-film) where hexane is stripped by indirect steam heating → recovered crude oil. Hexane vapors are condensed and recycled.
- **DTDC (Desolventizer-Toaster-Dryer-Cooler) (2 Marks)**: The wet meal (containing ~30% hexane) is processed through four stages:
  - **Desolventizing**: Spent steam strips residual hexane (~70–80 °C).
  - **Toasting**: 100–110 °C with live steam to destroy anti-nutritional factors (e.g., trypsin inhibitors in soybean).
  - **Drying**: Reduces moisture to 10–12%.
  - **Cooling**: Cools meal to 30–35 °C for storage.
- **Process Parameters**: Flake thickness 0.25 mm; residence time in extractor 60–90 min; miscella concentration 25–35%; hexane-to-flake ratio 1.0–1.2 : 1.

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given:
$$W_{\text{seed}} = 1500 \text{ kg}, \quad f_{\text{lipid}} = 0.20, \quad W_{\text{oil}} = 285 \text{ kg}$$

$$M_{\text{lipid available}} = 1500 \times 0.20 = 300 \text{ kg}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{285}{300} \times 100 = 95.0\,\%$$

> **Answer**: Extraction yield = **95.0%** (acceptable industry standard).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the principles of **hydrogenation, interesterification, and fractionation** as fat modification techniques. Compare their effect on **trans-fat formation** and **functional properties**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sample of refined sunflower oil (5.00 g) gave a titration reading of **3.85 mL with 0.1 N KOH**. Calculate the **% FFA (as oleic acid)**. If the FFA exceeds 2%, recommend the most suitable refining route and justify. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fat Modification Techniques (7 Marks)**

**1. Hydrogenation (2 Marks)**
- **Principle**: Catalytic addition of H₂ across C=C double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids using **Ni catalyst (0.05–0.2%)** at 150–200 °C and 1–5 bar pressure.
- **Effect**: Converts liquid oils to semi-solid fats; raises melting point, improves oxidative stability.
- **Drawback**: Produces **trans-fatty acids (TFAs)** due to half-hydrogenation intermediates. TFAs are linked to cardiovascular disease (WHO recommends < 1% of energy intake).

**2. Interesterification (2.5 Marks)**
- **Principle**: Rearrangement of fatty acids among glycerol positions **without changing degree of unsaturation**.
- **Chemical IE**: NaOMe catalyst at 80–100 °C → random distribution.
- **Enzymatic IE**: 1,3-specific lipases (e.g., *Rhizomucor miehei*) → produces 1,3-distributed TAGs.
- **Effect**: Modifies melting profile, plasticity, and crystallization behavior.
- **Advantage**: **Zero trans-fat formation**; increasingly preferred over hydrogenation.

**3. Fractionation (2.5 Marks)**
- **Principle**: Selective crystallization of high-melting TAGs by controlled cooling → separation into **stearin (solid)** and **olein (liquid)** by filtration.
- **Dry Fractionation**: Most common; no solvent used.
- **Solvent Fractionation**: Acetone/IPA → sharper separation but expensive.
- **Effect**: Yields specialty fractions — e.g., palm olein (cooking oil), palm stearin (margarine), cocoa butter equivalents from shea/illipe.

**Comparison Table (Bonus — Examiner-friendly)**

| Technique | Trans-fat | Functional Change |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| Hydrogenation | High (5–45%) | Saturation ↑, melting point ↑ |
| Interesterification | 0% | Crystallization profile altered |
| Fractionation | 0% | Separation by melting point |

---

**(b) FFA Determination (7 Marks)**

**Calculation (4 Marks):**

$$\text{FFA \%} = \dfrac{V \times N \times E \times 100}{W \times 1000}$$

Substituting:
$$\text{FFA \%} = \dfrac{3.85 \times 0.1 \times 282 \times 100}{5.00 \times 1000}$$

$$= \dfrac{3.85 \times 0.1 \times 282}{5.00 \times 10}$$

$$= \dfrac{108.57}{50} = 2.171\,\%$$

> **Answer**: FFA (as oleic) = **2.17% w/w** — exceeds the 2% threshold.

**Recommendation (3 Marks):**

- **Preferred Route**: **Physical Refining (Steam Refining)** — because at high FFA (> 2%), physical refining has lower oil loss and avoids soapstock disposal. However, the oil must be **thoroughly degummed** to reduce phosphorus content below 5 ppm (to avoid darkening at high deodorization temperatures).
- **Justification**: Chemical refining at this FFA would produce excessive soapstock (≈ 4.5% loss), while physical refining incurs a loss of only ~2.5% (essentially the FFA itself).
- **Additional Step**: Pre-bleaching with **acid-activated clay (0.5–1.0%)** is essential to decompose non-hydratable phosphatides before steam stripping at 240–260 °C under 3–5 mbar vacuum.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong Refining Route Recommendation**: Many students recommend chemical refining for *all* oils. The examiner **penalizes** this — always check FFA first.
> 2. **Mass-Balance Sign Error**: In yield problems, students often place the meal weight in the numerator. Watch units carefully.
> 3. **Confusing IV with PV**: IV measures *unsaturation* (initial quality); PV measures *oxidation* (storage deterioration). Don't interchange.
> 4. **Skipping DTDC Stages**: Forgetting toasting (anti-nutritional factor inactivation) in soybean extraction loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Missing Trans-fat Mention**: In any hydrogenation answer, **always note the TFA drawback** — KTU 2024 specifically tests this awareness.
> 6. **Wrong Emulsion Notation in Margarine**: Water-in-oil emulsion must be specified; oil-in-water is incorrect.
> 7. **Numerical Calculation Errors**: Especially in FFA — forgetting to multiply by 100 in the final step gives an answer 100× too small.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

✅ **Oilseeds** are seeds with 15–50% lipid stored in oleosomes inside cotyledon cells.

✅ **Three engineering domains**: Pre-treatment → Extraction → Refining.

✅ **Flake thickness (0.25–0.30 mm)** is critical — too thick → poor solvent diffusion; too thin → fines + channeling.

✅ **Cooking at 80–110 °C** denatures proteins, ruptures oleosomes, and inactivates enzymes.

✅ **Mechanical extraction**: expeller screw press; recovery 85–92%; residual oil in cake 5–10%.

✅ **Solvent extraction**: n-hexane; recovery 95–99%; residual oil in meal ≤ 1%.

✅ **Refining stages in order**: Degumming → Neutralization → Bleaching → Deodorization (RBD oil produced).

✅ **Physical refining** preferred when FFA > 2%; **chemical (caustic) refining** preferred when FFA < 2%.

✅ **Hydrogenation** uses Ni catalyst; produces trans-fats → WHO restriction.

✅ **Interesterification** (1,3-specific enzymatic) is the modern trans-fat-free alternative.

✅ **Fractionation** yields specialty fats: palm olein (cooking), palm stearin (margarine), CBE from shea/illipe.

✅ **Key formulas (must memorize)**:
   - Yield: $\eta = \dfrac{W_{\text{oil}}}{W_{\text{seed}} \times f_{\text{lipid}}} \times 100$
   - FFA: $\dfrac{V \times N \times E \times 100}{W \times 1000}$ (E_oleic = 282)
   - IV: $\dfrac{(B-S)\times N \times 12.69}{W}$
   - PV: $\dfrac{(S-B)\times N \times 1000}{W}$

✅ **By-products are valuable**: defatted meal (animal feed), soapstock (fatty acid source), spent bleaching earth (must be disposed as hazardous), deodorizer distillate (tocopherol/vitamin E recovery).

✅ **Quality control parameters**: Moisture ≤ 0.1%, FFA ≤ 0.1% (RBD), PV ≤ 2 meq/kg (fresh), color (Lovibond), iodine value, saponification value, slip melting point.

✅ **Industrial standards**: Residual oil in meal ≤ 1%; refining loss = FFA + gums + 1.5–2.0%; deodorization at 220–260 °C, < 5 mbar.

✅ **Hydrogenation selectivity**: $S > 1$ = preferential linoleate reduction; $S \geq 2$ desired for minimum trans-fat.

✅ **Major Indian & global context**: India is the world's largest importer of edible oil (palm from Indonesia/Malaysia); mustard (rapeseed) is the indigenous Indian oilseed.

✅ **FSSAI limits (India)**: Trans-fat in vegetable oils ≤ 2% (proposed 1% by 2026); iron-fortified edible oils encouraged; AGMARK grades for purity.

✅ **Common exam trick questions**:
   - *Why is coconut oil solid below 24 °C?* → High lauric acid (C12:0).
   - *Why is soybean oil refined before hydrogenation?* → Remove phospholipids, pigments, and FFA that poison Ni catalyst.
   - *Why must rice bran oil be processed immediately?* → Contains active lipase → rapid FFA rise.
   - *Why is palm oil fractionated?* → To separate oleine (cooking) from stearine (vanaspati/margarine).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Snack foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT862 - DEVELOPMENT OF PROCESSED FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Snack foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🍿 MODULE 3: SNACK FOODS — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEAGT862 – Development of Processed Food Products**, **Snack Foods** are defined as:

> **Snack Foods:** A category of convenience food products formulated to be consumed between main meals, characterized by specific textural attributes (crispness, crunchiness), controlled moisture content (**< 4% for fried/extruded snacks** and **6–10% for baked snacks**), extended shelf life, and ready-to-eat (RTE) convenience. They are produced through controlled unit operations such as **extrusion cooking, deep-fat frying, baking, popping, pelletization, or flaking**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3, Unit 3):**
> The 2024 scheme specifically targets the following sub-topics for ESE (End Semester Examination):
> 1. Classification of snack foods
> 2. Extruded snacks — raw materials, process, twin-screw vs single-screw
> 3. Pellet snacks (3D snacks) — formulation and processing
> 4. Popcorn and puffed snacks
> 5. Coated and flavored snacks
> 6. Quality parameters, packaging, and shelf-life

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your regular home-cooked meal — it requires preparation time, utensils, and a table setting. Now think of the small packets of food you can tear open, eat immediately with your fingers, and feel instant satisfaction. That is the world of snack foods.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a snack food factory like a controlled kitchen at industrial scale. The **dough** is your raw material (much like chapati dough), but instead of rolling and roasting on a tawa, it is forced under high pressure and temperature through a die — like squeezing toothpaste — and the sudden pressure drop causes the snack to puff up (like a balloon expanding when you release the nozzle). The water inside instantly flashes to steam, creating the airy, crunchy texture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplified Process Flow (Mental Model):**
> Raw Grains/Cereals → Milling → Mixing (with water, salt, flavor) → Cooking (heat + pressure) → Shaping/Expanding → Drying/Frying → Seasoning → Packaging

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Snack Food Science

Any snack food product is engineered by controlling three fundamental pillars:

1. **Pillar 1 — Material (Raw Material Composition):** Starch, protein, fiber, and moisture content of the base material.
2. **Pillar 2 — Process (Unit Operation):** Type of cooking (extrusion, frying, baking, popping).
3. **Pillar 3 — Texture & Sensory (Quality Attribute):** Crispness, expansion ratio, bulk density, color, and flavor.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Reference with COs (Course Outcomes):**
> - **CO3:** "Apply process technology for development of various snack food products" — *This note directly addresses this CO.*
> - **CO4:** "Evaluate quality and shelf-life of processed products" — *Quality parameters and packaging are covered.*

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Moisture Content (Extruded)** | **< 4%** | Microbial safety threshold |
| **Moisture Content (Baked)** | **6–10%** | Acceptable crispness |
| **Water Activity ($a_w$)** | **< 0.6** | Shelf stability |
| **Frying Temperature** | **150–190 °C** | Standard industrial fryer |
| **Extrusion Temperature** | **120–200 °C** | Cooking zone range |
| **Expansion Ratio (ER)** | **3–15** | Measure of puffing |
| **Bulk Density** | **0.1–0.5 g/cm³** | Light, airy snack |
| **Screw Speed (Extruder)** | **200–500 rpm** | Twin-screw range |
| **Glass Transition Temp ($T_g$)** | **~ 50–80 °C** for starches | Texture transition |
| **Residence Time in Extruder** | **10–60 seconds** | Short-time cooking |

---

## 1.5 Classification of Snack Foods (Syllabus Mandate)

Snack foods are broadly classified into **six industrial categories**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Focus:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes the following six classes. Memorize the example of each:

1. **Extruded Snacks** — *Example: Cheese balls, Kurkure, puffs (direct-expanded)*
2. **Pellet Snacks (3D Snacks)** — *Example: 3D pellet-based rings, stars, balls (half-products)*
3. **Popped / Puffed Snacks** — *Example: Popcorn, puffed rice (murmura), corn pops*
4. **Coated & Fried Snacks** — *Example: Potato chips, banana chips, namkeen*
5. **Baked Snacks** — *Example: Baked crackers, cookies, khakhra*
6. **Flaked & Toasted Snacks** — *Example: Corn flakes, chivda, baked sev*

A schematic tree is provided in **Section 4**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Sub-Topic Breakdown

### **A. EXTRUDED SNACKS — The Heart of Modern Snack Industry**

#### A.1 Definition
Extrusion cooking is a **High Temperature Short Time (HTST)** process in which moistened, starchy, and proteinaceous food material is forced through a die under controlled conditions of **temperature (120–200 °C)**, **pressure (20–150 bar)**, and **shear**, resulting in a plasticized melt that expands upon exiting the die.

#### A.2 The Four Operational Zones Inside an Extruder

The extruder barrel is conceptually divided into **four sequential zones**:

| Zone | Function | Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Feed Zone** | Receives raw material via hopper | Low temp, conveying |
| **2. Mixing / Kneading Zone** | Hydrates and mixes ingredients | 60–90 °C |
| **3. Cooking Zone** | Starch gelatinization, protein denaturation | 100–150 °C |
| **4. Die / Expansion Zone** | Final shaping and puffing | 150–200 °C, high pressure |

#### A.3 Single-Screw vs Twin-Screw Extruder (Critical for KTU)

| Feature | Single-Screw | Twin-Screw |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conveying Mechanism** | Drag flow (passive) | Positive displacement (active) |
| **Mixing Efficiency** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Residence Time Distribution** | Wide | Narrow & uniform |
| **Heat Transfer** | Limited | High (self-wiping) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Lower | Higher |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | High |
| **Best For** | Simple direct expansion | Co-extrusion, complex formulations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Expectation:** Students must be able to **compare and contrast** single vs twin-screw extruders — a guaranteed 7-mark question topic.

#### A.4 Physico-Chemical Changes During Extrusion
- **Starch Gelatinization:** Orderly starch granules absorb water and lose crystallinity.
- **Protein Denaturation:** Unfolding of protein structures.
- **Maillard Reaction:** Between reducing sugars and amino groups (gives golden color).
- **Retrogradation Suppression:** HTST minimizes post-process hardening.

---

### **B. PELLET SNACKS (3D SNACKS) — The Half-Product Innovation**

#### B.1 Concept
A **pellet snack** is a **half-finished product** manufactured at a central plant, then shipped to **satellite plants** where it is fried or expanded by the consumer-distributor. This separation of *cooking* and *expansion* is unique.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The 3D pellet (ring/star/ball shape) is dense and hard after extrusion at the factory. At the local fryer outlet, it is dropped in hot oil (180 °C, 8–12 seconds), and the trapped moisture inside instantly vaporizes → the pellet puffs up into a 3D snack.

#### B.2 Formulation Principles
- **High starch content** (corn grits, tapioca, potato) — provides puffability.
- **Low moisture** (10–14%) — enables rapid steam generation on frying.
- **Cellulating agents** — pre-gelatinized starch creates gas cells.
- **Bland base flavor** — allows oil absorption and seasoning coating.

#### B.3 Processing Steps
1. Dry-mix ingredients in a planetary or ribbon blender.
2. Feed into low-shear extruder (cooler than direct expansion: 70–100 °C).
3. Extrude through shaped die (ring, star, ball).
4. Dry pellets to **8–12% moisture**.
5. Pack in moisture-barrier pouches.
6. At retail/fryer: **fry at 175–185 °C for 8–15 s** → expansion ratio 6–12×.

---

### **C. POPPED / PUFFED SNACKS**

#### C.1 Popcorn Mechanism
Each popcorn kernel has a **pericarp** (hard outer shell) and a starchy endosperm containing 13–14% moisture. Upon heating:
- Internal water turns to steam (pressure builds to **~ 135 psi** inside kernel).
- The pericarp ruptures.
- Starch granules expand rapidly → fluffy popped corn.

**Optimal popping temperature:** **180–200 °C**.
**Popping yield:** **~ 98–99%** (industrial standard).
**Expansion ratio:** **30–40×** kernel volume.

#### C.2 Puffed Rice (Murmura)
Produced by **hot sand / hot air puffing (gun puffing)** — pre-conditioned rice grains (12–14% moisture) are rapidly heated → internal steam expansion → puffing.

---

### **D. COATED & FRIED SNACKS**

#### D.1 Deep-Fat Frying Physics
Frying is simultaneous heat and mass transfer. The governing rate equation is:

$$ J_w = \frac{k_m \cdot (a_w^{\text{surface}} - a_w^{\text{core}})}{L} $$

Where:
- $J_w$ = water flux (kg water / m²·s)
- $k_m$ = mass transfer coefficient
- $a_w^{\text{surface}}$ = water activity at surface
- $a_w^{\text{core}}$ = water activity at core
- $L$ = characteristic thickness (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does frying work?** Water inside the snack boils at ~ 100 °C, while the oil is at 150–190 °C. The escaping steam creates a **porous internal structure**, while the dehydrated outer surface becomes crisp and golden due to the **Maillard reaction**.

#### D.2 Oil Uptake Mechanism
Oil enters the snack via **two pathways**:
1. **Surface adhesion** (during removal from oil).
2. **Capillary suction** during cooling — as steam escapes and condenses, a pressure differential draws oil into the porous structure.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Mitigation:** Pre-drying the product, using **hydrocolloid coatings** (methylcellulose, pectin), and using **vacuum frying** significantly reduce oil uptake.

---

### **E. BAKED SNACKS**

#### E.1 Why Bake?
Baking offers a **healthier alternative** to frying (lower fat content: 4–10% vs 20–35%). However, baked snacks have a **denser texture** and require precise humidity control.

#### E.2 Parameters
- **Oven temperature:** 160–220 °C
- **Baking time:** 5–15 minutes
- **Final moisture:** 6–10%
- **Dough moisture (pre-bake):** 28–35%

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **One-page revision chart — memorize this for the 14-mark question.**

| Snack Type | Primary Process | Key Temp (°C) | Final Moisture | Expansion Ratio | Oil Content | Critical Parameter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direct-Expanded Extruded** | Hot extrusion | 150–200 | < 4% | 4–10× | 5–15% | Die pressure 30–100 bar |
| **Pellet / 3D Snack** | Cold extrusion + fry | 70–100 (extrude), 180 (fry) | 8–12% (pre-fry), < 3% (post-fry) | 6–12× | 20–30% | Pellet density 1.0–1.4 g/cm³ |
| **Popcorn** | Hot air / oil pop | 180–200 | 1–3% | 30–40× | 0–3% | Kernel moisture 13–14% |
| **Puffed Rice** | Gun puffing | 250–300 | 5–8% | 8–12× | 0–2% | Pre-conditioning moisture |
| **Potato Chips (Fried)** | Deep frying | 160–180 | 1–3% | 1× (no expansion) | 30–40% | Slice thickness 1–2 mm |
| **Baked Crackers** | Baking | 180–220 | 6–10% | 1–2× | 4–10% | Dough rheology |
| **Flaked Snacks** | Roller drying | 150–180 (roll) | < 5% | 3–5× | 5–12% | Roller gap 0.3–0.5 mm |

---

## 2.3 The "WHY–HOW" Framework (Engineering Logic)

**Q: Why is twin-screw extrusion preferred for complex snack formulations?**
**A (HOW):** Two intermeshing screws provide *self-wiping* action → continuous renewal of barrel surface → uniform heat transfer.
**A (WHY):** Starch-based mixtures with high fat/protein require homogeneous cooking. Single-screw cannot achieve this without long residence time, causing burnt patches.

**Q: Why do pellets need pre-conditioning?**
**A (HOW):** Pellets are manufactured with low moisture (10–14%) and shipped dense. The fryer outlet conditions the pellet by immersion in hot oil.
**A (WHY):** This **decouples the two HTST steps** (cooking vs expansion) — reduces distribution cost (lighter than expanded snacks) and allows **fresh frying at point-of-sale**.

**Q: Why is oil uptake high in fried snacks?**
**A (HOW):** As the snack cools, internal steam condenses, creating partial vacuum. Capillary action draws oil into the porous matrix.
**A (WHY):** Two industrial countermeasures:
1. **Pre-dry** the product surface.
2. **Apply hydrocolloid edible coating** — forms a moisture/oil barrier film.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

| Snack Segment | Indian Market Size (2024 estimate) | Growth Driver |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Extruded Snacks** | ~ ₹12,000 crore | Innovation in flavors, shapes |
| **Potato Chips** | ~ ₹8,500 crore | Healthier variants (baked) |
| **Namkeen / Savouries** | ~ ₹25,000 crore | Festive consumption |
| **Popcorn** | ~ ₹1,800 crore | Cinema & home consumption |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Application:** Companies like **PepsiCo (Kurkure, Lays)**, **ITC (Bingo!)**, and **Balaji Wafers** rely entirely on the principles covered in this module. Understanding extrusion die design and pellet formulation is direct employability in the Indian FMCG food industry.

---

## 2.5 Glass Transition Theory — The Texture Determinant

The crispness of a snack is governed by whether the product temperature is **above or below** its **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)**. A snack is **crisp** when its moisture and temperature place it in the **glassy state** (below $T_g$).

$$ T_g \approx T_g^{\text{dry}} - k \cdot W $$

Where:
- $T_g^{\text{dry}}$ = glass transition temp of dry material (e.g., ~ 220 °C for starch)
- $k$ = plasticizing constant (~ 10 °C per % moisture)
- $W$ = moisture content (% w/w)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application:** A snack stored in humid conditions absorbs moisture → $T_g$ drops → at room temperature, the snack may transition from glassy to rubbery state → **loss of crispness** (the "soggy chip" problem).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, NUMERICAL SOLUTIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Expansion Ratio Calculation (Extruded Snack)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern — 7 Mark Sub-Question]**

### Problem Statement
A corn-based snack is extruded through a 4 mm diameter die. The product expands upon exit to a 12 mm diameter cylindrical puff. Calculate the **Expansion Ratio (ER)** and **Bulk Density** if the extrudate mass per unit length is **0.85 g/cm**.

### Step 1: Define Expansion Ratio (ER)
The expansion ratio is the **ratio of the cross-sectional area of the expanded product to that of the die**.

$$ ER = \frac{A_{\text{expanded}}}{A_{\text{die}}} = \frac{\pi \cdot D_e^2 / 4}{\pi \cdot D_d^2 / 4} = \left(\frac{D_e}{D_d}\right)^2 $$

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$ ER = \left(\frac{12 \text{ mm}}{4 \text{ mm}}\right)^2 $$

$$ ER = (3)^2 = 9 $$

### Step 3: Interpret
An **ER of 9** indicates a moderately well-expanded snack. Industrial high-quality puffs typically achieve **ER = 6–15**.

### Step 4: Calculate Bulk Density ($\rho_b$)
Bulk density is the mass per unit volume of the expanded product.

$$ \rho_b = \frac{m}{A_e \cdot L} = \frac{0.85 \text{ g/cm}}{\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)(1.2 \text{ cm})^2 \cdot 1 \text{ cm}} $$

$$ \rho_b = \frac{0.85}{1.1309} = 0.7515 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark:** A good light extruded snack has bulk density of **0.1–0.5 g/cm³**. Our calculation gives 0.75 g/cm³, suggesting this is a **denser, less-expanded** snack. This could be improved by increasing feed moisture or extrusion temperature.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Glass Transition Temperature Drop on Moisture Absorption

### Problem Statement
A potato chip with initial moisture **1.5%** has $T_g^{\text{dry}} = 230$ °C and plasticizing constant $k = 8$ °C per % moisture. The chip is accidentally stored at **85% RH** and absorbs moisture until equilibrium at **7%**. Find the **new $T_g$** and comment on texture.

### Step 1: Recall the Equation

$$ T_g \approx T_g^{\text{dry}} - k \cdot W $$

### Step 2: Calculate Initial $T_g$

$$ T_{g,1} = 230 - (8 \times 1.5) $$

$$ T_{g,1} = 230 - 12 = 218 \text{ °C} $$

### Step 3: Calculate Final $T_g$ (after moisture absorption)

$$ T_{g,2} = 230 - (8 \times 7) $$

$$ T_{g,2} = 230 - 56 = 174 \text{ °C} $$

### Step 4: Texture Analysis
- **Initial state:** $T_{g,1} = 218$ °C ≫ 25 °C (room temp) → Chip is in **glassy state** → **CRISP**.
- **Final state:** $T_{g,2} = 174$ °C ≫ 25 °C → Still in **glassy state** (mathematically).
- **BUT** — the plasticization also softens cell walls, increases free volume, and **sensory crispness is lost** as the matrix becomes **rubbery-leathery** at higher moisture.

> [!WARNING]
> **Real-world lesson:** Even when $T_g$ is technically above room temperature, **moisture-induced plasticization** degrades sensory crispness. This is why snack packets have **desiccant sachets** (silica gel) and **aluminum foil laminates**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Residence Time in Twin-Screw Extruder

### Problem Statement
A co-rotating twin-screw extruder has a **barrel length of 1200 mm** and operates at **300 rpm**. The **specific throughput** is **0.025 kg·s⁻¹ per (rpm·D³)**. If the screw diameter $D = 60$ mm, estimate the **residence time**.

### Step 1: Volumetric Throughput Estimate
For twin-screw extruders, the **specific throughput** is often expressed as:

$$ Q_{\text{specific}} = 0.025 \text{ kg} / (\text{s} \cdot \text{rpm} \cdot D^3) $$

With $D$ in meters:
$$ D^3 = (0.06)^3 = 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 $$

### Step 2: Mass Throughput
$$ \dot{m} = Q_{\text{specific}} \times N \times D^3 $$

$$ \dot{m} = 0.025 \times 300 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} $$

$$ \dot{m} = 1.62 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} $$

### Step 3: Mass Held in the Barrel
Assuming filled barrel and bulk density of dough $\rho_{\text{dough}} = 700$ kg/m³:
$$ V_{\text{barrel}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.06)^2 \times 1.2 $$

$$ V_{\text{barrel}} = 3.39 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 $$

$$ m_{\text{barrel}} = \rho \times V = 700 \times 3.39 \times 10^{-3} = 2.37 \text{ kg} $$

### Step 4: Residence Time

$$ t_{\text{res}} = \frac{m_{\text{barrel}}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{2.37}{1.62 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ t_{\text{res}} \approx 1463 \text{ s} \approx 24 \text{ minutes} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical context:** Actual residence time in snack extrusion is **much shorter (10–60 s)** because the barrel is only partially filled (~30–50%). Our calculation assumed a *fully filled* barrel. The more accurate estimate is:

$$ t_{\text{res, actual}} = t_{\text{res, full}} \times \text{Fill factor (0.3–0.5)} $$

$$ t_{\text{res, actual}} \approx 1463 \times 0.4 \approx 585 \text{ s} \approx 9.75 \text{ minutes} $$

For snack extrusion, **fill factor is much lower (~ 0.1–0.2)**, giving **tens of seconds**, matching industrial practice.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4: Oil Uptake in Fried Snack

### Problem Statement
A potato slice of thickness **1.5 mm** is fried at **180 °C** until final moisture is **2%**. Initial moisture is **80% (wb)**. Calculate the **oil uptake (%)** if final fat content is **32% (wb)**.

### Step 1: Mass Balance — 100 g of raw slice basis
- Initial water = 80 g
- Initial solids = 20 g
- Initial fat = 0 g (assume)

### Step 2: After Frying
Assume solids remain constant (20 g).
Let final mass of product = $M_f$.

Final moisture = 2% wb:
$$ \text{Water}_{\text{final}} = 0.02 \times M_f $$

Final solids = 20 g = (1 - 0.02) × $M_f$ ... wait, let's use directly:
$$ \text{Solids}_{\text{final}} = 0.32 \times M_f \text{ (since fat is part of solids)} $$

Wait, let's clarify: Final fat = 32% wb means fat = 0.32 × $M_f$.

If we assume non-fat solids are conserved at 20 g (raw basis), but here we work in product basis:
Non-fat solids = 20 g (since only water leaves, fat enters).

Let $x$ = final mass, fat = 0.32$x$, water = 0.02$x$, non-fat solids = 20 g.

$$ x = 0.32x + 0.02x + 20 $$

$$ x - 0.34x = 20 $$

$$ 0.66x = 20 $$

$$ x = 30.3 \text{ g} $$

### Step 3: Oil Uptake

$$ \text{Oil}_{\text{final}} = 0.32 \times 30.3 = 9.7 \text{ g} $$

### Step 4: Express as % of Raw Slice

$$ \text{Oil uptake (\%)} = \frac{9.7}{100} \times 100 = 9.7\% $$

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial relevance:** Oil uptake of ~ 10% (raw basis) is typical for potato chips. Reducing this is a major R&D focus using coatings, pre-drying, and vacuum frying.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Problem 5: Popping Yield of Popcorn

### Problem Statement
A 100 g batch of popcorn kernels yields **380 g of popped corn**. Calculate:
1. Popping yield (volume expansion, mL popped / g kernel)
2. Unpopped kernels percentage if 3 kernels remained unpopped (assume 1000 kernels per 30 g).

### Step 1: Volume Expansion (if 1 g popped corn = 15 mL)
Total popped volume = 380 × 15 = **5,700 mL**
Expansion = 5,700 / 100 = **57 mL/g**

### Step 2: Unpopped Kernels
Total kernels in 100 g = (100/30) × 1000 = **3,333 kernels**
Unpopped % = (3/3,333) × 100 = **0.09%**

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry target:** > 99% popping, expansion ratio > 35 mL/g. This batch is **premium grade**.

---

## 3.6 Summary Table: Analytical Solutions at a Glance

| Problem | Key Result | Industrial Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ER Calculation** | ER = 9 | Moderately expanded; aim for 10–15 |
| **$T_g$ Drop** | $T_g$ drops 44 °C | Texture plastization warning |
| **Residence Time** | ~ 24 min (filled), ~ 2–4 min (realistic) | HTST confirmed |
| **Oil Uptake** | 9.7% (raw basis) | Within normal range |
| **Popping Yield** | 57 mL/g | Premium grade popcorn |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.2 Twin-Screw Extruder Functional Architecture (Engineering Cross-Section)

> [!TIP]
> The following Mermaid block represents the **engineering architecture** of a co-rotating twin-screw extruder — the most critical equipment in the modern snack industry.

```mermaid
graph LR
    hopper[FEED HOPPER] --> fz[FEED ZONE Conveying]
    fz --> mz[MIXING ZONE 60 to 90 C]
    mz --> cz[COOKING ZONE 100 to 150 C]

    dz --> die[SHAPED DIE]

    cutter --> exp[EXPANSION FLASH STEAM]
    exp --> dry[DRYING COOLING]
    dry --> season[SEASONING DRUM]
    season --> pack[PACKAGING]

    fz -.feeds.-> mz
    mz -.heats.-> cz
    cz -.pressurizes.-> dz

    ctrl[PLC CONTROL PANEL Temp Pressure RPM] -.controls.-> fz
    ctrl -.controls.-> mz
    ctrl -.controls.-> cz
    ctrl -.controls.-> dz

```

```mermaid
    lex --> shapedie[SHAPED DIE ring ball star]

```

## 4.4 The Crispness–Moisture Relationship (Glass Transition Concept Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Snack Product] --> B[Water Activity aw]
    A --> C[Temperature T]
    A --> D[Glass Transition Tg]

    B --> B1{aw greater than 0.6}
    B1 -- Yes --> X1[Microbial growth risk]
    B1 -- No --> X2[Microbial safe]

    C --> C1{T greater than Tg}
    C1 -- Yes --> Y1[Rubbery state SOFT]
    C1 -- No --> Y2[Glassy state CRISP]

    D --> D1[Moisture increases]

    D2 --> D3[Texture loss STALE]
```


---

## 4.6 Packaging Architecture for Snack Foods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Packaging is a **CO4-mapped** syllabus area. Snacks require **moisture, oxygen, and light barriers** to maintain $a_w < 0.6$ and prevent oxidative rancidity.

| Layer | Material | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Outer Layer (Print)** | PET (Polyester) | Printability, gloss |
| **Middle Barrier** | Aluminum Foil (7–9 µm) | Oxygen, light, moisture barrier |
| **Inner Sealant** | LLDPE / CPP | Heat seal, food contact safe |

**Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** N₂ flushing displaces O₂ → prevents fat oxidation → extends shelf life from 3 months to 6–12 months.

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Q: Differentiate between single-screw and twin-screw extruders in the context of snack food processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single-Screw | Twin-Screw |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mixing | Limited, drag flow only | Excellent, positive displacement |
| Heat transfer | Poor | Self-wiping, uniform |
| Residence time | Wide distribution (3–5 min) | Narrow, uniform (10–60 s) |
| Cost & complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Best for | Cereal cooking, simple puffs | Co-extrusion, complex recipes, snacks with fats |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Twin-screw extruders are preferred for **direct-expanded snack production** due to better mixing, controlled residence time, and ability to handle high-fat formulations.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Q: What are pellet snacks? Explain the principle behind their expansion during frying.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Pellet snacks (also called 3D snacks) are **dense, half-finished extruded products** manufactured with low moisture (10–14%) at a central plant, then distributed to retail fry-outlets for final expansion.
- **Principle of expansion (2 marks):** During frying at 175–185 °C, the **trapped moisture inside the dense pellet rapidly vaporizes**, creating internal steam pressure of 4–6 bar. The starch matrix softens, the gas cells expand, and the pellet **puffs up to 6–12 times** its original volume within 8–15 seconds. Upon cooling, the structure sets as a porous, crispy 3D snack.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers **internal choice** between two full-length alternatives. We provide both **Question A** and **Question B** as parallel options, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Focus: Extrusion & Process Parameters

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]**
**Q: Describe in detail the construction and working of a co-rotating twin-screw extruder for the manufacture of direct-expanded snack foods. Include a labeled diagram and discuss the role of each zone.**

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Define HTST extrusion; mention the role of high temperature (120–200 °C), pressure (30–100 bar), and shear in cooking and expanding starch-based foods.

2. **Construction (2 marks):** Mention:
   - Feed hopper with volumetric or gravimetric feeder
   - Barrel (modular, segmented, jacketed for heating/cooling)
   - Two intermeshing screws rotating in the same direction (co-rotating)
   - Screw elements: conveying, kneading, reverse-pumping
   - Die plate (shapes: ring, star, ball, curl)
   - Rotary cutter (face-cut)
   - PLC control panel for temperature, pressure, RPM

3. **Four Functional Zones (2 marks):**
   - **Feed Zone:** Receives dry material via hopper, ambient temp.
   - **Mixing/Kneading Zone:** Water injection, hydrates starch (60–90 °C).
   - **Cooking Zone:** Starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, Maillard browning (100–150 °C).
   - **Die/Expansion Zone:** Final cooking (150–200 °C), pressure builds up, sudden release at die → flash evaporation → puffing.

4. **Process Variables and Their Effects (2 marks):**
   - **Screw speed ↑** → ↑ throughput, ↓ residence time, ↓ expansion
   - **Feed moisture ↑** → ↑ expansion (up to a limit), softer texture
   - **Barrel temp ↑** → ↑ expansion, darker color
   - **Die diameter ↓** → ↑ expansion, more shear

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]**
**Q: Discuss the quality parameters used to evaluate extruded snack foods. How are they measured and what are the standard specifications?**

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Physical Parameters (3 marks):**
   - **Expansion Ratio (ER):** Cross-sectional area of extrudate / die area; measured by vernier caliper; standard: 4–15.
   - **Bulk Density (g/cm³):** Mass per unit volume; measured by seed displacement or rapeseed method; standard: 0.1–0.5 g/cm³.
   - **Hardness/Crispness:** Measured by Texture Analyzer (3-point bend or puncture); reported in Newton (N).
   - **Color:** Measured by Hunter Lab colorimeter; L* (lightness), a* (redness), b* (yellowness); L* typically 60–85.
   - **Moisture:** Hot air oven (105 °C, 3 h) or moisture analyzer; standard < 4%.

2. **Chemical Parameters (2 marks):**
   - **Fat content:** Soxhlet extraction; standard 5–15% (extruded) vs 20–35% (fried).
   - **Water activity ($a_w$):** Measured by $a_w$ meter (Rotronic, AquaLab); standard < 0.6.
   - **Acidity & Peroxide Value:** For shelf-life assessment; PV < 10 meq/kg for fresh snacks.

3. **Sensory Parameters (1 mark):**
   - **9-point Hedonic Scale** for overall acceptability.
   - **Crispness score** (1–5 scale by trained panel).

4. **Microbiological (1 mark):**
   - Total plate count < 10⁴ cfu/g; yeast & mold < 10² cfu/g; coliforms absent.

---

### **📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Focus: Pellet Snacks & Quality Engineering

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]**
**Q: What are 3D pellet snacks? Explain their formulation, manufacturing process, and the physics of expansion during frying at the retail outlet.**

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** 3D pellet snacks are **dense, half-finished, half-products** made by low-shear extrusion at a central plant, designed to be **fried or microwaved** at the point of sale to achieve 6–12× expansion.

2. **Formulation (2 marks):**
   - **Starch base (60–80%):** Corn grits, tapioca, potato starch — high amylopectin preferred.
   - **Cellulating agent (5–10%):** Pre-gelatinized starch creates gas cells.
   - **Salt (1–2%):** Flavor.
   - **Emulsifier (0.5–1%):** Mono-/diglycerides — improves texture.
   - **Water (10–14%):** Critical for steam generation on frying.

3. **Manufacturing Process (2 marks):**
   - Dry blending → preconditioning (water addition) → low-shear extrusion (70–100 °C) → die shaping (ring, ball, star) → pre-drying (50 °C, 1 h) → packaging in moisture-barrier pouches → shipment to retail fry-outlets.

4. **Physics of Expansion (2 marks):**
   - On immersion in 180 °C oil, the **surface temperature of the pellet exceeds 100 °C within 2–3 seconds**.
   - Internal moisture **flashes to steam** → pressure builds to 4–6 bar inside.
   - The **starch matrix softens (exceeds $T_g$)** → plastic deformation allows expansion.
   - As the pellet exits the oil and cools, the matrix **resolidifies in the puffed shape**.
   - The result is a **low-density (0.05–0.15 g/cm³), crispy, 3D snack** with porous microstructure.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]**
**Q: Explain the concept of glass transition temperature ($T_g$) and its relevance in maintaining the crispness and shelf-life of snack foods. Discuss how packaging controls moisture and oxygen to preserve quality.**

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

1. **Glass Transition Theory (2 marks):**
   - $T_g$ is the temperature at which an amorphous polymer (starch, protein) transitions from a **rigid glassy state** to a soft rubbery state.
   - A snack is **crisp when stored below $T_g$** and loses crispness as $T \to T_g$.
   - The Gordon-Taylor / Couchman-Karasz equation relates $T_g$ to moisture:

$$ T_g^{\text{mix}} = \frac{w_1 T_{g,1} + k \cdot w_2 T_{g,2}}{w_1 + k \cdot w_2} $$

where $w_1, w_2$ are mass fractions and $k$ is a constant.

2. **Effect of Moisture on $T_g$ (1 mark):**
   - Water acts as a **plasticizer** — it depresses $T_g$.
   - For starch, $T_g$ drops by **~ 6–10 °C per 1% increase in moisture**.
   - A snack that absorbs moisture to 8–10% may have $T_g$ drop near room temperature → **soggy texture**.

3. **Role of Packaging in Shelf-Life (3 marks):**
   - **Moisture Barrier:** Aluminum foil laminate (PET/AL/PE) gives **Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR) < 0.5 g/m²·day**.
   - **Oxygen Barrier:** Aluminum foil + nitrogen flushing reduces O₂ < 2% — prevents **oxidative rancidity** of fats.
   - **Light Barrier:** Metallized layer blocks UV — prevents photo-oxidation.
   - **Desiccant Sachet:** Silica gel (1–3 g) inside the pouch keeps internal RH < 30%.
   - **MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging):** N₂ flushing extends shelf-life from 3 months to 9–12 months.

4. **Standard Shelf-Life Specifications (1 mark):**
   - Extruded snacks: 6–9 months in laminated pouches.
   - Fried snacks: 4–6 months (due to fat oxidation).
   - Baked snacks: 3–6 months.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in Snack Food Questions (Based on Examiner Reports):**
>
> 1. **❌ Confusing Extrusion with Pelletization (–2 marks):** Students often state that pellet snacks are also "extruded" without clarifying that they use **low-shear cold extrusion** (70–100 °C), unlike direct-expanded snacks (150–200 °C).
>
> 2. **❌ Forgetting the Role of Water in Frying (–2 marks):** Always mention that **water → steam** drives the porous structure in frying. Many students say "frying makes it crispy" without explaining the heat-mass transfer mechanism.
>
> 3. **❌ Mixing up Glass Transition with Melting Point (–1 mark):** $T_g$ is **NOT** the melting point. It is a second-order transition. Use the phrase "transition from glassy to rubbery state."
>
> 4. **❌ Missing the Die Pressure Concept (–2 marks):** The die pressure (30–100 bar) is what causes **flash evaporation** upon exit. Without high pressure, there is no expansion.
>
> 5. **❌ Not Linking Quality Parameters to CO4 (–1 mark):** Always tie your answer to **$a_w$ < 0.6, moisture < 4%, and standard plate count < 10⁴ cfu/g** when discussing shelf-life.
>
> 6. **❌ Omitting Safety in Popcorn (–1 mark):** Always mention the **pericarp integrity** and **internal pressure ~ 135 psi** as the popping mechanism — not just "heating."

---

## 5.4 🌟 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before walking into the exam hall. Each ✓ = a potential 1–2 mark direct pickup.**

- ✅ Snack food = RTE convenience product with **crisp texture** and **low moisture (< 4% fried, 6–10% baked)**.
- ✅ Six industrial classes: **Extruded, Pellet, Popped, Fried, Baked, Flaked**.
- ✅ HTST extrusion = **High Temperature Short Time**, with **4 zones** (Feed → Mix → Cook → Die).
- ✅ Twin-screw > Single-screw for snacks (mixing, HTST, narrow residence time).
- ✅ Expansion ratio = **$(D_e / D_d)^2$**; typical 4–15.
- ✅ Bulk density target for extruded snacks: **0.1–0.5 g/cm³**.
- ✅ Pellet snacks = **half-products**, expanded **6–12× at retail fry-outlet** in 8–15 s.
- ✅ Popcorn popping: pericarp rupture at **~ 135 psi internal pressure**; **30–40× expansion**.
- ✅ Frying = simultaneous **heat + mass transfer**; oil uptake via **capillary suction on cooling**.
- ✅ Glass transition temperature $T_g$ determines crispness; water = plasticizer that **lowers $T_g$**.
- ✅ Quality parameters: ER, bulk density, hardness, color (L\*a\*b\*), moisture, $a_w$, fat %, TPC.
- ✅ Packaging = **PET/Al/PE laminate** + N₂ flushing + desiccant → shelf life 6–9 months.
- ✅ Standard $a_w$ for shelf-stable snacks: **< 0.6**.
- ✅ Industrial benchmark oils: **palmolein, sunflower**; frying temp **150–190 °C**.
- ✅ Maillard reaction → **golden-brown color** in both extrusion and frying.
- ✅ Starch gelatinization occurs at **60–80 °C** in presence of water.
- ✅ Critical $T_g$ drop: **~ 6–10 °C per 1% moisture gain** in starchy snacks.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 3: SNACK FOODS (PEAGT862)**
