# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram — Thermal Processing Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Food Material] --> B{Cleaning & Grading}
    B --> C[Blanching 70-100 C / 1-10 min]
    C --> D{Thermal Route Selection}
    D --> E[Pasteurization HTST 72 C / 15 s]
    D --> F[Evaporation Multiple Effect]
    D --> G[Drying / Dehydration Hot Air]
    D --> H[Distillation Volatility Based]
    E --> I[Packaging Refrigerated]
    F --> J[Concentrated Product]
    G --> K[Dried Stable Product]
    H --> L[Purified Volatile Fraction]
    J --> M[Market / Storage]
    K --> M
    I --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Multiple-Effect Evaporator (Triple Effect)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Live Steam In] --> B[Effect 1 Highest Pressure]
    B --> C[Vapor to Effect 2 as Heating Medium]
    C --> D[Vapor to Effect 3 as Heating Medium]
    D --> E[Final Vapor to Condenser]
    B --> F[Concentrate to Effect 2]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Final Concentrate Pump Out]
    G --> H[Cooling Storage]
    H --> I[Packing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each "Effect" is a heat exchanger with the previous effect's vapor on one side and the food on the other. The pressure drops from Effect 1 → 3 so the boiling point of food falls, allowing lower-temperature boiling and *energy cascade reuse*.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — HTST Plate Heat Exchanger for Milk Pasteurization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Raw Cold Milk In] --> R1[Regeneration Section Pre-warm]
    R1 --> H1[Heating Section Steam Side 72 C]
    H1 --> HOLD[Hold Tube 15 s]
    HOLD --> R1
    R1 --> C1[Cooling Section Cooling Water]
    C1 --> M2[Past. Milk Out to Packing]
    FLOW[Hot Water Return from HOLD] --> H1
    COOL[Chilled Water] --> C1
```

> Note: The regeneration section saves up to **90 %** of heating and cooling energy by transferring heat from outgoing pasteurized milk to incoming cold milk.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Spray Dryer (for Milk Powder / Instant Coffee)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Feed Concentrate] --> B[Atomizer Disc or Nozzle]
    B --> C[Drying Chamber Hot Air In]
    C --> D[Droplets Formed Micron Size]
    D --> E[Moisture Evaporates in Seconds]
    E --> F[Dry Powder Falls]
    F --> G[Cyclone Separator Air Out]
    G --> H[Powder Collection]
    H --> I[Bag Filter Fines Recovery]
    I --> J[Packaging]
    CK[Exhaust Air] --> G
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Mechanical Separation Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heterogeneous Feed] --> B{Particle Size}
    B --> C[Large > 100 micron]
    B --> D[Fine 1 to 100 micron]
    B --> E[Very Fine Colloidal]
    C --> F[Sieving / Screening]
    D --> G[Filtration Cake Type]
    E --> H[Centrifugation]
    F --> I[Two Size Fractions]
    G --> J[Filter Cake plus Filtrate]
    H --> K[Dense Phase plus Light Phase]
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Disc-Stack Centrifuge (Cream Separator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Whole Milk Feed] --> IN[Inlet at Centre]
    IN --> DSP[Disc Stack Rotating]
    DSP --> HV[Heavy Phase Skim Milk Moves Outward]
    DSP --> LV[Light Phase Cream Moves Inward]
    HV --> OUT1[Skim Milk Outlet]
    LV --> OUT2[Cream Outlet]
    MTR[Electric Motor at 6000 to 10000 rpm] --> DSP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Centrifugal force pushes **denser** skim milk **outward** to the bowl wall; **lighter** cream migrates **inward** toward the axis. This is opposite to gravity, which would let cream float up.

## 4.7 Block Diagram — Plate & Frame Filter Press

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SL[Slurry Feed Pump] --> IN[Inlet Manifold]
    IN --> P1[Plate 1 Chamber]
    P1 --> P2[Plate 2 Chamber]
    P2 --> P3[Plate N Chamber]
    P3 --> OUT[Filtrate Collection]
    CAKE[Cake Forms on Filter Cloth]
    PP[Pressing Hydraulic Ram] --> IN
    PP --> Q[Open Frame Cake Discharge]
```

## 4.8 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node IDs in the diagrams above use **pure alphanumeric strings** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `P1`, `DSP`, `MTR`) without reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style`. Labels are written in raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting, italics, or HTML tags — ensuring clean rendering in GitLab, GitHub, and Notion previews.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define pasteurization. Differentiate clearly between LTLT and HTST pasteurization methods.**

**Model Answer:**

**Pasteurization** is a controlled heat treatment given to liquid foods (typically milk) to destroy pathogenic vegetative microorganisms while preserving the sensory and nutritional quality of the product. It is named after Louis Pasteur and follows the principle that a brief exposure to moderate heat kills disease-causing bacteria without significantly altering the food.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | LTLT (Batch) | HTST (Continuous) |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | **63 °C** | **72 °C** |
| Holding time | **30 min** | **15 s** |
| Equipment | Vat / kettle | Plate heat exchanger |
| Mode | Batch | Continuous |
| Energy efficiency | Low | High (90 % regeneration) |
| Suitable for | Small dairies | Large industrial scale |

HTST is preferred industrially because of higher throughput, lower energy cost (due to regeneration section), and better retention of flavor and vitamins due to short hold time. **(3 marks)** — Awarded for definition (1 mark), comparison table (1 mark), and correct reasoning (1 mark).

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the principle of a disc-stack centrifuge as used for milk cream separation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **disc-stack centrifuge** is a high-speed rotating bowl fitted with a stack of conical discs (typically 50–120 cones spaced 0.5–2 mm apart). Whole milk is fed into the centre of the rotating bowl through an inlet.

**Principle:** When the bowl rotates at 6,000–10,000 rpm, the centrifugal force creates an effective acceleration of **5,000–10,000 g**. This forces the **denser skim milk outward** along the underside of the discs to the bowl wall, where it is discharged. The **lighter cream** migrates inward along the upper side of the discs and exits through a separate outlet. The conical disc stack shortens the settling distance, dramatically increasing separation efficiency.

**Diagram (schematic):**

```
        Motor (top)
            |
        ||||||| <--- Shaft
        |     |
        | / / /| <--- Disc stack (cones)
        |/ / / |
        |     | --> Heavy (skim) phase outlet
        |     | --> Light (cream) phase outlet
        ======  Bowl wall
```

Compared to gravity settling, disc-stack separator achieves separation in **seconds instead of hours**. **(3 marks)** — Awarded for principle (1 mark), disc-stack function (1 mark), advantages (1 mark).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **multiple-effect evaporator** with a neat sketch. Derive the relationship between steam economy and number of effects.

**(b)** A **double-effect evaporator** is used to concentrate **20,000 kg/h of milk** from **12 % to 50 % total solids**. Saturated steam at **2.5 bar (latent heat = 2,182 kJ/kg)** is supplied. Determine:
1. Total water evaporated per hour.
2. Steam economy of the system.
3. Live steam required per hour.
4. Compare energy consumption with a single-effect evaporator.

---

**Model Solution (a) — Working & Steam Economy Derivation (7 marks):**

**Working Principle:**

A multiple-effect evaporator (MEE) is a cascade of N evaporator bodies ("effects") operating at successively lower pressures. The **vapor generated in Effect 1 is used as the heating medium for Effect 2**, and so on. Live (fresh) steam is supplied **only to Effect 1**. The food flows from Effect 1 (highest pressure) to Effect N (lowest pressure), becoming more concentrated at each stage.

**Sketch (Schematic):**

```
    ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
    │   EFFECT 1   │  │   EFFECT 2   │  │   EFFECT 3   │
    │ Highest P    │─>│  Medium P    │─>│   Lowest P   │
    └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘
          ^                                      |
          | Live Steam (only here)               v
                                    ┌──────────────────┐
                                    │   CONDENSER      │
                                    └──────────────────┘
```

**Derivation of Steam Economy:**

Let $m_s$ = live steam to Effect 1 (kg h⁻¹), $m_{w,i}$ = water evaporated in Effect $i$, $N$ = number of effects.

By mass balance on vapor:
$$m_{w,1} = m_{v,2} \quad \text{(vapor from E1 heats E2)}$$

Continuing:
$$m_{w,i} = m_{v,i+1}$$

Total water removed:
$$M = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_{w,i}$$

For equal-area assumption: $m_{w,i} = m_w$ (constant per effect), so $M = N \cdot m_w$.

For Effect 1 with feed at boiling point:
$$m_s \cdot \lambda_s = m_{w,1} \cdot \lambda_1 \implies m_s = m_w$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{E = \frac{M}{m_s} = \frac{N \cdot m_w}{m_w} = N}$$

**Real correction:** $E \approx 0.7$–$0.85 \, N$ due to boiling-point elevation (BPE) and heat losses. **(7 marks)** — Diagram (2), Working (2), Derivation (3).

---

**Model Solution (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Feed rate $F = 20{,}000$ kg h⁻¹
- Initial solids $S_i = 12\%$
- Final solids $S_f = 50\%$
- $N = 2$ effects
- $\lambda_s = 2{,}182$ kJ kg⁻¹

**Step 1 — Solids mass (constant throughout):**
$$m_{\text{solids}} = 20{,}000 \times 0.12 = 2{,}400\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Final concentrate mass:**
$$m_f = \frac{2{,}400}{0.50} = 4{,}800\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Total water evaporated:**
$$m_w^{\text{total}} = 20{,}000 - 4{,}800 = 15{,}200\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Steam economy (theoretical, for $N=2$):**
$$E = N = 2$$

**Step 5 — Live steam required:**
$$m_{\text{steam}} = \frac{m_w^{\text{total}}}{E} = \frac{15{,}200}{2} = 7{,}600\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with single-effect:**
- Single effect: $E = 1 \implies m_{\text{steam,single}} = 15{,}200$ kg h⁻¹.
- Double effect: $m_{\text{steam,double}} = 7{,}600$ kg h⁻¹.
- **Steam saved** = $15{,}200 - 7{,}600 = 7{,}600$ kg h⁻¹.
- **% saving** = $(7{,}600 / 15{,}200) \times 100 = 50\,\%$.

**Conclusion:** A double-effect evaporator halves the live-steam consumption of a single-effect evaporator for the same evaporation duty. **(7 marks)** — Each numerical step (1), comparison (1), conclusion (1).

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** What is **Stokes' Law**? Derive the expression for terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle in a viscous fluid. State the assumptions and validity criterion.

**(b)** A fruit juice contains fine pulp particles of **diameter 80 µm** and **density 1,050 kg/m³**. The juice has **density 1,020 kg/m³** and **viscosity 1.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**. Determine:
1. Terminal settling velocity of pulp particles under gravity.
2. The effective settling velocity in a tubular centrifuge running at **6,000 rpm** with **bowl radius 8 cm**.
3. Comment on the suitability of gravity sedimentation for this juice in an industrial setting.

---

**Model Solution (a) — Stokes' Law Derivation (7 marks):**

**Statement:** Stokes' Law gives the terminal (constant) settling velocity of a small spherical particle moving under gravity through a viscous fluid in the **laminar (low Reynolds number)** regime.

**Derivation:**

Force balance on a sphere of diameter $d_p$, density $\rho_p$, settling in fluid of density $\rho_f$:

1. **Weight (downward):** $F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \, \rho_p \, g$
2. **Buoyancy (upward):** $F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \, \rho_f \, g$
3. **Drag (upward, opposing motion):** $F_D = 3 \pi \mu d_p v_s$ (Stokes drag, valid for $Re_p < 0.2$)

At terminal velocity, the net force is zero:
$$F_g = F_b + F_D$$

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = 3 \pi \mu d_p \, v_s$$

Solving for $v_s$:
$$\boxed{v_s = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g \, d_p^2}{18 \, \mu}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Particle is rigid and spherical.
2. Particle settles at terminal velocity (no acceleration).
3. Particle is far from container walls and other particles (no hindrance).
4. Flow around particle is laminar ($Re_p < 0.2$).
5. Fluid is Newtonian and incompressible.

**Validity Criterion:** $Re_p = \rho_f v_s d_p / \mu < 0.2$. **(7 marks)** — Statement (1), force balance (2), derivation (2), assumptions + validity (2).

---

**Model Solution (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $d_p = 80 \times 10^{-6}$ m = $8 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $\rho_p = 1{,}050$ kg m⁻³
- $\rho_f = 1{,}020$ kg m⁻³
- $\mu = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $N = 6{,}000$ rpm
- $r = 0.08$ m

**Step 1 — Terminal settling velocity under gravity:**

$$v_s = \frac{(1050 - 1020) \times 9.81 \times (8 \times 10^{-5})^2}{18 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_s = \frac{30 \times 9.81 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-9}}{0.027}$$

$$v_s = \frac{1.8835 \times 10^{-6}}{0.027} = 6.98 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m/s}$$

$$v_s \approx 6.98 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m/s} = 0.251\,\text{m/h}$$

**Step 2 — Verify Stokes validity:**
$$Re_p = \frac{\rho_f v_s d_p}{\mu} = \frac{1020 \times 6.98 \times 10^{-5} \times 8 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$Re_p \approx 3.8 \times 10^{-3} \ll 0.2 \quad \text{(Stokes' Law valid ✓)}$$

**Step 3 — Centrifugal g-factor at 6,000 rpm, $r = 0.08$ m:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 6000}{60} = 628.3\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$Z = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} = \frac{(628.3)^2 \times 0.08}{9.81}$$

$$Z = \frac{394{,}784 \times 0.08}{9.81} \approx 3{,}219$$

**Step 4 — Effective settling velocity in centrifuge:**

$$v_s^{\text{centrifuge}} = Z \cdot v_s^{\text{gravity}} = 3{,}219 \times 6.98 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.225\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Industrial Comment:**
- At gravity only, pulp settles at **0.25 m/h** → to settle in a 1 m tank takes **~4 hours** (impractical).
- In centrifuge at 6,000 rpm → settling rate ~**3,200× faster**, completing separation in **seconds**.
- **Conclusion:** Gravity sedimentation is **unsuitable** for industrial fruit-juice clarification due to slow rate. Centrifugation is the preferred industrial method. **(7 marks)** — Each step (1.5), validity check (0.5), industrial comment (1).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 4 Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Moisture Content:** Students often confuse **wet basis (wb)** with **dry basis (db)**. Question explicitly stating "dry basis" must be answered with $X = m_w/m_s$, not $m_w/(m_w + m_s)$. Mixing these **costs 1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Steam Economy:** Many students write $E = N \cdot 0.85$ as a *general* formula without stating the **assumptions** (equal-area, equal-rate, feed at boiling point). Always list assumptions to fetch full marks.
> 3. **Stokes' Law:** The classic error is to forget the **validity check** ($Re_p < 0.2$). Even if you compute a numerical answer, **0.5 mark is reserved for the validity criterion**.
> 4. **Centrifugal g-factor:** Use $\omega$ in **rad/s**, not rpm. Convert correctly: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
> 5. **Pasteurization standards:** Do **not** swap HTST and LTLT temperatures. Memorize: **63 °C / 30 min** = LTLT; **72 °C / 15 s** = HTST.
> 6. **Distillation:** Always check whether the system forms an **azeotrope** (ethanol-water does, at 95.6 % ethanol). Above this, distillation alone fails — a follow-up step (e.g., molecular sieve, azeotropic distillation) is required.
> 7. **Filtration equations:** When deriving Ruth's equation, **do not** drop the $\beta V^2/2$ term — it is the cake resistance term and is *the* reason cake filtration slows down with time.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🔥 A. Thermal Processing — Must-Memorize Triggers
- [ ] **Pasteurization LTLT** = 63 °C / 30 min. **HTST** = 72 °C / 15 s. **UHT** = 135–150 °C / 2–5 s.
- [ ] **Blanching** is **enzyme inactivation**, not sterilization. **Peroxidase test** = adequacy index.
- [ ] **Sterilization in canning** = 121 °C / 15 min at 15 psi (retort conditions). This is **commercial** sterility.
- [ ] **Evaporation Steam Economy** = $E = N$ (theoretical), $E \approx 0.8\,N$ (real).

### 🔥 B. Drying & Moisture — Formula Triad
- [ ] $M_{wb} = m_w/(m_w+m_s)$; $M_{db} = m_w/m_s$.
- [ ] $M_{db} = M_{wb}/(1-M_{wb})$; $M_{wb} = M_{db}/(1+M_{db})$.
- [ ] **Constant-rate** drying: $R_c = (h_c/\lambda_w)(T_a - T_s)$.
- [ ] **Falling-rate** drying: Fick's second law governs internal diffusion.

### 🔥 C. Mechanical Separation — Five Pillars
- [ ] **Sieving** → size separation using vibrating/rotating mesh.
- [ ] **Filtration** → solid from liquid via porous medium; **Ruth's equation**: $t/V = (\mu R_m / A \Delta P) + (\mu \beta / 2A\Delta P) V$.
- [ ] **Sedimentation** → gravity settling; **Stokes' Law**: $v_s = (\rho_p - \rho_f) g d_p^2 / (18\mu)$, valid for $Re_p < 0.2$.
- [ ] **Centrifugation** → $g$-factor $Z = \omega^2 r / g$; **disc-stack** for milk, **basket** for sugar crystals.
- [ ] **Distillation** → volatility-based; **relative volatility** $\alpha = (y_A/x_A)/(y_B/x_B)$.

### 🔥 D. Equipment Identification (often asked as "match the operation")
- [ ] **Tray dryer** → fruits, vegetables (low cost).
- [ ] **Spray dryer** → milk powder, instant coffee (liquid feed).
- [ ] **Drum dryer** → mashed potato, baby cereal (viscous paste).
- [ ] **Freeze dryer (lyophilizer)** → premium coffee, herbs, bioproducts.
- [ ] **Plate heat exchanger** → HTST pasteurization.
- [ ] **Multiple-effect evaporator** → milk concentrate, tomato paste, fruit juice concentrate.
- [ ] **Disc-stack centrifuge** → cream separation, oil refining.
- [ ] **Plate & frame filter press** → sugarcane juice, beer, wine clarification.
- [ ] **Vibratory screen / gyratory sifter** → flour grading, spice sorting.

### 🔥 E. Numericals Frequently Asked in KTU ESE
- [ ] Steam economy with multiple effects.
- [ ] Constant-rate drying time.
- [ ] Settling velocity (Stokes / Newton's regime).
- [ ] Centrifugal g-factor + scaled settling velocity.
- [ ] Filtration constant-pressure Ruth's equation.
- [ ] Moisture content conversion (wb ↔ db).

### 🔥 F. One-Liner Definitions (for 2-mark questions)
- **Pasteurization** = Mild heat to kill pathogens, retain quality.
- **Blanching** = Short heat to inactivate enzymes before further processing.
- **Sterilization** = Severe heat to kill all microbes including spores.
- **Evaporation** = Boiling water out of a liquid food.
- **Dehydration** = Removing water from a solid food to safe storage moisture.
- **Distillation** = Separating liquids by boiling-point difference.
- **Filtration** = Separating solids from liquids using a porous medium.
- **Sedimentation** = Gravity settling of solids in a liquid.
- **Centrifugation** = Settling under centrifugal force (high-g).
- **Sieving** = Size separation using a mesh.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical handling, conveying and elevation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT862 - DEVELOPMENT OF PROCESSED FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mechanical handling, conveying and elevation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mechanical Handling in Food Processing — Formal Definition

**Mechanical handling** refers to the science and engineering of moving, transporting, transferring, and positioning food materials (raw, intermediate, or finished) using machines and equipment powered by mechanical energy (electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic). It encompasses three sub-domains:

1. **Conveying** — Horizontal or near-horizontal transport of bulk/particulate food materials between processing points.
2. **Elevation** — Vertical or inclined lifting of materials between processing levels or to storage silos.
3. **Mechanical Handling** (broader sense) — Includes loading, unloading, stacking, palletizing, weighing, and dosing operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Mechanical handling, conveying and elevation is the systematic movement of food materials through a series of unit operations using equipment such as conveyors, elevators, hoists, and cranes, designed to minimize product damage, maintain hygiene (sanitary design), and ensure continuous material flow from raw reception to finished product dispatch.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory as a City Highway System"

Imagine a **food processing plant as a busy city**. The raw materials (milk, wheat, sugar) arrive at the "city gates" (receiving bay) and must travel through different "neighborhoods" (pasteurization → mixing → packaging → dispatch).

- **Conveyors** = **Highways & Roads** (horizontal transport)
- **Bucket Elevators** = **Vertical Flyovers / Escalators** (elevation between floors)
- **Pneumatic Conveyors** = **Underground Pipelines** (closed, dust-free movement)
- **Cranes & Hoists** = **Construction Cranes** (heavy bulk loading)

Without these transport systems, every product would need to be **hand-carried** — slow, unhygienic, and impossible at industrial scale.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Mechanical Handling Equipment

Mechanical handling equipment is broadly classified as shown below:

### A) Based on Direction of Movement

| Type | Function | Typical Equipment |
|------|----------|-------------------|
| **Horizontal Conveying** | Move materials along processing line | Belt, screw, chain, vibratory conveyors |
| **Inclined Conveying** | Transport up/down slopes (10°–25°) | Inclined belt, en-masse conveyors |
| **Vertical Elevation** | Lift materials between floors | Bucket elevators, spiral elevators, pneumatic lifts |

### B) Based on Material State

| Material Form | Suitable Equipment |
|---------------|-------------------|
| **Bulk solids** (grains, flour, sugar) | Belt, screw, bucket, pneumatic |
| **Liquids & pastes** (milk, sauce, batter) | Pumps, pipelines, screw (low speed) |
| **Discrete items** (bottles, packets, fruits) | Slat, chain, roller, pneumatic air-cushion |
| **Granules & powders** | Pneumatic, screw, vibratory |

---

## 1.4 Critical Role in Food Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic is HIGH-YIELD for KTU Exams:**
> - Appears regularly as a **Part B (14-mark)** question.
> - Examiner frequently tests: equipment selection, capacity formula, sanitary design features, and advantages/disadvantages comparison.
> - Direct linkage to **Course Outcome CO3**: *Apply unit operations in designing processed food products.*

### Key Industrial Requirements:
1. **Hygiene & Sanitation** — Equipment must be made of **stainless steel (SS 304 / SS 316)**, free of crevices, easily cleanable (CIP — Clean-In-Place).
2. **Gentle Handling** — Minimize mechanical damage to fragile foods (chips, fruits, bread).
3. **Dust Control** — Critical for flour, sugar, spices to prevent **explosion hazards (ATEX norms)**.
4. **Capacity Matching** — Conveyor capacity must match upstream/downstream equipment (no bottleneck).
5. **Energy Efficiency** — Lower kWh per ton moved = better operational economy.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used

- **Capacity (Q):** expressed in **tonnes/hour (T/h)** or **kg/min**
- **Belt speed (v):** in **m/s**
- **Power (P):** in **kW** or **HP** (1 HP = **0.7457 kW**)
- **Bulk density (ρ):** in **kg/m³**
- **Angle of repose:** critical for inclined conveyors (typically **≤ 20°–25°**)
- **Standard gravitational acceleration:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1 — Belt Conveyors

### Operating Principle
A continuous belt (rubber, PVC, or food-grade PU) is looped over two pulleys — a **head pulley** (drive) and a **tail pulley** (idler). The belt is supported by **idler rollers** in between. Food material is loaded at the tail end via a chute and discharged at the head end by centrifugal action or scraping.

### Key Design Parameters
- **Belt width (B):** typically **300 mm – 1200 mm**
- **Belt speed (v):** **0.5 – 3.0 m/s** for food applications (lower for fragile items)
- **Troughing angle:** **20°, 35°, 45°** of idlers
- **Maximum incline:** **18°–20°** for bulk food (limited by angle of repose and slip)

### Advantages
- Highest capacity per unit width
- Low power consumption
- Can handle a wide range of products
- Easy to clean (food-grade PU/PVC belts)

### Limitations
- Cannot handle sticky materials (dough, wet pulp)
- Belt wear and tracking issues
- Not suitable for very steep inclines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** For food applications, **PVC or PU food-grade belts** are preferred over rubber because rubber leaves carbon residue. White/colored belts allow visual contamination detection.

---

## 2.2 Type 2 — Screw Conveyors (Augers)

### Operating Principle
A helical screw blade (flight) rotates inside a **U-trough** or **tubular casing**, pushing material forward by the **push-pull action** of the flighting. Material moves axially due to the screw's rotation.

### Key Design Parameters
- **Screw diameter (D):** **100 mm – 600 mm**
- **Shaft speed (N):** **30 – 120 RPM** (food: lower end for gentle handling)
- **Pitch (S):** typically **S = D** (standard), **S = 0.5D** (slow), **S = 1.5D–2D** (fast)
- **Fill factor (φ):** **0.15 – 0.45** (lower for sticky, higher for free-flowing)

### Advantages
- **Enclosed design** → dust-free, hygienic
- Compact, simple construction
- Can handle powders, granules, pastes (low speed)
- Can mix and convey simultaneously

### Limitations
- High power consumption per ton
- Particle degradation at high speeds
- Limited length (max ~30 m typically)
- Not suitable for sticky, fibrous materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Industry Use:** Screw conveyors are extensively used for **flour, sugar, rice, dal, spices, and wet dough** (in pasta plants). The **tubular (pipe) screw conveyor** is preferred over U-trough for sanitary applications because it can be fully sealed and CIP-cleaned.

---

## 2.3 Type 3 — Bucket Elevators

### Operating Principle
Buckets are fixed on a **belt or chain** that runs between a **foot pulley/sprocket** (bottom, boot) and a **head pulley/sprocket** (top). Material is scooped up at the bottom, carried vertically, and discharged at the top by **centrifugal, gravity, or perfect-discharge** action.

### Types of Bucket Elevators (Discharge Method)
| Type | Discharge | Speed | Application |
|------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| **Centrifugal discharge** | Centrifugal | **1.0 – 1.5 m/s** | Free-flowing dry grains, sugar |
| **Gravity (continuous)** | Gravity | **0.5 – 0.7 m/s** | Friable/fragile (chips, flakes) |
| **Positive discharge** | Forced by chute | **0.4 – 0.6 m/s** | Sluggish/abrasive materials |

### Advantages
- Compact vertical footprint
- Can elevate to great heights (**50 m+**)
- Fully enclosed → dust-free, hygienic
- High capacity

### Limitations
- Material degradation at high speed (centrifugal type)
- Bucket wear and tear
- Requires substantial headroom

---

## 2.4 Type 4 — Pneumatic Conveyors

### Operating Principle
Food material is suspended in a **gas stream (air)** and transported through **pipes/ducts** using pressure differential generated by a **blower or compressor**.

### Modes of Operation
| Mode | Pressure | Application |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **Dilute phase (lean phase)** | Vacuum or low pressure | Short distances, light powders |
| **Dense phase** | High pressure (≥ 2 bar) | Long distances, friable products |
| **Air-activated gravity** | Air pads under material | Horizontal flow of cohesive powders |

### Advantages
- **Totally enclosed** → zero contamination, zero dust
- Flexible routing (pipes can turn, go up/down)
- Used for **flour, milk powder, sugar, starch, spice powders**

### Limitations
- High power consumption
- Particle degradation
- Not suitable for wet/sticky materials

---

## 2.5 Type 5 — Chain, Slat & Roller Conveyors

Used for **discrete items** — bottles, cans, cartons, packaged goods.
- **Chain conveyor:** Pallets/slats attached to chain.
- **Roller conveyor:** Gravity or motor-driven rollers.
- **Slat conveyor:** Slats form a continuous platform.

---

## 2.6 Type 6 — Vibratory Conveyors

### Operating Principle
A trough is vibrated at **high frequency (50–60 Hz)** and small amplitude (1–10 mm). Material "hops" along the trough in a series of parabolic jumps, achieving forward motion.

### Advantages
- Gentle handling — ideal for **frozen foods, snack pellets, fruits, vegetables**
- Self-cleaning (vibration dislodges sticky material)
- Can also serve as feeder, screen, dryer

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these formulas — they are KTU-exam favourites.**

| # | Equipment | Formula | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Belt Conveyor Capacity | $Q = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot k$ | Q in T/h, A = cross-section (m²), v = belt speed (m/s), ρ = bulk density (kg/m³), k = inclination factor |
| 2 | Belt Conveyor Belt Area (Troughed) | $A = B^2 \cdot \tan(\theta/2) + B \cdot h$ | B = belt width, θ = trough angle, h = surcharge depth |
| 3 | Belt Conveyor Power | $P = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v}{1000 \cdot \eta}$ | F_t = total effective tension (N), v = speed (m/s), η = drive efficiency |
| 4 | Screw Conveyor Capacity | $Q = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot n \cdot 60 \cdot \rho \cdot \phi \cdot C$ | D = diameter (m), S = pitch (m), n = rpm, φ = fill factor, C = incline factor |
| 5 | Screw Conveyor Power | $P = \dfrac{Q \cdot L \cdot \mu \cdot g}{3600}$ (horizontal) | Q = T/h, L = length (m), μ = friction factor (0.4–0.6), g = 9.81 m/s² |
| 6 | Bucket Elevator Capacity | $Q = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho \cdot v}{t}$ | V = bucket volume (m³), v = belt speed (m/s), t = bucket spacing (m) |
| 7 | Bucket Elevator Power | $P = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{367 \cdot \eta}$ | Q = T/h, H = lift height (m), η = overall efficiency (0.7–0.85) |
| 8 | Pneumatic Conveying Power | $P = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta p}{1000 \cdot \eta_{fan}}$ | Δp = pressure drop (Pa) |
| 9 | Inclination Factor (Belt) | $k = 1 - 0.01 \cdot (\beta - 10)$ | β = inclination angle (°), valid 10° < β < 22° |
| 10 | Belt Speed for Centrifugal Discharge | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{r \cdot g}{0.6}}$ | r = radius of head pulley (m) — minimum for throw |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Food Industry Sector | Preferred Equipment |
|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Dairy (milk powder, cheese)** | Pneumatic, tubular screw, vibratory |
| **Bakery (flour, dough)** | Pneumatic (flour), screw (dough) |
| **Snacks (chips, namkeen)** | Bucket elevator (gentle), vibratory, inclined belt |
| **Beverages (bottles, cans)** | Slat, chain, roller conveyors |
| **Fruits & Vegetables** | Vibratory, roller, water-flume |
| **Spices & powders** | Pneumatic (enclosed), screw |
| **Frozen foods** | Vibratory, stainless steel belt |

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Belt Conveyor Capacity (Troughed Belt)

### Step 1: Define Cross-Sectional Area of Material Load

A troughed belt carrying bulk material has a **trapezoidal cross-section** plus a **triangular surcharge** above the idlers.

Let:
- **B** = belt width (m)
- **θ** = troughing angle (typically 35°)
- **h_s** = surcharge depth (depends on material angle of repose α)

The cross-sectional area is:

$$A = A_{trough} + A_{surcharge}$$

$$A = \left(\frac{B}{2}\right)^2 \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot 2 + B \cdot h_s$$

### Step 2: Apply Geometry for Surcharge Depth

The surcharge sits on top of the troughed belt. Using the angle of repose **α** of the bulk material:

$$h_s = \frac{B}{2} \tan\alpha$$

Substituting back:

$$A = \frac{B^2}{2} \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + \frac{B^2}{2} \tan\alpha$$

### Step 3: Compute Volumetric Flow Rate

The material moves at belt speed **v** (m/s). Volume per second:

$$Q_{vol} = A \cdot v \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{s})$$

### Step 4: Convert to Mass Flow Rate

$$Q_{mass} = A \cdot v \cdot \rho \quad (\text{kg/s})$$

### Step 5: Convert to Tonnes per Hour

$$Q_{T/h} = \frac{A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot 3600}{1000} = 3.6 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho$$

### Step 6: Apply Inclination Factor (β in degrees, 10° ≤ β ≤ 22°)

$$Q_{effective} = Q_{T/h} \cdot k, \quad \text{where} \quad k = 1 - 0.01(\beta - 10)$$

### Final Master Equation

$$\boxed{Q_{T/h} = 3.6 \cdot B^2 \cdot \left[\frac{1}{2}\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\tan\alpha\right] \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot k}$$

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Screw Conveyor Capacity (Horizontal)

### Step 1: Define Volume per Revolution

For a standard full-pitch screw (S = D), one revolution advances material by **one pitch length (S)** through the **cross-sectional area swept by the flight**.

Area swept by flight:

$$A_{flight} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

### Step 2: Account for Fill Factor (φ)

Not the entire area is filled. The fill factor φ represents the fraction filled with material:

$$A_{effective} = \phi \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

### Step 3: Volume Conveyed per Revolution

Volume per revolution = effective area × pitch length:

$$V_{rev} = \phi \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot S$$

### Step 4: Volumetric Flow Rate

At rotational speed **n** (rev/min):

$$Q_{vol} = V_{rev} \cdot n = \phi \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot S \cdot n \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{min})$$

### Step 5: Convert to Mass Flow

$$Q_{mass} = \phi \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot S \cdot n \cdot \rho \quad (\text{kg/min})$$

### Step 6: Convert to Tonnes per Hour

$$\boxed{Q_{T/h} = \phi \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot S \cdot n \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{60}{1000} = 0.0471 \cdot \phi \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot n \cdot \rho}$$

### Step 7: Include Incline Factor for Inclined Screw

For incline angle **β**:
| β | Factor |
|---|--------|
| 0° (horizontal) | **1.00** |
| 10° | **0.90** |
| 15° | **0.80** |
| 20° | **0.65** |
| 90° (vertical) | **0.50** |

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Bucket Elevator Capacity

### Step 1: Volume Carried per Second

Buckets of volume **V_b** (m³) are mounted on belt at spacing **t** (m). At belt speed **v** (m/s), the number of buckets passing the discharge per second is:

$$N_{sec} = \frac{v}{t}$$

### Step 2: Total Volume Conveyed

$$Q_{vol} = V_b \cdot N_{sec} = \frac{V_b \cdot v}{t} \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{s})$$

### Step 3: Convert to Tonnes per Hour

$$\boxed{Q_{T/h} = 3.6 \cdot \frac{V_b \cdot v \cdot \rho}{t}}$$

### Step 4: Power Required

Power = (mass flow × lift height × g) / efficiency:

$$\boxed{P_{kW} = \frac{Q_{T/h} \cdot H \cdot g}{3.6 \cdot \eta \cdot 1000}}$$

Where:
- Q in T/h, H in m, g = 9.81 m/s², η = 0.7–0.85

---

## 3.4 NUMERICAL EXAMPLE (KTU-typical)

**Problem:** A screw conveyor is used to transport wheat flour. Given:
- D = 0.25 m, S = 0.25 m, n = 60 RPM, ρ = 600 kg/m³, φ = 0.30
- Length L = 10 m, friction factor μ = 0.5, η_drive = 0.85
- Horizontal installation

**Find:** (a) Capacity in T/h, (b) Power required in kW

### Solution (a):

$$Q = 0.0471 \cdot \phi \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot n \cdot \rho$$

Substituting values:

$$Q = 0.0471 \times 0.30 \times (0.25)^2 \times 0.25 \times 60 \times 600$$

$$Q = 0.0471 \times 0.30 \times 0.0625 \times 0.25 \times 60 \times 600$$

$$Q = 0.0471 \times 0.30 \times 0.0625 \times 0.25 \times 36000$$

$$Q = 0.0471 \times 168.75$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 7.95 \text{ T/h}}$$

### Solution (b):

Power required to overcome friction along length L:

$$P = \frac{Q \cdot L \cdot \mu \cdot g}{3600 \cdot \eta}$$

$$P = \frac{7.95 \times 10 \times 0.5 \times 9.81}{3600 \times 0.85}$$

$$P = \frac{389.92}{3060}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 0.127 \text{ kW} \approx 127 \text{ W}}$$

---

## 3.5 ANALYTICAL COMPARISON MATRIX

| Parameter | Belt Conveyor | Screw Conveyor | Bucket Elevator | Pneumatic |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Direction** | Horizontal/Incline (≤20°) | Horizontal/Vertical | Vertical | Any direction |
| **Capacity** | Very High (up to **5000 T/h**) | Medium (up to **200 T/h**) | High (up to **1000 T/h**) | Medium (up to **100 T/h**) |
| **Distance** | Long (>100 m) | Short (<30 m) | Vertical (up to 50 m) | Long (>300 m) |
| **Power/Ton** | **Low** | High | Low | **Highest** |
| **Dust** | Some | Low (enclosed) | Very Low | **Zero** |
| **Sanitation** | Good (PU belts) | Excellent (tubular) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Gentle Handling** | Moderate | Low (high shear) | Moderate | Moderate-High |
| **Cost** | Moderate | **Low** | Moderate-High | **High** |
| **Food Examples** | Cereals, bread, fruits | Flour, sugar, spices | Grains, pellets, chips | Flour, milk powder |

---

## 3.6 KEY DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS FOR FOOD INDUSTRY

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Discussion Points (often asked as 7-mark sub-question):**

1. **Material of Construction:** **SS 304** for general food, **SS 316** for corrosive/acidic foods (tomato, citrus). Avoid carbon steel (rusts, contaminates).
2. **Surface Finish:** **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm** (mirror polish) to prevent bacterial adhesion and ease cleaning.
3. **CIP Compatibility:** Equipment must allow Clean-In-Place — no dead spots, dead legs > **3D** rule (branch length < 3× diameter).
4. **FDA / FSSAI Compliance:** Food contact surfaces must use **FDA-approved polymers** (PU, HDPE, PTFE) and food-grade lubricants (H1 grade).
5. **Dust Explosion Safety:** For flour, sugar — equipment must be **ATEX-certified** with explosion vents, grounding, and inerting options.
6. **Belt Tracking & Tensioning:** Take-up pulley/spring tensioner to prevent slippage.
7. **Vibration Isolation:** Rubber mounts on foundations to prevent noise and structural fatigue.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define mechanical conveying. List any four types of conveyors used in the food industry.**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanical Conveying:** It is the controlled movement of food materials from one processing point to another using mechanical equipment powered by electric, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy, replacing manual handling to improve hygiene, speed, and efficiency.

**Four Types of Conveyors (any 4):**
1. **Belt Conveyor** — for cereals, snacks, packaged goods
2. **Screw Conveyor** — for flour, sugar, spices, wet dough
3. **Bucket Elevator** — for vertical lifting of grains, pellets, sugar
4. **Pneumatic Conveyor** — for enclosed transfer of milk powder, flour
5. **Vibratory Conveyor** — for fragile items like chips, frozen foods
6. **Chain/Slat Conveyor** — for bottles, cans, cartons

(Any four well-explained examples carry full 3 marks.)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between a belt conveyor and a screw conveyor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Belt Conveyor | Screw Conveyor |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Principle | Continuous belt over pulleys | Helical screw rotating in trough |
| Direction | Mainly horizontal/inclined (≤20°) | Horizontal or vertical |
| Capacity | Very high (up to 5000 T/h) | Moderate (≤200 T/h) |
| Material | Free-flowing bulk solids | Powders, granules, pastes |
| Power | Low per ton | High per ton |
| Dust | Some exposure | Enclosed → low dust |
| Length | Long (>100 m) | Short (<30 m) |
| Cleaning | Moderate | Excellent (tubular type) |

(3-mark answer = 3 valid distinguishing points.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain in detail the different types of bucket elevators with their construction, working principle, and applications in food processing.**

#### (a) **Construction and Working Principle** [7 Marks]

A bucket elevator consists of:
- **Boot section (bottom):** Houses the foot pulley/sprocket, feed inlet, and take-up tensioner.
- **Head section (top):** Houses the drive pulley/sprocket, motor, gearbox, and discharge spout.
- **Belt or chain:** Carries buckets; chain is used for heavy/abrasive materials; belt for light/clean materials.
- **Buckets:** Fabricated from SS 304, riveted or bolted to belt/chain at regular spacing.
- **Casing:** Encloses the elevator to prevent dust and contamination.

**Working Principle:**
Material is fed into the boot section through a chute. The buckets scoop up the material at the bottom and carry it vertically. At the top, material is discharged by **centrifugal force** (high speed) or **gravity** (low speed), falling through a discharge spout into the next equipment.

**Three Types Based on Discharge:**

1. **Centrifugal Discharge Bucket Elevator**
   - Belt speed: **1.0–1.5 m/s**
   - Discharge by centrifugal force
   - For free-flowing dry grains, sugar, rice
   - High capacity but slightly more product damage

2. **Gravity (Continuous) Discharge Bucket Elevator**
   - Belt speed: **0.5–0.7 m/s**
   - Buckets discharge material as they pass over the head pulley, by gravity
   - Used for **fragile/friable** products like chips, flakes, breakfast cereals
   - Lower capacity but **gentle handling**

3. **Positive Discharge Bucket Elevator**
   - Belt speed: **0.4–0.6 m/s**
   - Buckets are inverted and material is pushed out by a chute/snout
   - For **sluggish, sticky, or abrasive** materials like wet grains, fertilizers
   - Higher power required

#### (b) **Applications, Capacity Formula & Selection** [7 Marks]

**Applications in Food Processing:**
- Grain elevators in rice/flour mills
- Sugar refineries (granulated sugar elevation)
- Snack food plants (potato chips, namkeen)
- Spice processing plants
- Feed mills and pellet plants
- Confectionery (sugar, cocoa powder)

**Capacity Formula:**

$$Q_{T/h} = 3.6 \cdot \frac{V_b \cdot v \cdot \rho}{t}$$

Where:
- V_b = bucket volume (m³), typically 1.5–15 litres
- v = belt speed (m/s), depends on discharge type
- ρ = bulk density of material (kg/m³)
- t = bucket spacing (m), typically 0.3–0.6 m

**Power Required:**

$$P_{kW} = \frac{Q_{T/h} \cdot H}{367 \cdot \eta}$$

Where H = lift height (m), η = 0.7–0.85, **367 = (1000 × g) / 3.6** is a derived conversion constant.

**Selection Criteria:**
- Material properties (friability, flow, abrasiveness)
- Required capacity
- Lift height
- Sanitary standards (food-grade belt, SS buckets)
- Available floor space

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Describe the construction, working, types, advantages and disadvantages of screw conveyors. Derive the capacity equation and discuss its application in food industries.**

#### (a) **Construction, Working, Types, Advantages & Disadvantages** [7 Marks]

**Construction:**
- **Helical flight** welded or bolted to a central shaft
- **U-trough or tubular casing** (food-grade SS 304)
- **End bearings** at drive and tail ends (thrust type)
- **Hanger/intermediate bearings** every 2–3 m to support long shafts
- **Drive assembly**: motor + gearbox + coupling
- **Feed inlet** (hopper) and **discharge outlet**

**Working Principle:**
When the shaft rotates, the helical flight **pushes material forward** axially due to the inclined plane effect of the helix. Material is **not dragged** but is **pushed** — this is why screw conveyors can move material uphill.

**Types (based on flight design):**

1. **Standard Pitch Screw (S = D):** Most common, general purpose.
2. **Short Pitch (S = 0.5D):** For wet, sticky, sluggish materials.
3. **Long Pitch (S = 1.5D–2D):** For free-flowing, high-capacity transfer.
4. **Cut Flight / Cut-and-Folded:** For mixing and conveying simultaneously.
5. **Ribbon Flight:** Continuous ribbon (no center) for sticky/wet materials.
6. **Paddle Flight:** For gentle handling and mixing.

**Advantages (5 valid points):**
1. **Enclosed** → dust-free, hygienic (suitable for food)
2. Simple, compact, low cost
3. Can handle powders, granules, and pastes (low speed)
4. Reversible direction of flow
5. Can mix, blend, and convey simultaneously
6. Easy to clean (especially tubular type with CIP)
7. Multiple inlets and discharge points possible

**Disadvantages (3 valid points):**
1. High power consumption per ton moved
2. Particle degradation at high rotational speeds
3. Limited length (max 30 m) due to shaft deflection
4. Not suitable for fibrous or highly abrasive materials

#### (b) **Capacity Equation Derivation & Food Industry Applications** [7 Marks]

**Derivation (as in Section 3.2):**

Starting from volume swept per revolution:

$$V_{rev} = \phi \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot S$$

Multiplying by **n** revolutions per minute and density ρ:

$$Q_{mass} = \phi \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot S \cdot n \cdot \rho \quad (\text{kg/min})$$

Converting to **Tonnes/hour:**

$$\boxed{Q_{T/h} = 0.0471 \cdot \phi \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot n \cdot \rho \cdot C_{incline}}$$

**Numerical Example (in solution):**
Given D = 0.25 m, S = 0.25 m, n = 60 RPM, ρ = 600 kg/m³, φ = 0.30, horizontal:
**Q = 7.95 T/h** (full calculation shown in Section 3.4).

**Food Industry Applications:**

| Food Material | Application |
|---------------|-------------|
| **Wheat flour** | Mill to sifter transfer |
| **Sugar (granulated)** | Silo to packaging |
| **Rice, dal, pulses** | Cleaning to storage |
| **Spices (turmeric, chilli)** | Grinding to packing |
| **Wet dough** | Mixer to forming machine (pasta) |
| **Cocoa powder, milk powder** | Dryer to silo |
| **Animal feed pellets** | Mixer to bagging |

**Why screw conveyors are preferred in food:**
- **CIP-compatible** (tubular design)
- **No dust emission** (enclosed)
- **No contamination** (SS 304 contact surfaces)
- **Energy-efficient** for short distances
- **Self-feeding** (screw pulls material from hopper)

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Pattern):**

1. **Forgetting Units in Formula Substitution:** Always show units in every step. If you write $Q = 0.0471 \times 0.30 \times 0.0625 \times ...$ without units, examiner deducts 0.5–1 mark even if the answer is correct.

2. **Wrong Conversion Factor:** Converting kg/min to T/h requires **× 60/1000 = 0.06**, not 60. Many students write 60 instead of 0.06, getting an answer 1000× too large.

3. **Mixing Up Belt Conveyor Speed Range:** Food belt conveyors run at **0.5–2.5 m/s**, NOT 5 m/s. Higher speeds cause product damage and dust.

4. **Ignoring Inclination Factor:** If the problem states incline = 15°, you MUST multiply by inclination factor (k = 0.90). Skipping this = -1 mark.

5. **Confusing "Conveying" with "Elevation":** Horizontal/inclined transport = **conveyor**; vertical lift = **elevator**. Examiners are strict on terminology.

6. **Missing Sanitary Design Points:** For food industry questions, always mention **SS 304, CIP, FDA-grade belts, Ra ≤ 0.8 μm** — these are favourite "high-mark differentiator" points.

7. **Not Drawing a Labeled Diagram:** Even a simple block diagram of the equipment fetches 1–2 marks. Always include one.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] **Mechanical handling** = moving food materials via machines (vs. manual).
- [ ] **Conveying** = horizontal; **Elevation** = vertical; **Handling** = broader (loading, stacking).
- [ ] **6 main equipment types:** Belt, Screw, Bucket, Pneumatic, Vibratory, Chain/Roller.

### ✅ Selection Logic
- [ ] **Long horizontal distance + high capacity** → **Belt conveyor**.
- [ ] **Enclosed, hygienic, short distance, powders** → **Screw conveyor**.
- [ ] **Vertical lift of bulk solids** → **Bucket elevator**.
- [ ] **Dust-free, flexible routing, powders** → **Pneumatic conveyor**.
- [ ] **Fragile products (chips, fruits)** → **Vibratory conveyor**.

### ✅ Critical Numbers (Memorize!)
- [ ] Belt conveyor max incline: **18°–20°**
- [ ] Belt speed (food): **0.5 – 2.5 m/s**
- [ ] Screw pitch standard: **S = D**
- [ ] Screw fill factor: **0.15 – 0.45**
- [ ] Centrifugal bucket elevator speed: **1.0 – 1.5 m/s**
- [ ] Gravity discharge bucket elevator speed: **0.5 – 0.7 m/s**
- [ ] Power conversion: **1 HP = 0.7457 kW**
- [ ] Standard g = **9.81 m/s²**

### ✅ Master Formulas (3 Must-Know)
- [ ] Belt: $Q = 3.6 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot k$
- [ ] Screw: $Q = 0.0471 \cdot \phi \cdot D^2 \cdot S \cdot n \cdot \rho$
- [ ] Bucket: $Q = 3.6 \cdot \dfrac{V_b \cdot v \cdot \rho}{t}$ and $P = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{367 \cdot \eta}$

### ✅ Food-Industry Specific Requirements
- [ ] **SS 304** for general; **SS 316** for acidic foods.
- [ ] Surface finish **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm**.
- [ ] **CIP (Clean-In-Place)** compatibility.
- [ ] **FDA-approved** polymers for food contact.
- [ ] **ATEX certification** for dust-explosive materials (flour, sugar).

### ✅ Common Exam Traps
- [ ] Watch units — kg/s vs T/h vs kg/min.
- [ ] Inclination factor k must be applied.
- [ ] Terminology: "conveying" ≠ "elevation."
- [ ] Always include a labeled diagram for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Size reduction and classification, mixing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAGT862 - DEVELOPMENT OF PROCESSED FOOD PRODUCTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Size reduction and classification, mixing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Size Reduction — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Size Reduction** is a fundamental unit operation in food processing that involves the mechanical disintegration of solid food materials into smaller particles, thereby increasing the surface area to volume ratio, facilitating subsequent operations such as mixing, heat transfer, extraction, drying, and packaging. In the context of KTU 2024 syllabus, size reduction is identified as a critical preprocessing operation in the manufacture of processed food products like flours, spices, sugar, milk powder, instant coffee, and cereal-based products. **Standard Metric:** - Particle size is typically expressed in **micrometers (μm)** or **millimeters (mm)**. - The standard international sieve series is the **ISO 565 / Tyler equivalent** mesh. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** > Imagine trying to dissolve a large sugar cube in your tea versus using fine sugar powder. The powder dissolves almost instantly because it has a much larger surface area exposed to the liquid. Size reduction does exactly this — it breaks down bulk food into smaller pieces to speed up cooking, extraction, drying, and even flavor release. Without size reduction, producing smooth chocolate, fine spice powders, or instant soup mixes would be impossible! --- ### 1.2 Classification — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Classification** is the separation of a mixture of solid particles of differing sizes into two or more fractions, based on the geometric size of the particles. The under-sized (fines) pass through the apertures of the screen, while the over-sized (coarse) particles are retained. **Standard Metric:** - Effectiveness of separation is expressed as the **Screening Efficiency (η)**, ranging between **0 and 1 (or 0 to 100 %)**. --- ### 1.3 Mixing — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mixing** is a unit operation that combines two or more components (miscible or immiscible) into a homogeneous or heterogeneous mixture by mechanical agitation. It seeks to eliminate gradients in concentration, temperature, and physical properties. **Standard Metric:** - Degree of homogeneity is quantified by the **Coefficient of Variation (CoV)** or **Mixing Index (M)**. - Power consumption is expressed as **Power Number (Np)**, a dimensionless number. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** > Think of mixing a glass of sugarcane juice with ice and lemon. You stir it to uniformly distribute sugar, water, lemon acid, and ice particles. In an industrial mixer, this same idea is scaled up — the impeller pushes fluid, creating flow patterns that blend ingredients until uniformity is reached. --- ### 1.4 Importance of these Operations in Food Processing | Operation | Primary Purpose | Example Food Products | |---|---|---| | Size reduction | Surface area increase, ease of handling | Wheat flour, ground spices, sugar | | Classification | Quality control, particle size uniformity | Refined flour, sorted pulses | | Mixing | Homogeneous distribution | Dough, beverages, sauces, ice cream | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Principles of Size Reduction Four fundamental mechanisms are applied — singly or in combination — depending on the material properties (hardness, brittleness, fibrousness, moisture content). 1. **Compression** — Slow compressive force applied between two solid surfaces. - *Used for:* Coarse crushing of tough, brittle materials. - *Example equipment:* Jaw crusher, gyratory crusher, roll crusher. 2. **Impact** — Sudden high-velocity collision of particles with a moving hammer or wall. - *Used for:* Moderately hard, non-abrasive materials. - *Example equipment:* Hammer mill, impact pulverizer. 3. **Attrition (Rubbing)** — Shear stress between two surfaces grinds particles. - *Used for:* Tough, fibrous materials and brittle materials. - *Example equipment:* Roller mill, attrition mill. 4. **Cutting (Shearing)** — Sharp blades shear the material into uniform sizes. - *Used for:* Fibrous and cellular tissues. - *Example equipment:* Rotary cutter, knife mill, dicer, slicer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Food materials** are mostly **moist, fibrous, and tough**, hence **cutting and attrition** dominate the food industry, while **compression and impact** dominate mineral processing. --- ### 2.2 Energy Requirement Laws of Size Reduction The energy required to reduce the particle size from a larger dimension to a smaller one is given by three classical laws. Let $d$ be the average particle diameter, $d_1$ and $d_2$ be initial and final sizes, and $E$ be the energy per unit mass. #### **Rittinger's Law (1867) — Surface Area Theory** Energy required is proportional to the new surface area created: $$E = K_R \cdot \left( \frac{1}{d_2} - \frac{1}{d_1} \right)$$ where $K_R$ is Rittinger's constant. This law works best for **fine grinding** where new surface area is the dominant factor. #### **Kick's Law (1885) — Volume Theory** Energy required is proportional to the ratio of volume change: $$E = K_K \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)$$ This law applies best for **coarse crushing** where deformation energy dominates. #### **Bond's Law (1952) — Work Index Theory** A practical compromise between Kick and Rittinger: $$E = K_B \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_1}} \right)$$ $$E = W_i \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_1}} \right)$$ where $W_i$ is the **Bond Work Index** (kWh/ton) — the energy needed to reduce one ton of material from an infinite size to 100 μm. --- ### 2.3 Classification / Sieving Sieves are classified by **mesh number** — the number of openings per linear inch. - **Mesh number (N):** $$N = \frac{1 \text{ inch}}{\text{aperture} + \text{wire diameter}}$$ - **Screening Efficiency (η):** $$\eta = \frac{\text{Mass of undersize actually passing}}{\text{Mass of undersize in feed}} \times 100\%$$ An ideal screen has $\eta = 100\%$. In practice, $\eta$ ranges between **70–90 %** due to factors like near-mesh particles, blinding, and stratification. --- ### 2.4 Mixing — Mechanisms and Indices Mixing is achieved by three simultaneous mechanisms: 1. **Convective mixing** — Bulk movement of material between regions (handled by **impeller/agitator**). 2. **Diffusive mixing** — Random movement of individual particles across freshly created interfaces. 3. **Shear mixing** — Slip planes within the material break up clumps and disperse agglomerates. **Mixing Index (M):** $$M = \frac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}$$ where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of concentration after mixing, and $\sigma_0$ is the standard deviation of a completely segregated mixture. - **M = 0** → Perfectly homogeneous (ideal). - **M = 1** → Completely segregated (no mixing). **Coefficient of Variation (CoV):** $$\text{CoV} = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{c}} \times 100\%$$ Acceptable CoV for most food mixing operations: **≤ 5 %**. --- ### 2.5 Power Consumption in Mixing For an agitated vessel, the dimensionless **Power Number ($N_p$)** is defined as: $$N_p = \frac{P}{\rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5}$$ where: - $P$ = power input (W), - $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³), - $N$ = impeller rotational speed (rev/s), - $D$ = impeller diameter (m). The **Reynolds Number** for the mixer: $$N_{Re} = \frac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2}{\mu}$$ --- ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Concept | Formula | Units / Symbols | |---|---|---| | Rittinger's Law | $E = K_R (1/d_2 - 1/d_1)$ | $E$: kWh/ton, $d$: mm | | Kick's Law | $E = K_K \ln(d_1/d_2)$ | $E$: kWh/ton | | Bond's Law | $E = W_i (1/\sqrt{d_2} - 1/\sqrt{d_1})$ | $W_i$: kWh/ton | | Mesh Number | $N = 1 / (a + w)$ | $a$: aperture (in), $w$: wire diameter | | Screening Efficiency | $\eta = m_u/m_f \times 100$ | percent | | Mixing Index | $M = \sigma / \sigma_0$ | dimensionless | | Coefficient of Variation | $\text{CoV} = \sigma / \bar{c}$ | dimensionless | | Power Number | $N_p = P / (\rho N^3 D^5)$ | dimensionless | | Mixer Reynolds Number | $N_{Re} = \rho N D^2 / \mu$ | dimensionless | > ⚠️ **Critical Note (KTU Pitfall):** When writing LaTeX in tables, **never use `|`** in expressions like `|x|`. Use `\vert x \vert` instead, or the table will break. --- ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Food Industry:** Size reduction is essential in flour mills, spice processing, sugar refining, and cocoa powder production. Modern Buhler, Hosokawa, and Bühler-Miag roller mills dominate global food size reduction. - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Determines bioavailability of active ingredients. - **Cosmetics:** Particle size controls texture, spreadability, and color. - **Cement and Mining:** Industrial-scale size reduction models are based on the same laws (Rittinger, Kick, Bond). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Rittinger's Law (Surface Area Hypothesis) **Assumption:** The energy required for size reduction is directly proportional to the new surface area produced. **Step 1:** Consider a single spherical particle of diameter $d$. - Surface area of one particle: $A_s = \pi d^2$. - Volume of one particle: $V_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$. **Step 2:** For a unit mass of material, the number of particles $n$ is: $$n = \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Density} \times \text{Volume of one particle}} = \frac{M}{\rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}}$$ **Step 3:** Total surface area per unit mass: $$A_T = n \cdot A_s = \frac{M}{\rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}} \cdot \pi d^2 = \frac{6 M}{\rho_p \cdot d}$$ **Step 4:** Change in surface area per unit mass when going from $d_1$ to $d_2$: $$\Delta A = \frac{6M}{\rho_p}\!\left(\frac{1}{d_2} - \frac{1}{d_1}\right)$$ **Step 5:** By Rittinger's hypothesis, $E \propto \Delta A$: $$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{d_2} - \frac{1}{d_1}\right)$$ ✅ **Result verified.** This shows why $E$ is large when going to very fine sizes (i.e., $d_2$ is very small). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Bond's Law (Work Index Formulation) Bond assumed the geometric mean of Kick's and Rittinger's laws: **Step 1:** Kick's law: $E_K = K_K \ln(d_1/d_2)$. **Step 2:** Rittinger's law: $E_R = K_R (1/d_2 - 1/d_1)$. **Step 3:** Geometric mean: $$\sqrt{E_K \cdot E_R} \propto \sqrt{\ln(d_1/d_2) \cdot (1/d_2 - 1/d_1)}$$ **Step 4:** For narrow size distributions, the logarithmic term approximates to $\sqrt{d_1/d_2}$, giving the simplified working form: $$E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_1}}\right)$$ where $W_i$ is the **Bond Work Index**, a tabulated material property. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Bond's Law **Problem:** A food-grade material with Work Index $W_i = 12.5 \text{ kWh/ton}$ is to be crushed from an initial feed size $d_1 = 10 \text{ mm}$ to a product size $d_2 = 1 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the energy required per ton. **Solution:** $$E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_1}}\right)$$ $$\sqrt{d_1} = \sqrt{10} = 3.162 \text{ mm}^{1/2}$$ $$\sqrt{d_2} = \sqrt{1} = 1.000 \text{ mm}^{1/2}$$ $$E = 12.5 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1.000} - \frac{1}{3.162}\right)$$ $$E = 12.5 \cdot (1.000 - 0.316)$$ $$E = 12.5 \cdot 0.684$$ $$\boxed{E = 8.55 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Example — Mixing Index **Problem:** A spice powder mixer segregates with $\sigma_0 = 0.85$ initially. After 5 minutes of operation, 8 samples give concentration values with standard deviation $\sigma = 0.21$. Find the mixing index and percentage mixing achieved. **Solution:** $$M = \frac{\sigma}{\sigma_0} = \frac{0.21}{0.85} = 0.247$$ The degree of mixing is: $$\text{Degree of mixing} = (1 - M) \times 100 = (1 - 0.247) \times 100 = 75.3\%$$ ✅ A mixing index of **0.247** (or **75.3 % mixing**) indicates **moderately good** blending — appropriate for many food applications like spice mixes, but further mixing may be needed for pharmaceutical or infant formula applications where uniformity is critical. --- ### 3.5 Power Number from Reynolds Number (Standard Curves) For a given impeller geometry (e.g., Rushton turbine, pitched blade), the relationship: $$\begin{aligned} N_p &= f(N_{Re}) \\ \log N_p &= a - b \log N_{Re} \end{aligned}$$ - **Laminar regime ($N_{Re} < 10$):** $N_p \propto 1/N_{Re}$, hence $P \propto \mu N^2 D^3$. - **Transitional regime ($10 < N_{Re} < 10^4$):** Empirical fit. - **Turbulent regime ($N_{Re} > 10^4$):** $N_p \approx$ constant (e.g., **$N_p \approx 5$** for Rushton turbine), hence $P \propto \rho N^3 D^5$. **Worked Example:** A Rushton turbine ($D = 0.5$ m, $N = 2$ rev/s) agitates a sauce of density $\rho = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and viscosity $\mu = 0.5 \text{ Pa·s}$. $$N_{Re} = \frac{1050 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.5^2}{0.5} = \frac{1050 \cdot 0.5}{0.5} = 1050$$ Since $10 < N_{Re} < 10^4$, we are in the transitional regime. From standard charts for a Rushton turbine at $N_{Re} \approx 10^3$, $N_p \approx 4.5$. $$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5 = 4.5 \cdot 1050 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.03125$$ $$\boxed{P \approx 1181 \text{ W} \approx 1.18 \text{ kW}}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Size Reduction Process Line ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Material Feed
e.g. Wheat Grains] --> B[Coarse Crusher
Jaw or Roll Crusher] B --> C[Intermediate Grinder
Hammer or Roller Mill] C --> D[Classifier
Sieve or Air Separator] D --> E{Q Size Check} E -->|Meets spec| F[Finished Fine Product
e.g. Flour] E -->|Too coarse| G[Return Oversize Loop] G --> C H[Power Source] -.-> B H -.-> C H -.-> D ``` > **Reading the Flow:** Material is fed into a coarse crusher, passed to an intermediate grinder, then sent to a classifier (sieve). Oversized particles are recycled (re-circulating load) back to the grinder, while correctly sized fines exit as the finished product. This is the **closed-circuit grinding** arrangement used in modern food flour plants. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Equipment Family Tree of Size Reduction ```mermaid flowchart TD SR[Size Reduction Equipment] SR --> CO[Compressive] SR --> IM[Impact] SR --> AT[Attrition] SR --> CT[Cutting] CO --> CO1[Jaw Crusher] CO --> CO2[Gyratory Crusher] CO --> CO3[Roll Crusher] IM --> IM1[Hammer Mill] IM --> IM2[Pin Mill] IM --> IM3[Disintegrator] AT --> AT1[Roller Mill] AT --> AT2[Attrition Mill] AT --> AT3[Ball Mill] CT --> CT1[Knife Cutter] CT --> CT2[Dicer] CT --> CT3[Rotary Slicer] CO1 --> APP1[Used for hard foods
e.g. sugar, salt lumps] IM1 --> APP2[Used for cereals, spices] AT1 --> APP3[Used for flour milling, chocolate refining] CT1 --> APP4[Used for vegetables, fruits, meat] ``` --- ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Mixing Equipment Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD MX[Mixing Equipment] MX --> ST[State of Material] ST --> LQ[Liquids / Pastes] ST --> SD[Dry Solids] ST --> VS[Very Viscous Pastes] LQ --> LQ1[Propeller Mixer] LQ --> LQ2[Turbine Impeller] LQ --> LQ3[Paddle Mixer] SD --> SD1[Ribbon Blender] SD --> SD2[Tumble Drum Mixer] SD --> SD3[V-Cone Blender] VS --> VS1[Sigma Blade Mixer] VS --> VS2[Planetary Mixer] VS --> VS3[Banbury Mixer] LQ1 --> U1[Used in dairy, beverage tanks] SD1 --> U2[Used in spice blends, flour] VS1 --> U3[Used in dough, chocolate conching] ``` --- ### 4.4 Block Flow — Closed-Circuit Mixing with Quality Feedback ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ingredients Inlet
Hopper / Tank] --> B[Mixing Vessel] B --> C[Impeller Agitator] C --> D{Sensor Probe
Conductivity / NIR} D -->|CoV below threshold| E[Discharge Outlet] D -->|CoV above threshold| F[Extended Mixing Time] F --> C E --> P[Packaging Line] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]** #### **Q1. Define size reduction. Mention any two mechanisms with example equipment.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** Size reduction is the mechanical breakdown of solid food material into smaller pieces to increase surface area and facilitate subsequent processing operations like mixing, drying, and extraction. **Mechanisms:** 1. **Compression** — slow crushing between two surfaces; e.g., *jaw crusher*. 2. **Impact** — sudden collision; e.g., *hammer mill*. *(Any two of compression, impact, attrition, cutting may be cited.)* --- #### **Q2. Differentiate between Rittinger's and Kick's laws of size reduction.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Rittinger's Law | Kick's Law | |---|---|---| | Basis | Surface area created | Volume deformation | | Best suited for | Fine grinding | Coarse crushing | | Formula | $E = K_R(1/d_2 - 1/d_1)$ | $E = K_K \ln(d_1/d_2)$ | | Material behavior | Brittle, hard | Tough, deformable | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, Module 4 carries one Part-B question of **14 marks** with internal choice. Below are two parallel question choices for examiner pattern reference. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** #### (a) Explain the different mechanisms of size reduction with suitable examples of equipment for each. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** The four principal mechanisms are: **(i) Compression:** Force applied gradually between two solid surfaces to crush the material. Particle is squeezed until it breaks along internal cracks. - *Examples:* Jaw crusher, gyratory crusher, roll crusher. - *Application:* Sugar cane crushing, oilseeds (cracked before pressing). **(ii) Impact:** A high-velocity blow from a rotating hammer or particle-on-particle collision transfers kinetic energy and shatters the material. - *Examples:* Hammer mill, pin mill, disintegrator. - *Application:* Spice grinding, cereal milling. **(iii) Attrition (Rubbing):** Two hard surfaces rub past each other, applying shear stress. The particles are torn apart by friction. - *Examples:* Roller mill, attrition mill, ball mill. - *Application:* Wheat to flour, cocoa nib grinding. **(iv) Cutting (Shearing):** Sharp blades slice through fibrous material with a shearing action. - *Examples:* Knife cutter, dicer, rotary slicer, Guillotine. - *Application:* Vegetables, fruits, meat processing, frozen block cutting. In food processing, since most raw materials are **moist and fibrous**, **cutting and attrition** dominate. Selection of mechanism depends on hardness, moisture, fiber content, and desired final particle size. --- #### (b) Derive Rittinger's law of size reduction. State its limitations. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** Rittinger assumed that the **energy required is proportional to the new surface area produced**. Consider a unit mass of material consisting of $n$ particles, each of diameter $d$: $$n = \frac{1}{\rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}} = \frac{6}{\rho_p \pi d^3}$$ Total surface area: $$A = n \cdot \pi d^2 = \frac{6}{\rho_p \pi d^3} \cdot \pi d^2 = \frac{6}{\rho_p d}$$ For unit mass, when particle size changes from $d_1$ to $d_2$: $$\Delta A = \frac{6}{\rho_p}\!\left(\frac{1}{d_2} - \frac{1}{d_1}\right)$$ By Rittinger's hypothesis, $E \propto \Delta A$, hence: $$E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{d_2} - \frac{1}{d_1}\right)$$ **Limitations:** 1. Assumes particles are perfectly spherical (real food materials are irregular). 2. Holds only for **fine grinding**; fails in coarse crushing. 3. Ignores elastic deformation energy. 4. Does not account for material heterogeneity. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** #### (a) Describe the working principles and applications of hammer mill and roller mill in food processing. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Hammer Mill:** *Working principle:* A rotor fitted with swinging hammers rotates at high speed (1500–4000 rpm). Food material fed through a hopper is hit by hammers, accelerated, and shattered by impact against breaker plates and screens. Particles below screen aperture exit. *Components:* Rotor, hammers, screens, drive shaft, feed inlet, discharge outlet. *Applications in food processing:* - Spice powder production (turmeric, chilli, coriander). - Cereal grain size reduction (maize, sorghum). - Animal feed milling. - Sugar grinding. *Advantages:* High capacity, simple design, suitable for a wide range of materials. *Limitations:* High heat generation (may degrade heat-sensitive nutrients), excessive fines, high dust. **Roller Mill:** *Working principle:* Two counter-rotating corrugated or smooth rollers rotate at different speeds (typically 1:1.25 to 1:2). Material is nipped between the rollers and subjected to compression and shear, resulting in controlled size reduction. *Components:* Two rollers (one fast, one slow), feeders, scrapers, differential drive system. *Applications in food processing:* - Wheat flour milling (gradual reduction system). - Cracker and biscuit production. - Oilseed crushing. - Malt milling for brewing. *Advantages:* Uniform particle size, lower heat generation, energy-efficient for cereals. *Limitations:* Higher capital cost, requires precise roller gap adjustment, not suitable for very hard or fibrous materials. --- #### (b) Explain the principles of mixing. Discuss the classification of mixers used in food industries with examples. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Principles of Mixing:** Mixing is the unit operation that combines two or more components to achieve a desired degree of uniformity. Three mechanisms operate simultaneously: 1. **Convective Mixing:** Macroscopic movement of material between regions of the mixer, driven by impeller or paddle motion. Responsible for **bulk blending**. 2. **Diffusive Mixing:** Random, microscopic inter-particle movement across freshly formed interfaces. Responsible for **local homogenization**. 3. **Shear Mixing:** Slip planes in the material break up clumps and disperse agglomerates. Critical in **viscous systems** like dough and chocolate. **Goodness of mixing** is measured by the **mixing index** $M = \sigma / \sigma_0$, with $M$ approaching 0 representing ideal homogeneity. **Classification of Mixers:** **(A) For liquids and low-viscosity fluids:** - *Propeller mixer* — for water-like fluids; e.g., beverage tanks. - *Turbine impeller mixer* — for moderate viscosity; e.g., dairy, sauces. - *Paddle mixer* — gentle agitation; e.g., juice blending. **(B) For dry solids (powders, granules):** - *Ribbon blender* — U-shaped trough with helical ribbon; e.g., spice mix, premixes. - *Tumble drum mixer (V-cone, double-cone)* — tumbling action; e.g., infant formula, cocoa powder. - *Vertical screw mixer* — central screw lifts and redistributes particles. **(C) For pastes and doughs (high viscosity):** - *Sigma blade mixer* — two intermeshing blades; e.g., biscuit dough. - *Planetary mixer* — beater rotates while orbiting; e.g., cake batter. - *Banbury mixer* — intensive shear with floating weight; e.g., chewing gum, chocolate compounding. **Selection criteria:** Viscosity of the mix, batch vs continuous operation, sensitivity of ingredients (heat, shear), desired mixing index, and capital cost. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Size Reduction & Mixing Questions:** > > 1. **Mixing up the laws:** Students often write Kick's law formula in a Rittinger's law answer. Memorize: *Rittinger = reciprocals of sizes, Kick = log of size ratio, Bond = reciprocals of square roots of sizes*. > 2. **Forgetting units:** Bond Work Index $W_i$ is in **kWh/ton**, not kW. Always include the unit. > 3. **Confusing sieve mesh:** More mesh number = **smaller** aperture, not larger. > 4. **Power number misuse:** $N_p$ is dimensionless. Students forget the formula and copy the Reynolds number expression. > 5. **Mixing Index sign:** $M = \sigma/\sigma_0$ lies between **0 and 1**, where **0 is perfectly mixed** — this is counter-intuitive and frequently marked wrong. > 6. **Forgetting internal choice:** In Part B, KTU 2024 scheme allows choice. Always attempt the easier option completely. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Size reduction** increases surface area to enhance downstream operations. - **Four mechanisms:** Compression, impact, attrition, cutting. - **Three laws:** Rittinger (fine), Kick (coarse), Bond (general). - **Bond's Law formula:** $E = W_i (1/\sqrt{d_2} - 1/\sqrt{d_1})$ is the **most asked** in KTU. - **Work Index ($W_i$)** is a material property; typical values: limestone ~12, sugar cane ~10, wheat ~5 kWh/ton. - **Classification** uses mesh number; higher mesh = smaller aperture. - **Screening efficiency (η)** ≤ 100 %; typical food industry value: 70–90 %. - **Mixing mechanisms:** Convective, diffusive, shear — occur simultaneously. - **Mixing Index (M)** ranges 0 (perfect) to 1 (segregated); CoV ≤ 5 % is acceptable. - **Power Number ($N_p$)** is dimensionless; constant in turbulent regime. - **Equipment selection** depends on material moisture, hardness, fibrousness. - **Closed-circuit grinding** improves efficiency and reduces over-grinding. - Always **state units** in numerical answers (kWh/ton, mm, μm, Pa·s).

topics.title: Kneading, blending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT862 - DEVELOPMENT OF PROCESSED PROCESSED FOOD PRODUCTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kneading, blending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Kneading

**Kneading** is a unit operation in food processing that involves the repeated mechanical working, folding, stretching, and compressing of a viscoelastic mass (typically dough) to develop a homogeneous structure, uniform ingredient distribution, and desired rheological properties such as gluten development, elasticity, and texture.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Kneading is a *mechanical mixing process* in which a semi-solid or dough-like food mass is subjected to shearing, compression, and folding forces to achieve homogeneity and to induce structural changes (e.g., gluten network formation in wheat dough).

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Blending

**Blending** is the unit operation of combining two or more ingredients — in solid, liquid, or powder form — into a uniform, homogeneous mixture without initiating chemical or significant structural transformation of the components. The primary objective is uniformity of composition.

> [!NOTE] **Core Distinction**
> - **Kneading** is a *destructive-developmental* process (alters protein/starch structure).
> - **Blending** is a *conservative-uniformity* process (retains component properties).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

> **Analogy: Making Tea vs. Kneading Atta**
> - **Blending** is like adding sugar and milk to a cup of tea and stirring — you are just *mixing* to make the taste uniform everywhere in the cup. The sugar crystal structure does not change.
> - **Kneading** is like pressing and folding atta (flour) dough repeatedly — here, the gluten proteins *physically align and cross-link* into a network, transforming a sticky powder into a smooth, elastic ball. The structure of the dough has been *developed*, not just mixed.

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics in Kneading & Blending

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Kneading time (bread dough) | **8 – 20** | minutes |
| Kneading speed (planetary) | **60 – 300** | rpm |
| Blending time (powder) | **5 – 30** | minutes |
| Mixing index (M) | **0 (poor) – 1 (perfect)** | dimensionless |
| Batch capacity | **5 – 2000** | kg |
| Power input | **0.5 – 50** | kW |

> [!NOTE] **Standard Threshold (KTU Emphasis)**
> A mixing index of **M ≥ 0.95** is the industry benchmark for a *commercially acceptable* blend in food powders.
